items.t: Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon — 'Internal Classic' (Nei Jing, 內經)
items.detail: 2 volumes, 81 chapters each: Plain Questions (Su Wen, 素問) & Spiritual Pivot (Ling Shu, 靈樞). Compiled between the late Warring States period (475–221 BCE) and the Han dynasty (206 BCE–220 CE).

items.t: Discussion on Cold Damage and Miscellaneous Diseases
items.detail: By ZHANG Zhong Jing (張仲景, 150–219 CE). Split into Treatise on Cold Damage (Shang Han Lun, 傷寒論) & Essential Prescriptions from the Golden Cabinet (Jin Gui Yao Lue, 金匱要略).

items.t: The Classic of Difficulties (Nan Jing, 難經)
items.detail: By Bian Que / Qin Yue Ren (扁鵲 / 秦越人, 401–310 BCE), state of Qí, Zhou dynasty / Warring States period.

blocks.heading: Wen Bing (Warm Disease) theory
blocks.lead: Collection of theories on warm diseases, Ming (1368–1644) to Qing (1644–1912) dynasties.

items.t: On Epidemic Warm Diseases (Wen Re Lun, 溫熱論; 1746)
items.detail: By YE Tian Shi (葉天士, 1667–1746).

items.t: Detailed Analysis of Warm Diseases (Wen Bing Tiao Bian, 溫病條辨; 1798)
items.detail: By WU Ju Tong (吳鞠通, 1758–1836).

items.t: On Pestilence (Wen Yi Lun, 溫疫論; 1642)
items.detail: By Wu Youxing (1580–1660). Proposed the theory of Li Qi (excessive influences) — pestilence not from exogenous pathogens but from infection; enters through mouth and nose; curable by herbs.

Believed to be first in China to use anaesthesia in surgery — general anaesthetic combining wine with Ma Fei San (麻沸散). Famed for acupuncture, moxibustion, herbs, and the Exercise of the Five Animals (Wu Qin Xi, 五禽戲: tiger, deer, bear, ape, crane).
Handy Therapies for Emergencies (Zhou Hou Fang, 肘后方). Recorded Qing Hao (淸蒿) — source of Artemisinin (Qing Hao Su, 淸蒿素), later isolated by Tu You You (屠呦呦, 1930– ). Also wrote Bao Pu Zi (抱朴子).
Ge Hong's wife; first female to practice moxibustion for medical conditions.

Essential Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold (Qian Jin Yao Fang, 千金要方) + Supplement (Qian Jin Yi Fang, 千金翼方). Emphasized combined treatment; 'On the Absolute Sincerity of Great Physicians' = TCM 'Hippocratic Oath'.

rows: Divine Farmer's Materia Medica (神農本草經)
Author unknown, c. 200–250 CE — 365 entries in 3 volumes
Canon of Acupuncture & Moxibustion (鍼灸甲乙經)
Wang Shu He (王叔和, 210–258 CE) — 10 volumes
Lei Gong Pao Zhi Lun (雷公炮炙論)
Lei Xiao (雷斅), Southern & Northern dynasties
Zhu Bing Yuan Hou Lun (諸病源候論)
Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue (小兒藥證直訣)

blocks.heading: Divine Farmer's Materia Medica — details
blocks.lead: First/oldest book specialized in materia medica; 3 volumes, 365 entries.

items.detail: King-minister-envoy-assistant (君臣佐使); five tastes (酸鹹苦辛); four natures cold-cool-warm-hot (寒熱溫凉); toxic / non-toxic (有毒無毒).

blocks.heading: TCM from 900 to 1900 CE
blocks.lead: Song (960–1279), Jin (1115–1234), Yuan (1271–1368): scholar-physicians (儒醫), government oversight, large-volume publishing, clinical specializations, scholastic branches (學派).

items.detail: Liu Wan Su (劉完素) — 'Cooling', fire & heat; Zhang Cong Zheng (張從正) — 'Purging'; Li Dong Yuan (李杲) — 'Fortify the earth', Treatise on the Spleen & Stomach (脾胃論); Zhu Zhen Heng (朱震亨) — 'Nourishing Yin'.

items.t: Compendium of Materia Medica (Ben Cao Gang Mu, 本草綱目; 1578)
items.detail: By Li Shi Zhen (李時珍, 1518–1593); 52 volumes, ~1800 medicinal elements.

blocks.heading: Acupuncture in Europe & the United States

items.t: Reached Europe by the early 16th century; transmitted via Latin translations (17th–19th c.).

items.detail: French scholar-diplomat in China 1901–1917; introduced classical acupuncture theory to Europe. Coined the terms 'meridian' and 'energy'.
J. Reston's 1971 New York Times article + President Nixon's 1972 China visit sparked widespread interest. FDA reclassified acupuncture needles out of 'experimental devices' in 1996.

WHO (1989) guidelines: 2500 hrs for non-physician acupuncturists, 200 hrs for physicians. NCCAOM (est. 1984) exam covers acupuncture, herbal, point location, clean needle technique. L.Ac. = a state-granted title, not a degree.

acu-found.title: Yin-Yang & Five Elements
acu-found.titleCn: 陰陽學說 · 五行學說

blocks.heading: Basics of Yin-Yang theory
blocks.lead: Yin and Yang are NOT absolute but rather relative within a relationship or a matter.

rows: Moving · Outwards · Upwards · Warming/Hot · Clear/Bright · Energy
Static · Inwards · Downwards · Cooling/Cold · Turbid/Dark · Material

items.t: Yin and Yang may transform into each other under special circumstances
items.detail: e.g. night (Yin) changes to day (Yang)

items.t: Yin and Yang are two phases of a cyclical movement and two states of density of matter

items.detail: Relative opposites within a relationship or matter — up/down, left/right, heaven/earth, moving/static, day/night, hot/cold, fire/water.

items.t: Mutual interdependence of Yin and Yang
items.detail: Dependent on each other; may not exist alone. No downwards without upwards, no heat without cold, no night without day.
Not static but dynamic equilibrium + mutual consumption. Seasons: winter→spring→summer consumes Yin and generates Yang; day/night follows the same pattern.
Yang transforming to Yin and Yin to Yang under special circumstances — day to night, summer to winter.

blocks.heading: Application — Structure of the human body
blocks.lead: Inner/outer (內外), back/abdomen (背腹), Zang/Fu (臟腑), upper/lower (上下), exterior/interior (表裏).

rows: Upper part, outer sides of the 4 limbs, Fu organs (passage)
Lower part, inner sides of the 4 limbs, Zang organs (storage)

blocks.heading: Application — Physiological functions
blocks.lead: Functions and substances must work together to maintain life activities.

items.t: Excess of Yang → heat; prevalence of Yang causes Yin diseases
Excess of Yin → cold; prevalence of Yin causes Yang diseases
Damage of Yin or Yang eventually affects the other, and both become damaged

blocks.heading: Application — Diagnosis (8 principles)
blocks.lead: Exterior (表), interior (裏), cold (寒), heat (熱), deficiency (虛), excess (實), Yin (陰), Yang (陽).

rows: weak low voice, dislikes talking, weak breathing

items.cn: 熱者寒之; 寒者熱之
虛者補之; 實者瀉之

items.t: Excess of Yang → heat (+ damage to Yin fluids): use cold to treat heat + reduce excess
Excess of Yin → cold (+ damage to Yang Qi): use heat to treat cold + reduce excess
Deficiency of Yin → unable to control Yang → heat: nourish Yin and benefit water to control Yang heat
Deficiency of Yang → unable to control Yin → cold: supplement Yang and benefit fire to reduce Yin cold

blocks.heading: Five Elements — characteristics
blocks.lead: Five Elements (Wu Xing, 五行): Wood (木), Fire (火), Earth (土), Metal (金), Water (水).

items.detail: Mother-and-son relationship (子母).

items.t: Controlling (相克): Wood→Earth, Fire→Metal, Earth→Water, Metal→Wood, Water→Fire
Overacting (相乘): same sequence as controlling, but abnormal over-controlling under imbalance
Insulting (相侮): opposite to the controlling sequence; abnormal insulting under imbalance
Generating & controlling = normal sequences; overacting & insulting = abnormal sequences

blocks.heading: Five Elements — TCM application
blocks.headingCn: 五行學說在中醫學中的應用

items.t: Physiology: Wood→LV (spreading & growth), Fire→HT (flare up, warm), Earth→SP (growing & reaping), Metal→LU (clearing), Water→KI (moistening, descending, storing)
Pathology in generating sequence: mother's condition affects son (母病及子); son invades mother (子病犯母)
Pathology in controlling sequence: overacting (相乘) and insulting (相侮)

items.detail: 難經 六十九難: “In deficiency, fortify the mother; in excess, sedate the son” (虛則補其母, 實則瀉其子)

items.t: Vital substances: Qi (氣), Blood (Xue, 血), Body Fluids (Jin Ye, 津液) + Essence (Jing, 精) and Spirit (Shen, 神)
Blood & body fluids → nourishing + lubricating → Yin
Essence — broad sense: all vital substances ('essence Qi', 精氣); narrow sense: material for reproduction, closely related to KI
Sources: Pre-heaven Qi (先天之精氣) + food Qi (穀氣) + air (自然界的淸氣)
Produced by physiological actions of internal organs, especially LU, SP/ST & KI
Pre-heaven Qi is replenished by post-heaven Qi
items.detail: Dysfunction of SP/ST especially disturbs Qi production → Qi diseases.

rows: Growth & development; organ functions; production & circulation of blood; movement of body fluids
Source of body heat; supports organ activity and circulation
Protects the exterior against external pathogenic factors
Contains blood in vessels; controls sweat, urine, saliva, sperm, etc.

blocks.heading: Qi — mechanism (Qi Ji, 氣機)
blocks.lead: 4 basic movements required to maintain life: ascending (升), descending (降), exiting (出), entering (入).

items.detail: Stagnation of Qi (氣滯), reverse flow (氣逆), sinking (氣陷), collapse (氣脫), knotted Qi (氣結).

rows: Pre-heaven essence + post-heaven food essence; flows via Sanjiao (三焦)
Motive force for growth, warming, organ activity
Air (LU) + food essence (SP/ST), gathers in chest (膻中)
Breathing via trachea + circulates Qi and blood via HT; strength felt at Xu Li (虛里)
Refined part of food essence; within vessels, with blood
Nutrition + part of blood ('Ying blood', 營血); Yin
Food essence; outside vessels, in skin & flesh
Defends exterior, warms organs/skin, controls sweat pores; Yang

items.t: Both transform from food essence; Wei circulates outside vessels (Yang), Ying inside vessels (Yin)
Harmony → normal pore opening/closing, body temperature, sleep, and defense
Disharmony → fever & chills, no/profuse sweating, insomnia
Red fluid essential for life → nutrition and lubrication; must flow within the vessels (脈 / 血府)
Production: mainly nutritive Qi (營氣) + body fluids (津液); food essence + LU; essence(精)/KI ↔ blood(血)/LV
Functions: nourishing (營養), lubricating (滋潤), basis for mental activities (精神活動)

items.detail: Belongs to Yin (static) → requires Qi's motive force + Qi's holding function; depends on normal LU, SP, LV functions.

rows: joints, organs, brain, marrow — moistening & nourishing

items.t: Jin and Ye may transform into each other

items.detail: Food/drink → ST → refined essence → SP (transport), LU (disperse & descend to skin & KI/BL), KI (steam clear up, descend turbid to BL → urine), SJ (pathway), SI (separate clear from turbid).

blocks.heading: Relationships between vital substances
blocks.headingCn: 氣血津液之間的相互關係

items.t: Blood is the mother of Qi (血爲氣之母)
items.detail: Blood is nutrition ('fuel') to Qi.

items.t: Qi & body fluids: Qi produces (氣能生津), moves & transforms (氣能行化津), holds; fluids load Qi (津能載氣)
Blood & body fluids have the same origin — food essence; may damage each other
5 Zang organs (五臟): produce & store essence; solid
items.detail: Heart 心, Lung 肺, Spleen 脾, Liver 肝, Kidney 腎 (+ Pericardium 心包).

items.t: 6 Fu organs (六腑): receive, transform & transport food Qi; hollow
items.detail: Gallbladder 膽, Stomach 胃, Small Intestine 小腸, Large Intestine 大腸, Bladder 膀胱, Triple Energizer 三焦.

items.t: Extraordinary organs (奇恒之府): appear like Fu but store like Zang
items.detail: Brain 腦, Marrow 髓, Bone 骨, Vessels 脈, Gallbladder 膽, Uterus 女子胞.

blocks.heading: Heart (Xin, 心)
blocks.lead: The 'king' organ (君主之官). Located in the chest, above the diaphragm, protected by the pericardium.

items.t: HT governs blood and blood vessels (心主血脈)
HT governs the spirit / mind (心主神志)
Related to joy (心在志爲喜); controls sweat (心在液爲汗)
Manifests in the complexion; opens into the tongue (心在竅爲舌)

items.detail: Envelope of the Heart; pathogenic factors invade PC first, protecting HT.

blocks.heading: Lung (Fei, 肺)
blocks.lead: At the top of the chest cavity ('umbrella'); delicate organ, easily affected by heat/cold. Po (魄) resides here.

items.t: LU governs Qi and controls respiration (肺主氣, 司呼吸)
LU controls dispersing and descending of Qi and fluids (肺主宣發和肅降)
LU regulates water passages (肺通調水道)
LU controls all vessels and regulates activities (肺朝百脈, 主治節)
Affected by sadness/worry; controls nasal mucus; controls skin & manifests in body hair; opens into the nose

blocks.heading: Spleen (Pi, 脾)
blocks.lead: In the middle, below the diaphragm. With ST = digestive system; 'the root of the post-heaven essence' (後天之本).

items.t: SP governs transformation and transportation (脾主運化)
items.detail: Transforms/transports food stuff and fluids.

items.t: SP controls the raising of clear Qi (脾主升淸)
items.detail: vs. ST descending the turbid; prevents organ prolapse.

items.t: SP controls Blood (脾主統血) — holds blood within vessels
Affected by pensiveness; controls saliva; controls flesh & 4 limbs; opens into the mouth, manifests in the lips

blocks.heading: Liver (Gan, 肝)
blocks.lead: Below the diaphragm, hypochondriac area. Wood (木). Hun (魂) resides here.

items.t: LV ensures the smooth flow of Qi (肝主疏泄)
items.detail: Regulates Qi mechanism, promotes SP/ST digestion & bile, regulates emotion, and ovulation/menses/ejaculation.
Regulates blood volume for activity vs. rest.

items.t: Affected by anger; tears are its fluid; opens into the eyes; controls the sinews; manifests in the nails

blocks.heading: Kidney (Shen, 腎)
blocks.lead: At the lumbar area, 2 sides of the spine. 'The root of pre-heaven' (腎爲先天之本). Water (水).

items.t: KI stores essence, governs birth, growth, reproduction & development (腎藏精)
items.detail: Essence = pre-heaven + post-heaven. Celestial Water (Tian Gui, 天癸) governs sexual maturity.

items.t: KI Yin (nourishing/lubricating) & KI Yang (motive force/warming) — root of all organs' Yin and Yang
KI governs water (腎主水); controls reception of Qi (腎主納氣)
Affected by fear; controls spittle; controls bones, produces marrow & fills the brain; manifests in the hair; opens into the ears & controls the 2 lower orifices

items.detail: Root of the Original Qi and source of physiological fire; warms the lower Jiao, SP/ST for digestion, and the essence/uterus.

rows: Stores & excretes bile (from LV essence); controls decisiveness. Both a Fu and an extraordinary organ.
Receives (受納) and rots/ripens food (腐熟水穀); controls descending of Qi (以降爲和).
Receives & transforms; separates the clear from the turbid (泌別淸濁).
Passage & conduction; transforms stools and reabsorbs fluids.
Controls whole-body Qi mechanism & transformation; pathway for water/fluids.
Above diaphragm — HT, LU, head, face
Diaphragm to umbilicus — ST, SP, (LV), GB
Below umbilicus — KI, BL, SI, LI

items.t: Brain (Nao, 腦) — 'sea of marrow'; controls intelligence, sight, hearing, smell, speech
Uterus (Nu Zi Bao, 女子胞) — regulates menstruation, houses the fetus; related to Ren Mai (任脈) & Chong Mai (衝脈), and to HT, LV, SP
Channels and collaterals (Jing Luo, 經絡) = channels (Jing Mai, 經脈, main routes) + collaterals (Luo Mai, 絡脈, branches)
Move Qi and blood to the whole body; connect Zangfu with other parts; upward, downward, outward & inward pathways

items.detail: 12 regular channels (十二經脈; 正經); 8 extraordinary channels (奇經八脈); 12 divergent channels (十二經別).
15 collaterals (十五別絡); superficial collateral (浮絡); minute collateral (孫絡).

items.t: Also: 12 muscle regions (十二經筋) & 12 cutaneous regions (十二皮膚)

blocks.heading: 12 regular channels — naming
blocks.lead: Yin/Yang × hand/foot. Yang: Taiyang (太陽), Shaoyang (少陽), Yangming (陽明). Yin: Taiyin (太陰), Shaoyin (少陰), Jueyin (厥陰).

rows: Hand Taiyin Lung 手太陰肺經
Hand Yangming Large Intestine 手陽明大腸經
Foot Yangming Stomach 足陽明胃經
Foot Taiyin Spleen 足太陰脾經
Hand Shaoyin Heart 手少陰心經
Hand Taiyang Small Intestine 手太陽小腸經
Foot Taiyang Bladder 足太陽膀胱經
Foot Shaoyin Kidney 足少陰腎經
Hand Jueyin Pericardium 手厥陰心包經
Hand Shaoyang Sanjiao 手少陽三焦經
Foot Shaoyang Gallbladder 足少陽膽經
Foot Jueyin Liver 足厥陰肝經

items.t: Yin channels run on the inner side of limbs; Yang channels on the outer side

items.detail: LU → LI → ST → SP → HT → SI → BL → KI → PC → SJ → GB → LV → LU (repeats).

blocks.heading: 8 extraordinary channels
blocks.lead: Connect and regulate the 12 regular channels; closely related to extraordinary organs + LV and KI.

rows: Sea of Yin channels (陰脈之海); related to uterus/conception
Sea of Yang channels (陽脈之海); related to KI, brain, marrow
Sea of the 12 channels & 'sea of blood' (血海); related to menses
Yin / Yang Motility 陰蹻/陽蹻脈
Nourish eyes, eye opening/closing, lower-limb movement
Yin / Yang Linking 陰維/陽維脈
Yin Wei binds all Yin channels; Yang Wei binds all Yang channels

items.detail: Strengthen exterior-interior relationships; reach areas the regular channels don't. Pathway: separating (離) → entering (入) → exiting (出) → uniting (合).
12 from regular channels + major collateral of SP (脾之大絡) + DU + REN; branch below elbows/knees.

items.t: Superficial (浮絡) and minute (孫絡) collaterals — progressively smaller, more superficial

items.detail: Bind skeletal structures, aid joint bending/stretching; follow regular channel pathways but do not reach organs.
Superficial skin divisions along the 12 regular channels; most superficial tissue → protection.

items.t: Connect interior-exterior, upper-lower, organs with other structures
Circulate Qi & blood, nourish/moisten organs and structures
React & transmit — 'obtaining Qi' (得氣), 'moving Qi' (行氣) along the channels
Maintain dynamic balance (harmonize Yin and Yang)

items.detail: Explain pathology (pathways of evil Qi), aid diagnosis, guide treatment (local/distal points; herbs 'pertain' to channels, 藥物歸經).

items.t: Six pathogenic factors (Liu Yin, 六淫); Epidemic Qi (Li Qi, 癘氣)
Food (飮食); Work and rest (勞逸)
Phlegm fluids (Tan Yin, 痰飮); Blood stasis (Yu Xue, 瘀血); External injuries (外傷)

blocks.heading: Six pathogenic factors (6 evil Qi)
blocks.lead: Wind, Cold, Summer-Heat, Dampness, Dryness, Fire/Heat. Normally '6 Qi' (climatic conditions); become pathogenic when abnormal or when the body's right Qi is insufficient.

items.t: Related to season, weather, environment; may invade singly or combined
May transform into other factors during disease
Initially invade the superficial body and/or via nose/mouth
5 internally generated factors (內生五邪): Wind, Cold, Dampness, Dryness, Fire — from Zangfu dysfunction (no internal Summer-Heat)

blocks.heading: Characteristics of each factor
blocks.headingCn: 六淫的性質與致病特點

rows: Yang; main in spring but all seasons. Moving & changing (善行數變); opens pores; the leading factor others rely on.
Yin; main in winter. Damages Yang Qi; coagulates & stagnates (→ pain); contracts & pulls (spasm). External (傷寒/中寒) & internal.
Yang; summer only, purely external. Scorching; ascends & disperses; damages Qi and body fluids; often combines with dampness.
Yin; main in late summer. Heavy & turbid; sticky & stagnant; downward-pouring; blocks Qi & damages Yang. External & internal.
Main in fall; warm-dryness (溫燥) & cool-dryness (凉燥). Damages body fluids; damages LU.
Yang; flames upward. Damages Qi & fluids; produces wind & stirs blood; may produce abscess; relates to HT & blood.

items.t: Rapid development, severe, similar manifestations, infectious
Spread influenced by weather, environment/food, prevention/separation, social factors

blocks.heading: Seven emotions (Qi Qing, 七情)
blocks.lead: Joy 喜, Anger 怒, Worry 憂, Pensiveness 思, Sadness 悲, Fear 恐, Shock 驚. Sudden-strong or prolonged stimulation disturbs the Qi mechanism.

items.t: Directly damage the internal organs (vs. external factors that attack the exterior first)

items.detail: Anger → Qi rises; Joy → Qi relaxes/slows; Sadness → Qi consumed; Fear → Qi sinks; Shock → Qi chaotic; Pensiveness → Qi knotted.

items.t: Imbalance of starvation & fullness (飢飽失常) — too little or too much damages SP/ST
Dirty/contaminated food (飮食不潔) — ST/intestinal diseases
Preference for certain flavors (飮食偏嗜)
items.detail: Raw/cold → cold-damp; pungent/hot → accumulated heat; excess of one taste can harm the corresponding Zang organ.

items.t: Over-work (過勞): physical (fatigue), mental (damages HT/SP), sexual (damages KI essence)
Over-rest (過度安逸): inhibits Qi/blood flow, weakens SP/ST
External injuries (外傷): cuts, fractures, burns, frostbite, insect/animal bites, etc.

items.detail: From disordered water metabolism (LU, SP, KI, SJ). Phlegm (痰) sticky-turbid; Fluids (飮) watery-clear. Visible & invisible phlegm. 'Phlegm is the cause of 100 diseases.' Tongue slippery-greasy; pulse slippery/wiry.
From Qi deficiency/stagnation, blood-cold, blood-heat, or bleeding. Pain is stabbing, fixed, worse at night, dislikes pressure; dark-purplish complexion/lips/tongue; choppy pulse.

items.t: Right Qi (正氣) = normal function + resistance + recovery; Evil Qi (邪氣) = disease-causing factors
Insufficient right Qi → disease occurs; struggle between right and evil Qi decides onset, depth and type
Internal factors (constitution, mental/emotional state) + external factors (weather, environment, injuries) together determine occurrence
Pathogenesis (病機) = mechanism of occurrence, development and transformation of disease
Struggle between right Qi and evil Qi disturbs Yin-Yang balance, Zangfu functions, channels, and Qi-blood mechanisms → systemic or local pathological changes

rows: Exuberance of evil Qi (邪氣亢盛), right Qi still relatively intact → fierce struggle
Insufficiency of right Qi (正氣不足) → weak struggle
Weak constitutions; later stages; chronic conditions

items.t: Mixed excess & deficiency (虛實錯雜) — coexistence, or transformation between them
True/false: true deficiency-false excess (眞虛假實), true excess-false deficiency (眞實假虛)
Excess of Yang (陽偏勝) → excess-heat (實熱證); 'victory of Yang causes Yin diseases'
Excess of Yin (陰偏勝) → excess-cold (實寒證); 'victory of Yin causes Yang diseases'
Deficiency of Yang (陽偏衰) → empty-cold (虛寒證); mainly SP & KI
Deficiency of Yin (陰偏衰) → empty-heat (虛熱證); mainly LV & KI
Mutual damage (陰陽互損): damage of Yin affecting Yang (陰損及陽) / Yang affecting Yin (陽損及陰) → both deficient (陰陽兩虛)
Repulsion (陰陽格拒): excess Yin repelling Yang → true cold-false heat (眞寒假熱); excess Yang repelling Yin → true heat-false cold (眞熱假寒)
Collapse (陰陽亡失): collapse of Yang (亡陽) or Yin (亡陰) → emergency

items.detail: From pre-heaven deficiency, SP/LU/KI dysfunction, work-rest imbalance, chronic conditions. Fatigue, weak limbs, dizziness, spontaneous sweating, easy colds.
Blockage of Qi movement → distension, pain; may cause blood stasis or phlegm. Often LU, LV, SP, ST.
Adverse upward movement; mainly LU, ST, LV.
Type of Qi deficiency with weak lifting → prolapse (ST, KI, uterus, rectum); 'middle Qi' sinking.

items.t: Closure (氣閉) — loss of consciousness; Exhaustion (氣脫) — Qi escapes uncontrollably (following bleeding/sweating)

items.detail: Pale lustreless complexion/lips/tongue/nails, dizziness, palpitations, fatigue, numbness, dry eyes.
Slowed/stagnant circulation → fixed stabbing pain, lumps, dark-purplish signs.
Heat in blood + reckless movement → bleeding, Yin damage.

items.t: Stagnation of Qi and blood (氣滯血瘀)
Qi unable to hold blood (氣不攝血) → bleeding
Exhaustion of Qi following blood loss (氣隨血脫)
Deficiency of both Qi and blood (氣血兩虛)
Qi and blood unable to nourish channels (氣血不榮經脈) → numbness, motor/sensory problems
Insufficiency of body fluids (津液不足)
items.detail: Jin easily exhausted & replenished; Ye difficult to damage but hard to replenish once lost.

items.t: Obstruction of distribution/excretion (津液輸布排泄障碍)
items.detail: Dysfunction of LU, SP, LV, SJ, KI → water-damp, phlegm-fluids, edema.

items.t: Retention of fluids blocking Qi (津停氣阻); Qi collapse following fluid loss (氣隨液脫)

rows: Related to LV. LV Yang → wind (肝陽化風); extreme heat generates wind (熱極生風); Yin deficiency (陰虛動風); blood deficiency (血虛生風); blood dryness (血燥生風).
Yang deficiency (esp. SP/KI) → empty-cold + Yin-cold products (water-damp, phlegm).
SP dysfunction → water-damp ('SP deficiency generating damp', 脾虛生濕); relies on KI Yang.
Insufficient fluids → dry; common to LU, ST, LI (dry cough, glossy-red tongue, constipation).
Excess Yang → fire (陽氣過盛化火); stagnation → fire (邪鬱化火); emotions → fire (五志化火); Yin deficiency → empty-fire (陰虛火旺).

blocks.heading: Pathogenesis of Zangfu organs
blocks.lead: Yang Qi (陽氣) = Qi + Yang (warming/motive); Yin blood (陰血) = blood + Yin (nourishing/calming). Root of Yin-Yang = KI; Qi & blood rely on SP/ST.

rows: HT fire; HT Qi/Yang deficiency; HT Yin deficiency; HT blood deficiency; HT blood stagnation
LU Qi deficiency; LU Yin deficiency
SP Qi/Yang deficiency; water-damp in the middle; SP Qi & Yin deficiency
LV Qi stagnation; LV fire; LV blood/Yin deficiency; LV Yang rising; LV wind
KI essence deficiency; KI Qi unsecure; KI Yin deficiency; KI Yang deficiency

items.t: Exuberance/decline of Jingluo's Qi and blood (經絡的氣血偏盛偏衰)
Adverse flow (經絡的氣血逆亂) → circulation problems, bleeding
Stagnant flow (氣血運行不暢); exhaustion (氣血衰竭) → end of life

blocks.heading: Prevention (Yu Fang, 預防)
blocks.lead: Preventing occurrence or further development of disease; 'un-occurred disease' (Wei Bing, 未病).

items.t: Before occurrence — strengthen the right Qi
items.detail: Regulate the mind/emotions (Shen); strengthen the body with exercise; regulate diet, environment, work-rest; use medicinal substances; avoid invasion of evil Qi.
Timely/early diagnosis & proper treatment; treat unaffected organs to control transmission.

items.t: Treatment principles (治則) = general rules; treatment methods (治法) = specific methods based on them
Example — Principle: supplement right Qi & remove evil Qi (扶正祛邪)
Methods (supplement): fortify Qi (益氣), nourish blood (養血), enrich Yin (滋陰), tonify Yang (補陽)
Methods (remove evil): sweating (發汗), vomiting (涌吐), purging (攻下)
Root & branch (標本) are relative: right Qi (root) vs evil Qi (branch); cause (root) vs manifestations (branch); original (root) vs new condition (branch)

items.detail: Most common — cold/heat treated with heat/cold; excess/deficiency with removing/supplementing.
Used with true-false patterns (true heat-false cold, etc.).
Severe/urgent → treat the branch; moderate → treat the root; both urgent → treat together.

blocks.heading: Supplement right Qi & remove evil Qi

rows: Supplement the right Qi (扶正): tonify Qi / warm Yang / nourish blood / enrich Yin
Remove evil Qi (祛邪): resolve exterior (sweating), vomiting, purging, warm the middle, move blood
Supplement & remove at the same time

items.t: Harmonize Yin and Yang (調整陰陽): remove excess (損其偏盛), supplement deficiency (補其偏衰)
Regulate Zangfu (調整臟腑功能): consider effects on other organs
Regulate Qi and blood (調理氣血)
items.detail: 'Qi is the commander of blood; blood is the mother of Qi.' Qi deficiency → tonify Qi + nourish blood; Qi stagnation → tonify/regulate Qi + move blood; Qi unable to hold blood → tonify Qi + check bleeding.
Summer → avoid over-sweating (damages Qi/fluids); winter → avoid cold methods (damages Yang); gentler approaches for children, elderly, pregnancy, weak constitutions.

blocks.heading: Purpose & main sources of infection
blocks.lead: The Clean Needle Technique (CNT) was established to reduce the risk of Hepatitis B and other blood-borne transmission in the clinical setting.

items.detail: Self-produced infection carried from one body location to another (e.g., a needle inserted too deep in the abdomen, then reused elsewhere). Single-use disposable needles control this.
Transferred from another person or the environment. Drives most CNT procedures/requirements.

items.t: Always wash hands between patients, before and after needling
Always use sterile single-use needles/instruments that break skin (lancets, 7-star, plum blossom)
Always establish a clean field before performing acupuncture
Always immediately and safely isolate used needles and swabs
Wash hands between patients and before/after acupuncture, and whenever contaminated
Alcohol sanitizer and medical gloves are NOT substitutes for hand washing — only used when washing is not possible
Gloves required if practitioner has a hand wound (cuts/abrasions)
Practitioner with significant infected lesion (herpes, staph) should not practice until healed — gloves are not acceptable protection in this case
Must be Health-Canada approved medical devices, in intact packaging
Do not use if seal broken, moisture/damage present, or past expiry
Needle shaft must remain sterile before/during insertion; each filiform needle punctures once only
Disposable lancets/3-edged needles strongly recommended for blood-letting

blocks.heading: Establishing the clean field & safe disposal

items.t: Set up without instruments touching clothes/contaminated material; keep alcohol swabs from making the field damp
Sterile needles must never be placed on a non-sterile surface (tray, cotton)
Waste bag and sharps container are set up away from the clean field
Dispose of needles immediately in sharps container without touching it; dispose of cotton/waste without touching bag; wash hands
Wash hands; inspect site for lesions/contraindications; skin must be clean, free of cuts/wounds/infection
Visibly dirty areas (feet, neck) washed first, then swabbed with 70% ethyl/isopropyl alcohol
Swab from center outward in a rotary motion to reduce recontamination risk; allow alcohol to dry
If re-palpating after cleaning, hands must be clean
Open single-use needles/instruments just before use, in front of the patient
Never touch the needle shaft with bare fingers or non-sterile material after removal from packaging
If the shaft becomes contaminated before insertion, discard — do not use
Use sterile gauze to support the shaft once inserted / if inserted without a guide tube; guide tubes must be sterile
Gloves required: patient bleeding, open lesions/contagious disease, practitioner has skin infection/unbandaged wound, or blood-borne exposure risk (lancets, 3-edged, plum blossom/7-star)
Removal: wash hands immediately before; remove without touching shaft/site with bare fingers; dispose immediately in sharps container
Use sterile cotton ball to press insertion site after withdrawal; do not re-palpate with bare finger unless washed/sanitized; wash hands thoroughly after treatment

rows: Dispose immediately after use; discard if seal broken (even unused) or past sterility expiry
Must be pre-sterilized, packaged per needle/set; discard after the treatment session in which the seal is broken
Disposable heads discarded immediately after use; handles cleaned with high-level disinfectant between same-patient sessions; handle discarded once all head packs used on that patient

Not sterile procedure, but clean/disinfect after each patient. Intact skin = low-level disinfectant; non-intact skin (used with needling) = intermediate level; if bleeding occurs, autoclave. Bamboo cups avoided (hard to disinfect)
Must be sterile — disposable strongly recommended
Clean & disinfect between patients; sterilize based on use

blocks.heading: Needlestick accidents
blocks.lead: Injuries from needles that unintentionally puncture skin; can transmit blood-borne infections (Hepatitis B, HIV).

items.t: Always account for every needle inserted, removed, and disposed of
Check patient, bed, linens, and surrounding area before/during/after treatment for needles
When changing linens, hold the sheet by the edges (don't sweep hands over the area) so needles can drop safely
Use gloves, mask, and goggles for blood-letting with 3-edged needle/lancet
Practitioners/staff should be immunized against Hepatitis B and get annual blood tests for HIV, Hepatitis C, other blood-borne disease
Patient source of contamination should be asked to consent to testing (not required to comply)

blocks.heading: Recommended actions after a needlestick accident

items.detail: Let the wound bleed briefly; wash gently with soap and water; apply antiseptic + clean dressing; flush eye/nose/mouth or broken-skin exposure with water for 10+ minutes; wash with soap and water
Review patient history, question patient, seek cooperation; if HBV/HCV/HIV status unclear, seek consent for testing; keep records of consent process

items.t: 3. Refer staff member for specialist care
items.detail: Infectious disease specialist or ER for advice and possible post-exposure prophylaxis, administered as quickly as possible; keep emergency numbers readily available

acu-tech.title: Contraindications, Precautions & Managing Adverse Reactions
acu-tech.titleCn: 禁忌、注意事項與異常情況處理

items.t: Upper abdomen, lumbosacral region, and strongly-sensational points needled with extreme caution, only by experienced practitioners
Some ear points may induce labor

items.detail: Abdomen, lower back, sacral region
SP6, LI4, BL67, BL60, ST12, GB21 — (note: this same contraindication is why LI4, GB21, BL60, BL67 are used clinically to induce labor)

items.t: Contraindicated during menses, except for regulating menses
Avoid needling in the hours before/after: sexual intercourse, eating, drinking alcohol, long-distance travel, etc.
Avoid when patient is in a state of great fright or anger
Practitioners act within scope of practice; may need first aid/CPR or to send patient to emergency care
Stroke or heart attack: call 911 immediately — acupuncture must not be the sole treatment
Acupuncture may aid preparation for/recovery from surgery, but must not replace a required surgical procedure
Not used alone for malignant tumors; needling at tumor site only by advanced, experienced practitioners; may complement pain relief and chemo/radiotherapy side-effect management

rows: Infant fontanelle, external genitalia, nipples, eyeball, umbilicus
LI13 (radial collateral artery/vein), RE8 (navel), ST17 (nipple), LV12 (femoral artery)
Avoid or great caution — including anticoagulant therapy/medication (aspirin excepted)
Extra precautions for inflamed, irritated, lesioned, or broken skin
Points near large blood vessels or vital organs contraindicated for deep insertion

blocks.heading: Additional precautions — needle with caution or not at all if patient is:

items.t: Intoxicated or under the influence of drugs
Confused, emotionally unstable, non-cooperative, or has needle phobia
An unstable diabetic (risk to blood sugar and peripheral circulation)
Has a pacemaker/electronic implant (caution with electro-acupuncture)
Myocardial damage from prior heart-muscle infection
Suppressed immune system (prior infection, anti-rejection meds, high-dose steroids)
Do not needle patients who are over-hungry, tired, or nervous
Know local anatomy: internal organs & safe depths, major blood vessels, nerves in the point area

blocks.heading: Fainting / loss of consciousness
blocks.lead: Usually from nervousness, fatigue, hunger, profuse sweating, severe diarrhea, weak constitution, improper position, or overly forceful manipulation.

items.detail: Nausea/possible vomiting; dizziness, vertigo; visual swaying; chest oppression/palpitations; pale complexion/lips. More severe: weak pulse, cold extremities, cold sweat, low BP, loss of consciousness
Carefully withdraw all needles; lie patient flat, head down, feet elevated, keep warm; offer warm water or sugar water; most recover with rest — seek medical help if symptoms persist
Gentle needle manipulation; monitor early signs; treat susceptible patients lying down or well-supported while seated
Muscle spasm/sudden movement, or rotation too wide/one-directional entangling muscle fibers

Reassure patient, ask to relax; may need to leave needle in place, or tap/massage around the point; needling a nearby point can help relax the muscle; if entangled, gently rotate in the opposite direction to loosen
Poor needle quality, erosion/cracks at shaft-handle junction, sudden patient movement/spasm, improper withdrawal, excessive force, external force striking the needle
Withdraw slowly, following the course of the bend, and replace
Never insert to the handle — always keep at least 1/4 of the shaft exposed above skin; examine needles beforehand; avoid excessive manipulation force; remind patients to stay still

Keep patient still/calm (prevents sinking deeper); if visible above skin, remove gently with forceps; if at skin level, press around site to expose the end, then remove; if completely under skin, seek medical/surgical assistance — do not cut the skin yourself

items.t: Apply compression with cotton balls for a few seconds; cold compression if needed

acu-tech.title: Acupuncture Needles: Structure, Specification & Practice
acu-tech.titleCn: 毫針的結構、規格與練習

blocks.heading: Structure and specification of filiform needles

image.caption: Filiform needle structure: tip, body, root, handle, tail

items.t: Common material: stainless steel (flexible, strong, oxidation resistant)
Note: silver, gold, or alloys are sometimes used

items.detail: Tip (Point) → Body → Root → Handle → Tail

blocks.heading: Length & diameter of commonly used filiform needles

items.detail: Too sharp (may break off) or too blunt (causes pain)
Burrs — check by dragging a dry cotton ball along the needle
Cracks, corrosion

items.t: Begin with 1′ (0.3mm thickness) needles, progress to longer or thinner needles

items.detail: Cotton ball / cloth pad → practitioner's own arms or legs → fellow students

items.t: Choose the posture most comfortable for the patient, allowing them to stay still for 5–60 min and giving access to needed points

items.detail: Supine (仰臥, face up) · Prone (俯臥, face down) · Lateral recumbent (側臥) · Sitting (several variations)

items.t: Sitting may not suit nervous first-timers, tired, or severely ill patients — fainting risk
Pillows or other supports may maximize comfort
Treatment may be split into 2+ sections with different postures to access all needed points
Clean the point with a cotton ball soaked in 75% alcohol (optionally 2.5% iodine first, then remove with 75% alcohol)
Clean practitioner's hands: wash with soap, then rub with 75% alcohol
Avoid touching the body of the needle

items.detail: Holds the needle with thumb, index, middle fingers. 1) Forces/controls the tip to penetrate skin level. 2) Manipulates the needle with the necessary technique

Presses firmly near the point with the nail, supporting the needle body. 1) Searches/fixes the point. 2) Guides the tip with the nail. 3) Holds the body while inserting/manipulating. 4) Avoids injuring blood vessels during insertion

items.t: 1) Penetrating the superficial layers of skin
2) Moving the needle to the desired depth

items.detail: For short needles (2′ or shorter). Press the nail (left thumb/index) onto the skin over the point; support the needle tip against the nail while holding the needle with the right hand; insert next to the nail

For long needles on thick musculature (hips, legs) or joining points. Hold the needle tip with the pressing hand (dry cotton ball) while inserting — or alternatively hold the tip, leaving 5–10mm exposed, with the puncturing hand
Spread loose/folded skin (abdomen, elderly) to increase surface tension for easier penetration
Pinch thin skin (face, head) for easier penetration

items.t: Requires an insertion tube (plastic or steel) and tail-less needles

items.detail: 1) Gentle tapping of the needle into the skin. 2) Removing the guide tube. 3) Inserting the needle to the appropriate depth

acu-tech.title: Angle & Depth of Insertion, Withdrawing the Needle

blocks.heading: Angle of insertion
blocks.lead: Correct angle and depth bring both therapeutic effect and safety.

rows: Most common — used on most points
Where an internal organ lies beneath, or subcutaneous flesh is thin
Little flesh beneath skin (head, face, neck), or when joining a few points with one needle

items.t: Local anatomy — avoiding excessively deep insertion
Whether De Qi has been obtained
Patient constitution, age, season (summer vs. winter), and pathogen depth (superficial vs. deep)
Rotate slightly while withdrawing (prevents adhesion to tissue) while pressing the skin
Withdraw to just below the skin and retain briefly before full withdrawal — prevents bleeding and reduces post-needling pain
Slow, careful withdrawal around the eyes, with longer pressing afterward
Swab the area with alcohol-soaked cotton if necessary
Speed and other details depend on the desired effect — supplementing or draining

acu-tech.title: Needle Manipulation: Obtaining Qi & Manipulation Techniques

blocks.heading: Obtaining / Arrival of Qi (De Qi, 得氣)

items.detail: Moderate, sinking, or tight sensation under the needle tip ("fish bite")
Soreness, numbness, heaviness, distension around the point, or a sensation travelling/transmitting in a direction

items.t: If De Qi is not achieved
items.detail: Practitioner feels a hollow, smooth, or loose sensation; patient feels nothing
"The needling sensation must be achieved no matter how many methods are used or how long it may take"

items.t: <Great Compendium of Acupuncture and Moxibustion, 鍼灸大成>
items.detail: "De Qi alone is the measure of the treatment. If the Qi does not arrive, there is no treatment"
Incorrect point location, imperfect manipulation, wrong angle/depth, weak constitution, etc.
Lift a short distance, then thrust back to original depth; maintain constant direction of insertion; avoid raising too far or too deep
Grasp the handle between thumb and forefinger, twist first one way then the other; maintain constant depth; generally avoid exceeding one full turn (360°)
The two basic techniques may be used independently or combined

blocks.heading: Secondary manipulation techniques
blocks.lead: Purpose: hasten and promote the circulation of Qi and blood in the channel to facilitate De Qi.

image.caption: Massage · Trembling · Shaking · Twisting · Flicking · Scraping

items.detail: Gently massage/press/tap above and below the point along the channel; disperses Qi and blood when there is tense sensation (pain) around the needle
Gently scrape (repeated upward motion with the nail of the forefinger) the handle of the needle with the thumb on the tail
Gently flick the needle handle to cause the body to vibrate
Rotate 2–3 or 3–5 times each round in one direction while lifting/thrusting, to avoid entwining muscle fiber
Shake the handle of the needle
Apply swift lifting/thrusting movements of small amplitude to cause a type of vibration
Used to treat excess syndrome (實證)
Used to treat deficiency syndrome (虛證)
To harmonize Yin and Yang (陰陽)
Use either method after De Qi is achieved; one or more methods may be combined in practice
ST36 (足三里), RE4 (關元)
LI11 (曲池), Shi Xuan (十宣)

rows: Gently and slowly, small amplitude, short duration
Rapidly and forcefully, large amplitude, long duration
Heavy thrusting, gentle lifting, short duration
Gentle thrusting, heavy-swift lifting, long duration
Slow, deliberate insertion (less rotation), rapid withdrawal
Needling along the direction of Qi flow in the channel
Needling against the direction of Qi flow
Insert on exhalation, withdraw on inhalation
Insert on inhalation, withdraw on exhalation
Press the hole as soon as the needle is withdrawn
Enlarge the hole as the needle is withdrawn

items.t: Even rotation or lifting-thrusting — neither tonifying nor sedating

image.caption: Heaven (天, just below skin) · Human (人, intermediate) · Earth (地, deepest)

items.detail: Heaven (天, just below skin) · Human (人, intermediate) · Earth (地, deepest)

items.t: Suitable only for points in thick muscle — not used on the head or chest
Only 1–2 points commonly chosen per method in a single treatment

blocks.heading: 1) Setting the mountain on fire (燒山火, Burning mountain fire)

items.t: 1. Insert to heaven level, manipulate with supplementing method
2. Insert to human level, manipulate with supplementing method
3. Insert to earth level, manipulate with supplementing method

items.detail: Repeat until a warm, slightly burning sensation is achieved — usually 3 trials with a break. At each level, combine 9-rotation or lifting-thrusting with breathing & speed
Deficiency-cold conditions — cold-type Bi syndrome, impotence, incontinence, etc.

blocks.heading: 2) Penetrating the heaven's coolness (透天涼, Penetrating heaven cooling)

items.t: 1. Insert to earth level, manipulate with draining method
2. Insert to human level, manipulate with draining method
3. Insert to heaven level, manipulate with draining method

items.detail: Repeat until a cool, clear sensation is achieved — usually 3 trials with a break. At each level, combine 6-rotation or lifting-thrusting with breathing & speed
Excess-heat conditions — heat-type Bi syndrome, high fever, mania, etc.

Strengthens the function of acupuncture (relieving pain, quieting the spirit, etc.); allows other stimulation methods (warming needle, electrical, intermittent) — or may retain without additional manipulation
Clinically determined by patient constitution and condition; needle may be withdrawn after De Qi, or retained from a few minutes to a few hours
Frees the flow of Qi and blood; disperses cold and eliminates damp; warms Yang; prevents disease
Mugwort (Ai Ye, 艾葉) — warm; bitter & pungent; enters LV, SP, KI; disperses cold, alleviates pain, warms channels, stops bleeding. Good consistency for cones, burns well, commonly available

blocks.heading: Mugwort (Ai Ye, 艾葉; Artemisia Argyi) grading

rows: Gentler cauterization, less smoke/odor, easily shaped, allows thread/sesame-size cones
Heats rather than cauterizes; needs larger quantity

items.t: Harvested in early summer after leaves develop their woolly underside; dried, aged, crushed and sifted to separate wool from unwanted material

items.detail: Direct moxa: scarring (瘢痕灸) / non-scarring (無瘢痕灸). Indirect moxa: ginger-, garlic-, salt-, Fu Zi-insulated
Moxa sticks (艾條灸), incl. Great Monad's herbal moxa stick (太乙鍼灸) and Thunder-Fire moxa stick (雷火鍼灸); Warming the needle (溫鍼灸); Warming moxa box (溫灸器灸)
Burning Rush moxibustion (燈草灸); Mustard Seed / blister-inducing moxibustion (白芥子灸, 天灸, 發泡法)

blocks.heading: Direct moxibustion (直接灸) — burning cones on the skin
blocks.lead: Common cone sizes (by location/age): wheat (麥粒) < Cang Er Zi/Xanthium (蒼耳子) < Lian Zi/Lotus Seed (蓮子).

items.t: Scarring moxibustion (瘢痕灸, 化膿灸)
items.detail: Most potent form — no blister means no healing. Used for empty-cold or damp-cold patterns. Garlic juice (or similar) secures the cone. Process: burn cone completely on the point → wipe clean and repeat prescribed number of rolls → blister forms in 1–3 days (may take up to 1 month to heal), leaving a small scar → keep clean, change dressing to prevent infection
Same as direct moxa, but cone is removed at 3/5–3/4 burnt, when the patient feels pain, or when skin turns red. Accidental scarring possible. Less potent — used for milder deficiency-cold patterns

blocks.heading: Indirect moxibustion (間接灸) — insulating medium between cone and skin

rows: Perforated ginger slice (~0.2″) on point, cone burned on top; replace slice after 4–5 cones
Vomiting, abdominal fullness/pain, diarrhea from cold; middle-jiao deficiency; shan qi; wind-cold-damp Bi; wind-cold exterior; general Yang deficiency
Fill umbilicus with salt level with abdomen (or salt atop a ginger slice), burn cone on top
Cold damage; cholera with vomiting/diarrhea; wind-strike desertion pattern; returns Yang, stems counter-flow, secures against desertion
Perforated garlic slice (~0.2″) on point, cone on top; replace after 4–5 cones
Deficiency taxation; sore toxicity; scrofulous swellings; abdominal masses; early-stage skin ulcers with boils
Fu Zi powder mixed with rice wine, formed into a ~0.1″ perforated crust, used like garlic method
General Yang deficiency; enduring lesions/boils (stop pustulation, permit healing); impotence, premature ejaculation from Ming Men fire decline

items.t: Cylinder-shaped poles (Ai Ye + herbs e.g. Rou Gui, Gan Jiang, Du Huo); held ~1″ above the point, moved in slow circular motion for 3–15 min until skin reddens
The Great Monad's herbal moxa stick (太乙鍼灸)
items.detail: 24g fine powder (Ren Shen, Chuan Shan Jia, Shan Yang Xue, Qian Nian Jian, Chan Di Feng, Rou Gui, Xiao Hui Xiang, Cang Zhu, Gan Cao, Fang Feng, She Xiang) + Ai Rong

items.t: The Thunder-Fire moxa stick (雷火鍼灸)
items.detail: Ai Rong 125g + Chen Xiang, Mu Xiang, Ru Xiang, Qiang Huo, Gan Jiang, Qian Nian Jian (9g each) + She Xiang. Used for wind-cold-damp Bi joint pain, hemiplegia, motor impairment
Point stick at ~2–3cm distance until skin reddens; for chronic conditions
Move the stick rapidly (vertically/transversely/circularly) over the point; for acute conditions

blocks.heading: Warming the needle & warming moxa box

items.detail: Insert needle; wrap moxa wool (protected by foil/paper) around the handle and light it. Alternative: place a small perforated moxa roll on top of the needle handle — safer, ash less likely to fall
Moxa wool burned inside a box with a cloth barrier against the skin; box moved slowly over the area until skin reddens. Particularly useful for women, children, sensitive skin

blocks.heading: Other types of moxa (non Ai Ye)

items.detail: Dip Deng Xin Cao in sesame oil, light it, quickly apply to the point and remove immediately. For abdominal pain, stomachache, acute gastroenteritis

items.t: Mustard Seed / blister-inducing moxibustion (白芥子灸, 天灸, 發泡法)
items.detail: "Cold" moxa — no ignition. Bai Jie Zi powder + water paste applied to the point, left until blisters form naturally. For Bi syndrome, facial paralysis, asthma

Upper region before lower; Yang areas before Yin; fewer-cone areas before more-cone areas; small cones before larger cones. Example: rectal prolapse → DU1 first (draws anus back), then DU20 (elevates Qi in middle Jiao)
"以火補者，毋吹其火，須自滅也. 以火瀉之，疾吹其火，傳其艾，須其火滅也." — Supplementing: let the cone burn out on its own, don't extinguish artificially. Draining: blow on it (faster burning) and add another cone until extinguished
Blisters usually heal untreated; large/non-healing blisters should be punctured, drained, and dressed if infected

items.t: No moxibustion for excess heat (實熱證) or fever from Yin deficiency (陰虛發熱)
No scarring moxa on the face, 5 sensory organs, or major blood vessels
No moxa on the abdomen/lower back of pregnant women
Avoid on patients who are hungry, over-eaten, or intoxicated
ST36, LI11, LI4, SP6 · BL12, BL23, BL43, KI1 · RE4, RE6, RE8, RE12 · DU4, DU12, DU14

blocks.heading: 3-edged needle (三稜鍼, 鏠鍼) — Sharp-Edged Needle
blocks.lead: A thick, sharp, 3-edged needle used for blood-letting; largely replaced by pre-sterilized disposable lancets in modern clinics.

items.detail: "For stubborn stagnation, blockage of the channels, or diseases of the 5 Zang organs with long-term stagnancy... it causes bleeding by pricking to eliminate heat and treat stubborn diseases from stasis"

Excess (cold or heat) syndromes: unconsciousness, hyperpyrexia, heatstroke, tense wind-strike syndromes, acute sore throat, congested/swollen eyes, stubborn ringworm, early carbuncles, sprains, contusions, malnutrition, indigestion, hemorrhoids, Bi syndrome, headache, erysipelas, numb fingers/toes, etc.
Weak, pregnant, or postpartum patients; hemorrhagic patients; anemia or low blood pressure

Apply pressure to restrict blood flow (increases vein visibility) → clean the area → prick swiftly & decisively to ~0.1″ depth → allow/squeeze a few drops of blood → press with sterile cotton until bleeding stops

items.t: Sterilizing local area and needles is important

items.detail: Tap (hold ~2″ above the skin, strike perpendicularly with a loose constant wrist movement) over points, affected skin, or along the channel

items.t: Light tapping = no bleeding; heavy tapping = slight bleeding (used for blood stasis)

items.detail: Headache, back pain, skin numbness, hypertension, menstrual pain, some skin conditions

items.t: Contraindicated for ulcerations or traumatic injuries

items.detail: Grain type (seed) and tack-needle type

items.t: Retained generally 1–3 days depending on weather (temperature)

items.detail: Chronic/stubborn/painful conditions — headache, dysmenorrhea, insomnia, hypertension, Bi syndrome

blocks.heading: Cupping (拔罐, 附缸)
blocks.lead: Attaching a jar (glass, bamboo, plastic, ceramic) to the skin, causing local congestion via negative-pressure suction.

items.detail: Hold 95% alcohol-soaked cotton with forceps, ignite; circle the fire inside the cup briefly to burn the air, then quickly place on skin. Variations: fire throwing, cotton-wool sticking, fire in the lid
Boil the cup in plain or herb water (Dang Gui, Du Huo, Hong Hua, Ma Huang, Ai Ye...); cover opening with a cold towel then rapidly place on site. For wind-cold-damp Bi, cough, stomachache, vomiting
10–15 minutes, or repeat placing/removing until local congestion appears; may blood-let afterward
Lubricating oil on cup rim and skin, then fire cupping or Guasha-like scraping — for numbness, pain, Bi syndrome
Prick the area with a 3-edged needle, then cup to induce bleeding
Place a cup over an inserted needle

items.t: Gently release suction around the cup rim when withdrawing — forceful removal is painful
Patient should be in a comfortable position
Careful not to burn the skin

items.detail: Skin ulcers, edema, areas over large blood vessels; high fever, convulsion; abdominal/sacral region of pregnant women
Chinese soup spoon, worn coin, honed animal bone, water buffalo horn, jade, etc.

items.t: "Sha-bruising" (light bruising) from repeated scraping is part of the treatment

items.detail: Releases unhealthy elements from injured areas, stimulates blood flow and healing
Apply massage oil to skin and tool edge → press the smooth edge firmly, move down the muscles/along channels → repeat pressured strokes until Sha-bruises appear

items.t: Regular needling + electrical stimulation — a small current passed between pairs of acupuncture needles
Length and intensity of stimulation are controllable via the electro-stimulator
2. Connect needles to the electro-stimulator (power off)
3. Turn power on, select desired waveform
4. Slowly increase output to the desired level
Points selected in pairs to conduct current, usually unilateral (one side)
Usually 1–3 pairs — too many points causes overly strong (painful) stimulation
Intensity kept between sensory threshold (first noticeable stimulation) and pain threshold (first sign of pain) — a very narrow range requiring slow, careful adjustment

rows: Relieve pain, encourage tranquility, relieve muscular/vascular spasm, acupuncture anesthesia
Pain relief, sprains, bruising, peripheral shoulder arthritis, stagnant Qi/blood, sciatica, facial paralysis, myasthenia, local cold injuries

items.detail: Same range as filiform needle — all kinds of pain, Bi/Wei syndrome, muscle/ligament/joint injuries, acupuncture anesthesia

items.t: Avoid accidental electrical shocks; avoid breaking needles or tissue burns from direct current contact
Increase intensity slowly and carefully — sudden change may cause muscular contraction ("electrical torturing")
Avoid the current loop passing through the heart

blocks.heading: Overview & nomenclature
blocks.lead: WHO (international) standard scalp acupuncture + other lines. MS = "micro-system" + "scalp-point". Locations/indications correspond to acupuncture channels and functional zones of the brain. 14 lines total: MS1–MS14.

image.caption: Scalp functional zones — side view (motor / premotor / sensory / auditory / visual areas, cerebellum)
Scalp functional zones — back view (visual area, cerebellum, inion, atlas)

rows: Coronary artery disease, chest pain, asthma, bronchitis, insomnia
Acute/chronic gastritis, gastric/intestinal ulcer, LV/GB diseases
1″ straight down from 0.75cm medial to ST08 (頭維)
Functional uterine bleeding, impotence, seminal emission, uterine prolapse, frequent/urgent urination
Lower back/thigh/foot conditions (paralysis, numbness, pain), cortical polyuria, rectal prolapse, enuresis, hypertension, apex headache

blocks.heading: MS6–MS7 — Points on the sides (temporal) of the head

image.caption: MS6 (anterior oblique) & MS7 (posterior oblique) temporal lines

rows: Anterior point (1″ anterior to DU20) of Sishencong (四神聰) to GB06 (懸釐)
5 divisions — motor: upper 1/5 lower limb, middle 2/5 upper limb, lower 2/5 face/aphasia/drooling (opposite side)
5 divisions — sensory: upper 1/5 lower limb, middle 2/5 upper limb, lower 2/5 face numbness (opposite side)

image.caption: MS8, MS9, MS10, MS11 side-of-head lines

rows: 1.5″ backward from BL07 (通天), along BL channel
Lower back and thigh conditions (paralysis, numbness, pain)
1.5″ backward from GB17 (正營), along GB channel
One-sided headache (migraine), aphasia, facial numbness, mouth diseases
One-sided headache (migraine), tinnitus, hearing loss, dizziness

blocks.heading: MS12–MS14 — Points on the back (occipital) of the head

rows: Difficulty maintaining balance (cerebral origin), occipital headache

items.detail: Post-stroke paralysis/numbness/aphasia, cortical polyuria, dizziness, tinnitus, epilepsy, decreased/underdeveloped mental-intellectual ability
Headache, hair loss, paralysis from spinal injury, hypertension, mental disorders, insomnia, eye/nose disorders, shoulder pain, lower back/thigh pain, pain of any nature
Sitting or lying down

1.5–3″ filiform needles. After sterilization, quickly insert at ~30° to the scalp, proceed until resistance reduces (loose connective tissue reached), then direct parallel to the scalp and insert further (0.5–3″) with rotation
Hold the handle between thumb and index finger; rotate ~200 times/min for 2–3 min (or use electrical stimulation instead); retain for 20–30 min, manipulating (rotating) 2–3 times while retained
About 3–5 min after manipulation: burning, numb, distending, and/or twitching sensation in the affected area

Secure/press skin around the point with the pressing hand; gently rotate with the puncturing hand while withdrawing; quick withdrawal while resistance-free at the tip, slow down, full withdrawal; ensure proper sterilization and prevent bleeding

items.t: Relatively strong stimulation — possible dizziness/nausea/unconsciousness (暈鍼)
No scalp acupuncture on infants with an unclosed fontanel
No scalp acupuncture during wind-stroke episode or excessively high blood pressure
Extreme caution with high fever, acute inflammation, heart failure
Apply pressure with cotton balls/dry patches for 1–2 minutes if bleeding occurs after withdrawal

blocks.heading: Indications of the ear points
blocks.lead: Most point names are self-explanatory; indications draw on both TCM and Western theory. Commonly used for pain and addiction.

rows: Numbness, pain, sprain, arthritis, contusion of the corresponding joint
Abdominal cavity, digestive system & gynecological diseases

Menstrual disorders, pelvic inflammation, impotence, seminal emission
Insomnia, irritability, inflammation, asthma, cough, vertigo, urticaria; sedative & analgesic
Digestive/circulatory dysfunction, acute infantile convulsions, asthma, dysmenorrhea
Furuncles, rhinitis / maxillary sinusitis, common cold
Nocturnal enuresis, uterine bleeding, acute infantile convulsions
Asthma, cough, mumps, nocturnal enuresis, acute infantile convulsion

Tooth 1/2, Tongue, Maxilla, Mandible, Eye, Cheek, Internal Ear, Tonsil
Tooth extraction/toothache; tongue swelling/aphasia; jaw pain; conjunctivitis/myopia; trigeminal neuralgia/facial acne; tinnitus/otitis media; tonsillitis
Skin diseases, headache, sciatica, lumbar/back pain, cough, asthma; Anti-hypertensive groove — hypertension

items.detail: e.g. stomach disease → Stomach; shoulder pain → Shoulder; sore throat → Throat

e.g. hypertension → Anti-hypertensive Groove; duodenal ulcer → Duodenum; irregular menses → Uterus
e.g. congested/swollen/painful eye → Ear Apex; depressive-manic psychosis → Ear Shenmen; toothache → Tooth

items.t: Fewer is better — select about 2–3 points per treatment in general
One-sided disorder → select points on the same or opposite side
Bilateral disorders or internal-organ disease → bilateral selection

items.detail: Observe with eyes (form/structural changes, discoloration, papules, scaling, nodules, congestion, depressions, blisters); detect tender spots with a small pointed tool (gentle, even pressure); detect lower electrical resistance in the affected area (higher conduction)
75% alcohol, or 2% iodine followed by 75% alcohol
Short filiform needle; thumbtack needle (皮內鍼); electro-needling; point injection (0.1–0.3mL); magnets or ear seeds (pressed periodically)
Short filiform needle: 10–30 min generally, 1–2 hours for pain. Embedded needles: 2–3 days or 7–10 days depending on weather (infection risk)
Press the hole with a dry cotton ball (bleeding); sterilize the site again with iodine or alcohol (infection prevention)

items.t: 1–2 treatments/day for acute conditions; every day or every other day for chronic conditions
8–12 treatments = one course; 1 week break between courses
Electrical resistance varies even in healthy people — interpret carefully alongside diagnosis
Contraindicated for pregnant women (especially history of spontaneous abortion); caution with the aged/weak
For sprain/motor-impairment conditions, move, massage, or moxa the affected site after the ear needling sensation is obtained

acu-points.title: Introduction to Channels & Collaterals
acu-points.source: Compiled from standard TCM channel theory

blocks.heading: What are the channels and collaterals?
blocks.lead: The channel system (經絡, Jing Luo) is the network that connects the interior and exterior of the body, links the Zang-Fu organs to the limbs and sensory orifices, and is the pathway through which Qi and Blood circulate.

items.detail: "Channel" or "meridian" — the main trunks, running longitudinally and relatively deep
"Collateral" — smaller branches connecting channels to each other and to the body surface
12 Primary Channels, 12 Divergent Channels, 8 Extraordinary Vessels, 15 (or 16) Luo-connecting channels, 12 Sinew Channels, 12 Cutaneous Regions, and countless Minute Collaterals (Sun Luo, 孫絡)

items.t: Connecting interior and exterior, upper and lower
items.detail: Link Zang-Fu organs to the limbs, sense organs, skin, muscles, sinews and bones into one integrated whole

items.t: Transporting Qi, Blood, and Body Fluids
items.detail: Nourish tissues and organs and maintain normal physiological activity
External pathogens invade via the channels from the exterior to the interior; internal organ disease can also manifest along channel pathways (referred pain, channel-associated symptoms)
The basis for acupuncture, moxibustion, acupressure, and Tui Na — stimulating a point transmits an effect along its channel to distant/internal areas

blocks.heading: The Twelve Primary Channels (十二正經)
blocks.lead: The core of the whole system. Each is named by a combination of Hand/Foot, Yin/Yang (Tai Yin, Shao Yin, Jue Yin, Yang Ming, Tai Yang, Shao Yang), and its associated Zang or Fu organ.

items.detail: Hand channels run through the arm; Foot channels through the leg. Yin channels are on the medial/anterior aspect and belong to Zang (solid) organs; Yang channels are on the lateral/posterior aspect and belong to Fu (hollow) organs

Lung → Large Intestine → Stomach → Spleen → Heart → Small Intestine → Bladder → Kidney → Pericardium → Sanjiao → Gallbladder → Liver → (back to Lung) — a continuous 24-hour cycle, each channel governing a 2-hour period

Yin channels of the arm run on the medial arm (Tai Yin anterior, Jue Yin middle, Shao Yin posterior); Yang channels of the arm run on the lateral arm (Yang Ming anterior, Shao Yang middle, Tai Yang posterior). On the leg, the pattern is broadly mirrored, with the exception that at the ankle/lower leg, Liver (Jue Yin) crosses in front of Spleen (Tai Yin) below the mid-calf

Each Yin channel pairs with a Yang channel of the same name/limb (e.g. Lung–Large Intestine, Stomach–Spleen), connected by their Luo-connecting points; paired organs share a close physiological relationship

blocks.heading: The Twelve Divergent Channels (十二經別)

items.t: Branches that split off from the 12 primary channels, usually near an elbow or knee joint, and travel deeper into the body

items.detail: Strengthen the interior-exterior relationship between paired Yin-Yang channels; connect the primary channels more closely to their internal Zang-Fu organs; reach areas the primary channels do not directly access (e.g., the heart, certain sense organs)

Described as "leaving, entering, emerging, joining" (離入出合) — a divergent channel leaves its primary channel, enters the chest/abdomen to link with its Zang-Fu organ, emerges at the neck, and joins (rejoins) its paired Yang channel

items.t: The 6 pairs form the "Six Confluences" (六合)
items.detail: Each Yin-Yang pair of divergent channels converges at one point, reinforcing the 12 exterior-interior relationships

blocks.heading: The Eight Extraordinary Vessels (奇經八脈)

items.detail: Du Mai (督脈, Governing Vessel), Ren Mai (任脈, Conception Vessel), Chong Mai (衝脈, Penetrating Vessel), Dai Mai (帶脈, Belt Vessel), Yin Wei Mai (陰維脈), Yang Wei Mai (陽維脈), Yin Qiao Mai (陰蹻脈), Yang Qiao Mai (陽蹻脈)

They are not directly linked to specific Zang-Fu organs, have no strict Yin-Yang exterior-interior pairing, and (except Du and Ren) have no points of their own — they borrow points from the 12 primary channels and Du/Ren

Act as reservoirs that regulate the flow of Qi and Blood in the 12 primary channels — like lakes overflowing from and draining back into rivers. Also bind the primary channels together (Dai Mai especially, which is the only channel running horizontally)
The only two of the eight with their own dedicated points; together with the 12 primary channels they make up the "14 channels" (十四經) that carry the majority of acupuncture points

One arising from the Luo-connecting point of each of the 12 primary channels, plus one each from Du Mai, Ren Mai, and the Great Luo of the Spleen (脾之大絡). (Some texts add the Great Luo of the Stomach for 16.)

Strengthen the union between exteriorly-interiorly related channels; each Luo-connecting channel spreads over the area between its point of origin and the region of its paired channel, reinforcing communication between them
Luo-connecting points can treat disorders of either the connecting channel's own channel or its paired channel — a foundation of the point-selection method "host-guest" (主客配穴)

blocks.heading: The Twelve Sinew Channels (十二經筋)

items.t: The most superficial part of the channel system — bind muscles, tendons and joints along the general course of the 12 primary channels

items.detail: Bind bones and joints together, maintain normal movement, and protect the deeper channels and organs
Unlike the primary channels, sinew channels do not connect to internal organs, and all originate at the extremities, moving centripetally toward the head/trunk
The basis for treating musculoskeletal / sinew disorders (Bi syndrome, sprains, spasm) — commonly treated with "Ashi" points and local/distal needling along the sinew pathway

blocks.heading: The Twelve Cutaneous Regions (十二皮部)

items.t: The most superficial layer, corresponding to the skin areas overlying each of the 12 primary channels and their branches

items.detail: The first line of defense against external pathogenic factors, and a site where internal disharmony can be reflected onto the body surface (changes in skin color, temperature, sensitivity)
Basis for cutaneous therapies — cupping, Guasha, plum-blossom (cutaneous) needling, and diagnostic skin observation
The smallest, most numerous branches of the Luo-connecting channels, spreading Qi and Blood to every part of the body, down to the smallest tissues
Superficial collaterals visible at the skin surface — the basis for observing surface veins/vessels (e.g. the finger veins in pediatric diagnosis, or sublingual veins) as a diagnostic sign

acu-points.title: Point Categories
acu-points.source: Compiled from standard TCM point-category theory

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.lead: "Special/specific points" (特定穴) are acupoints with a particular clinical significance beyond their location — grouped by function, not by channel. Knowing these categories is essential both for point selection and for exam questions.

items.t: ~15 categories in total, but a handful are tested and used far more than the rest: Five Shu (Antique) points, Yuan-source, Luo-connecting, Xi-cleft, Back-Shu, Front-Mu, and the Eight Confluent points

blocks.heading: Five Shu (Antique) Points (五輸穴)
blocks.lead: Each of the 12 primary channels has 5 points below the elbow/knee, likened to the flow of water from its source to the sea.

rows: Loss of consciousness, fullness under the heart, emotional/mental disorders — often bled
Heaviness of the body, joint pain (Bi syndrome)
Cough, asthma, sore throat, alternating chills & fever
Rebellious Qi, diarrhea, disorders of the Fu organs

items.detail: On each channel, the Five Shu point corresponding to the "mother" element (in the Sheng cycle) tonifies; the point corresponding to the "son" element drains — the classical basis for Five-Element point selection

items.t: One per primary channel (12 total), located near the wrist/ankle

items.detail: Where the Yuan (Source) Qi of the corresponding Zang-Fu organ is retained and can be accessed; reflect and regulate the state of that organ's Qi
The Yuan-source point coincides with the Shu-stream (Earth) point of the Five Shu set

Diagnostic (tenderness/change reflects organ disharmony) and therapeutic (tonify deficiency of the corresponding organ); often combined with the paired channel's Luo-connecting point ("host-guest", 主客配穴)

items.t: 15 (or 16) total — one for each of the 12 primary channels, plus Du Mai, Ren Mai, and the Great Luo of the Spleen

items.detail: The point where a channel's Luo-connecting vessel branches off to link with its exteriorly-interiorly paired channel
Treats disorders of both its own channel and its paired channel; "host-guest" pairing uses the Yuan point of the diseased channel + the Luo point of its paired channel

items.t: 16 total — one for each of the 12 primary channels plus the 4 limb-based extraordinary vessels (Yin Wei, Yang Wei, Yin Qiao, Yang Qiao)

items.detail: The point where a channel's Qi and Blood gather most deeply/intensely
First choice for acute and painful conditions of that channel/organ, especially acute pain and bleeding disorders (e.g. LU6 for hemoptysis, ST34 for acute stomach pain)

items.t: 12 points on the 1st line of the Bladder channel on the back, each named for and roughly level with its associated Zang-Fu organ

items.detail: The place where the Qi of a Zang-Fu organ is transported to the back; reflects and treats disorders of that organ, especially chronic/deficiency conditions of Zang organs
Often combined with the corresponding Front-Mu point (front-back / Yin-Yang pairing, 俞募配穴) for diagnosis and treatment

items.t: 12 points on the chest/abdomen, each associated with a Zang-Fu organ (some coincide with Ren Mai points)

items.detail: The place where the Qi of a Zang-Fu organ gathers on the front of the body; especially reflective of acute/excess disorders of Fu organs
Diagnostic tenderness points; paired with Back-Shu points for combined front-back treatment

items.t: Each point is the place where Qi of that tissue/substance converges most strongly, and is chosen for disorders of that tissue regardless of which channel it lies on

blocks.heading: Eight Confluent (Master/Coupled) Points (八脈交會穴)
blocks.lead: Each of the 8 Extraordinary Vessels connects to the 12 primary channels through one of these points on the limbs, and the 8 pair up into 4 functional couples.

rows: BL62 — inner canthus, neck, ear, shoulder, back
SI3 — inner canthus, neck, ear, shoulder, back
GB41 — outer canthus, ear, cheek, neck, shoulder
TE5 — outer canthus, ear, cheek, neck, shoulder

items.detail: 6 points on the leg, one per Fu organ (including 3 extra for the Sanjiao, Small Intestine, Large Intestine, which "lower He-sea" on the Stomach/Bladder channels) — first choice for Fu-organ (bowel) disorders
Points where two or more channels intersect — can treat disorders of any of the crossing channels (e.g. SP6, crossing point of the 3 leg Yin channels)

items.t: Window of the Sky points (天窗穴/天牖穴)
items.detail: A set of points around the neck/upper chest classically used for disorders of Qi rising to the head (dizziness, loss of voice, loss of consciousness)

items.t: Four Command points (四總穴) — not a special-point category but a classical mnemonic
items.detail: "Face and mouth — LI4; abdomen — ST36; back/lower back — BL40; head/nape — LU7 (or GB20)"

acu-points.title: Lung Channel (LU) — Shou Tai Yin
acu-points.source: Compiled from standard TCM point location/indication data

blocks.heading: Channel pathway
blocks.lead: Starts in the middle burner, descends to connect with the Large Intestine, turns back up through the cardia and diaphragm to enter the Lung; from the "lung system" at the throat it emerges below the clavicle (LU1), runs down the anterior-lateral (radial) aspect of the arm — in front of the Heart and Pericardium channels — crosses the cubital fossa, continues down the forearm, reaches the radial styloid, crosses the thenar eminence, and ends at the radial side of the thumb tip (LU11). A branch leaves above the wrist (near LU7) and runs to the radial side of the index finger, connecting to the Large Intestine channel (LI1).

items.t: Hand Tai Yin, paired (exterior-interior) with the Large Intestine channel
First channel in the 12-channel Qi circulation — "where Qi and Blood begin their cycle"
Governs Qi and respiration; associated with the skin/body hair, nose, and the emotion of grief/sadness

blocks.heading: Points of the Lung channel (LU1–LU11)
blocks.lead: ★ = high-yield / most frequently used and tested points on this channel.

rows: Front-Mu of the Lung; meeting point of Lung & Spleen channels
Tonifies and descends Lung Qi; clears Lung heat; regulates water passages
1 cun below LU2, 1st intercostal space, 6 cun lateral to the anterior midline
Cough, asthma, chest fullness/pain, shoulder & back pain
Oblique/transverse 0.5–0.8 cun laterally — never needle deeply or medially (pneumothorax risk)
Descends Lung Qi; clears the chest; alleviates cough and asthma
In the infraclavicular fossa, lateral to the coracoid process, 6 cun lateral to the midline
Cough, asthma, chest pain, shoulder pain
Oblique 0.5–0.8 cun laterally — avoid deep/medial insertion
Regulates Lung Qi; cools Blood; stops epistaxis
Medial upper arm, 3 cun below the anterior axillary fold, radial to biceps
Asthma, epistaxis, pain in the medial arm, goiter
Regulates Qi in the chest; calms rebellious Qi (retching)
Cough, chest pain, dry retching, arm pain
Clears Lung heat; descends rebellious Qi; stops cough (He-sea/Water point)
Cubital crease, radial side of the biceps brachii tendon
Cough, asthma, hemoptysis, sore throat, elbow/arm pain; acute vomiting/diarrhea (heatstroke, cholera-type patterns)
Perpendicular 0.5–1 cun, or prick to bleed
Stops bleeding; clears heat; benefits the throat (Xi-cleft — acute hemoptysis)
7 cun above LU9, on the line joining LU5 and LU9
Hemoptysis (key point), cough, asthma, sore throat, hemorrhoids, elbow/arm pain
Luo-connecting point; Command point of head & neck; Confluent point of Ren Mai
Releases the exterior; disperses Wind; regulates Lung Qi; opens Ren Mai; benefits the head/neck
Above the radial styloid process, 1.5 cun proximal to the wrist crease
Headache, neck stiffness, cough, asthma, sore throat, facial paralysis, wrist pain
Oblique 0.3–0.5 cun, directed proximally/distally along the channel
Descends Lung Qi; clears heat; benefits the throat
1 cun above the wrist crease, in the depression radial to the radial artery
Cough, asthma, chest pain, sore throat, wrist pain
Perpendicular 0.2–0.3 cun — avoid the radial artery
Shu-stream (Earth) point; Yuan-source point; Influential point of the Vessels
Tonifies Lung Qi; strengthens the pulse/vessels (Hui of Vessels); nourishes Lung Yin
At the radial end of the wrist crease, in the depression proximal to the radial styloid, at the radial artery
Cough, asthma, hemoptysis, chest pain, wrist pain; disorders of the pulse/vessels
Perpendicular 0.2–0.3 cun — avoid the radial artery
Clears Lung heat and Fire; benefits the throat; stops cough
Radial side of the midpoint of the 1st metacarpal, at the junction of the red and white skin
Sore throat, cough, hemoptysis, fever, loss of voice, infantile fright wind
Clears heat; benefits the throat; restores consciousness; stops bleeding
Radial side of the thumb, 0.1 cun proximal to the corner of the nail
Sore throat (key bleeding point for acute sore throat), fever, loss of consciousness, epistaxis, cough
Shallow 0.1 cun, or prick to bleed

acu-points.title: Large Intestine Channel (LI) — Shou Yang Ming
acu-points.source: Compiled from standard TCM point location/indication data

blocks.heading: Channel pathway
blocks.lead: Starts at the radial side of the index finger tip (LI1), runs along the dorsal-radial side of the index finger, through the space between the 1st and 2nd metacarpal bones (LI4), up the lateral forearm to the elbow (LI11), continues up the lateral upper arm to the shoulder (LI15), crosses over the top of the shoulder, runs along the neck (LI17, LI18), crosses the cheek, enters the lower gum, curves around the upper lip to the opposite side, and ends beside the opposite nostril (LI20), connecting to the Stomach channel.

items.t: Hand Yang Ming, paired (exterior-interior) with the Lung channel
Second channel in the 12-channel Qi circulation, following the Lung channel
Governs conduction/transmission (傳導); associated with fluids, and commonly used for facial/head disorders ("face and mouth belong to LI4")

blocks.heading: Points of the Large Intestine channel (LI1–LI20)
blocks.lead: ★ = high-yield / most frequently used and tested points on this channel.

rows: Clears heat; benefits the throat; restores consciousness
Radial side of the index finger, 0.1 cun proximal to the corner of the nail
Sore throat, toothache, numbness of fingers, fever, loss of consciousness
Shallow 0.1 cun, or prick to bleed
Clears heat; benefits the throat; stops epistaxis
Radial side of index finger, distal to the 2nd metacarpophalangeal joint, at the junction of red/white skin
Epistaxis, sore throat, toothache, facial paralysis
Clears heat; benefits the throat; reduces swelling
Radial side of index finger, proximal to the head of the 2nd metacarpal bone

acu-points.title: Stomach Channel (ST) — Foot Yang Ming
acu-points.source: Compiled from standard TCM point location/indication data

blocks.heading: Channel pathway
blocks.lead: Begins beside the nose (junction with LI), ascends to the inner canthus (meets BL), descends along the side of the nose, enters the upper gum, curves around the lips, descends to the jaw and ascends in front of the ear to the forehead corner (ST8). A branch runs from ST5 down the neck (ST9) into the supraclavicular fossa (ST12), through the diaphragm, entering the Stomach and connecting with the Spleen. The main external branch continues from ST12 down the chest (4 cun lateral to the midline) and abdomen (2 cun lateral) to the groin (ST30), then down the anterior-lateral thigh and leg, crossing the top of the foot, and ending at the lateral side of the 2nd toe (ST45). A branch leaves ST36 to the middle toe, and another leaves ST42 to the medial side of the big toe, connecting to the Spleen channel.

items.t: Foot Yang Ming, paired (exterior-interior) with the Spleen channel
The largest channel — 45 points, more than any other primary channel
Governs "reception" (受納) and "ripening/rotting" (腐熟) of food; Yang Ming is described as having the most abundant Qi and Blood of all channels
