items.detail: 1) Excess heat (實熱): great thirst with preference for cold water + red face, fever, restlessness, agitation, profuse sweating, large-surging pulse — interior excess heat severely damages body fluids, drinking is self-treatment. 2) Xiao Ke (wasting and thirst, 消渴): great thirst with drinking a lot + hunger with eating a lot, profuse urination, emaciation — LU heat (thirst) + ST heat (eating) + KI Yin deficiency (urination). 3) Temporary damage of body fluids: usually after profuse sweating, severe vomiting, or diarrhea

items.t: Thirst but little desire to drink (渴不多飮)
items.detail: Dry mouth or sensation of thirst + no desire to drink or drink just a little. Light body fluids damage, or problems with distribution of body fluids: Yin deficiency, damp-heat, phlegm-fluids, blood stasis

Dry mouth with little desire to drink + tidal fever, night sweating, red cheeks. Insufficient body fluids unable to nourish the mouth → dry mouth + no desire to drink (no excessive heat damaging body fluids)

Thirst with desire to drink a little + heaviness (head, body), low degree fever, stuffy epigastric sensation, greasy coating. Damp-heat hinders transformation of body fluids → fluids unable to nourish the mouth, but not severe thirst because of internal damp

Thirst with preference for warm water but just a little, sometimes vomiting upon drinking + dizziness, borborygmus. Phlegm-fluids (Yin pathogen) damage Yang → unable to transform body fluids and nourish the mouth → thirst but no desire to drink because of internal damp

Dry mouth, only rinses the mouth with water without swallowing + uneven pulse, stasis spots on the tongue. Blood stasis blockage impairs Qi transformation of body fluids → dry mouth + no damage to body fluids (no drinking)

items.t: "[When] Yang declines and Yin becomes exuberant, the eyes close. [When] Yin declines and Yang becomes exuberant, [one] wakes up."
items.cn: 陽氣盡, 陰氣盛, 則目瞑; 陰氣盡而陽氣盛, 則寤矣
items.detail: <Ling Shu: Chapter 28 (靈樞: 口問)>

items.t: Under normal condition, when Wei Qi is at the Yang channels, Yang is replete and one is awake; when Wei Qi moves into Yin channels at night, Yin becomes replete and one sleeps
When Yin and Yang lose harmony — Yang not entering Yin at night, or Yang unable to come out from Yin during the day — sleep problems (insomnia or lethargy) may occur

blocks.heading: Insomnia (失眠, 不寐)
blocks.lead: Relative imbalance between Yin and Yang (replete Yang, deficient Yin) → Yang unable to enter Yin → no place for spirit to rest → insomnia

items.t: 1) HT and KI disharmony (心腎不交)
items.detail: Difficulty falling asleep + agitation, dream a lot, tidal fever, night sweating, weak/sore lower back and knees. KI Yin deficiency or HT fire → KI water and HT fire unable to control each other → restless spirit

items.t: 2) HT and SP deficiency (心脾兩虛)
items.detail: Waking up often after sleep + palpitation, poor appetite, weakness, pale tongue. SP deficiency → unable to transform/transport food essence → HT blood deficiency → unable to nourish the spirit

items.t: 3) GB stagnation and phlegm harassing (膽鬱痰擾)
items.detail: Insomnia + easily frightened, dizziness, stuffy chest, agitation, bitter taste, nausea. Emotional stress → Qi stagnation → turns into fire and produces phlegm → phlegm-fire disturbing the spirit

Stuffy epigastric sensation, belching, abdominal distention, thick-greasy tongue coating. Improper diet (overeating) damages SP/ST → ST Qi unable to properly descend + turbid Qi rising → disturbing the spirit

blocks.heading: Lethargy (嗜睡, 多眠)
blocks.lead: Tiredness, likes to sleep

items.detail: Tired and falls asleep easily + dizziness, heavy sensation, greasy tongue coating. External summer-damp invasion or internal build-up of phlegm-damp disturbs SP → clear Yang unable to ascend → unable to nourish the head
Tired and sleepy after meals + weakness, poor appetite, pale tongue. SP/ST Qi deficiency → weakened transforming/transporting → clear Yang unable to ascend → unable to nourish the head

items.t: 3) HT and KI Yang deficiency (心腎陽衰)
items.detail: Tired and sleepy, cold limbs, weak pulse. HT/KI Yang deficiency + exuberant Yin cold → declined normal bodily function

items.t: 4) Febrile disease entering the Ying blood level (溫病熱入心包)
items.detail: Unclear mind and sleepy, delirious speech, fever, skin lesions, deep-red tongue, rapid pulse. Warm febrile disease invasion penetrating into PC → exuberant heat misting the spirit

items.t: Lower 2 orifices are the body's gates where cold-heat and excess-deficiency may be observed
Observe frequency, amount, color, smell, and other qualities

items.detail: Intestines (LI, SI) — actual pathway; SP/ST — rotting, transforming, ascending, descending; LV — smooth flow of Qi; KI (Mingmen, 命門) — providing heat. [Note] LU — interior-exterior relationship with LI
Regular frequency: 1/day or every other day + emptied sensation. Moist: neither dry nor loose, forms. No blood, sticky discharge, or undigested food
Deficiency → condition aggravates after bowel movement. Excess → condition alleviates after bowel movement. Heat → strong foul smell. Cold → no smell or fish smell

items.t: Prolonged intervals between bowel movements, or inability to push out stools even with urge

items.detail: Fever, abdominal distention and pain, red tongue with yellow-dry coating. Heat damages body fluids → dry intestines and stools
Pale-white complexion, preference for heat, deep-slow pulse. Excess cold blocks the intestines' movement
Dry stools, red tongue with scanty coating, thin-rapid pulse. Insufficient Yin fluids unable to nourish the intestines
Old age, post-partum — weakness to push out + dryness. Not enough Qi to push out stools + body fluids/blood unable to nourish the intestines

blocks.heading: Diarrhea (泄瀉)
blocks.lead: Loose, unformed, or watery stools with increased frequency

items.detail: Poor appetite, dull large-abdominal pain, loose stools. SP deficiency → unable to properly transform/transport → improper separating of clear and turbid (by SP & SI) → excessive fluids retained in the intestine

Chronic early morning (5am) abdominal pain followed by diarrhea (pain relieved after), sore lower back and knees. Declining Ming Men fire (命門火) unable to warm SP (becoming cold) → SP unable to transform/transport + struggle between Yin and Yang (early morning = Yang beginning to rise)
Emotional stress triggering abdominal pain and diarrhea, pain reduced/relieved after diarrhea
Stuffy epigastric sensation, belching with foul smell, abdominal pain relieved by diarrhea (eliminating turbid Qi). Improper diet (overeating, wrong food) damages ST and intestines
Diarrhea with mucus
Undigested food, incontinence of diarrhea

items.t: Others (quality of stools, sensation during bowel movement)
items.detail: Sometimes dry, sometimes loose stools → LV and SP disharmony (LV Qi stagnation over-controlling SP): abdominal distention, alternating diarrhea and constipation, difficulty having bowel movement although stools not dry. Dry stools first, loose afterward → SP deficiency. Unfinished sensation → LV Qi stagnation over-controlling SP (stagnation of intestinal Qi). Undigested food in stools → SP and/or KI deficiency
Bleeding before stools (near-site) + bright color → heat. Bleeding after stools (distal-site) + dark color (dark chocolate) → SP Qi unable to hold blood

items.t: Burning sensation at the anus during bowel movement + foul smell → damp-heat
Urgency of bowel movement and heavy sensation afterwards → damp-heat
Prolapsed rectum → SP Qi deficiency and sinking

items.detail: BL, SJ — pathway. KI — Qi transformation + distribution of body fluids. LU, SP — distribution of body fluids
3–5 times during the day and 0–1 during the night. Good flow + emptied sensation. No abnormal sensation (pain, burning, etc.). No blood, protein, etc.
Cold → pale-clear color + profuse urination. Heat → dark color + scanty urination
Profuse + pale (clear) color, aversion to cold → empty-cold. Profuse, thirst, hungry, emaciation → Xiao Ke (消渴) [LU, ST, KI]
Scanty + dark color → excess heat (damaging body fluids). Scanty + swelling of the body → edema (LU/SP/KI malfunction)
Frequent, clear urine, urinary incontinence, night urination (enuresis), large amount or dribbling, usually in the elderly → KI deficiency (KI unable to control the lower orifice)
Unable to hold + clear mind → unsecure KI Qi + BL unable to control urine. Enuresis → KI Qi deficiency + weak BL
Frequent urination, scanty-dark urine, urinary urgency, painful and/or burning urination. Damp-heat accumulation in the lower Jiao → inhibited Qi transformation function
Difficult (Long, 癃) or blocked (Bi, 閉) urination. Damp-heat, blood stasis, stone, KI Yang deficiency, Qi deficiency

blocks.heading: Women (婦女) — Menses (月經)
blocks.lead: Normal cycle: onset 14 (13) years → menopause 49 (48) years; cycle ~28 days; duration 5–7 days; proper red color without clots

items.detail: Menses coming about 1 week or more earlier than usual. Sticky dark-red menstrual blood + large amount → blood-heat (heat causing reckless movement of blood). Watery pale-red menstrual blood + large amount → Qi deficiency (Qi unable to hold blood)
Menses coming about 1 week or more later than usual. Watery pale-red menstrual blood, scanty amount → blood deficiency. Dark menstrual blood with clots, scanty amount → cold (blood stasis)

Menses coming about 1 week or more early or late than usual. Relatively dark-red blood with clots, scanty amount, distended/painful breast → Qi stagnation. Watery pale-red blood with clots, scanty (KI) or large (SP) amount → SP/KI deficiency

Lower abdominal pain before, during, or after menses. Severe pain before onset, relieved afterward → excess (Qi stagnation, blood stasis) — pain due to blockage (不通則痛). Dull pain after menses, sore lower back → deficiency (Qi and blood, KI) — dull pain due to improper nourishment. Cold pain during menses, alleviated by warmth → cold (cold blocking the channels → contraction of uterus and collaterals → acute pain)
No menses for 3+ consecutive months (except menopause, pregnancy, or breast feeding) → blood stasis, LV Qi stagnation, Xu Lao (虛勞)

items.t: 6) Beng Lou (abnormal bleeding: metrorrhagia or menorrhagia, 崩漏)
items.detail: Heat → dark blood with clots. Deficiency → pale-red blood without clots

Normal: scanty, clear (transparent), no smell. White, large amount, watery, no smell → white leukorrhea (白帶), cold-damp. Yellowish, large amount, sticky, foul smell → yellow leukorrhea (黃帶), damp-heat. Blood-tainted or mixed red-white, sticky, some smell → LV fire (LV Qi stagnation turning into fire). Red-profuse leukorrhea after menopause → cancer
Infertility → deficiency (blood, KI), excess (blood stasis, damp-heat). Miscarriage → deficiency (blood, KI, SP), excess (blood stasis). Vomiting (妊娠惡阻) → ST, LV

items.t: Postpartum (産後) — Lochia (bloody discharge, 産後惡露)
items.detail: Qi deficiency + sinking → large amount, pale color, fatigue, pale complexion. Blood heat → dark-red sticky, thirst, red complexion, constipation. Blood stasis → purplish-dark with clots, low abdominal pain that dislikes pressure

External wind invasion → fever and chills, body ache, headache. Exuberant fire evil → high fever, restlessness, thirst, dry mouth, constipation, dark urine. Blood deficiency + heat → low degree fever, dull abdominal pain, dizziness, pale complexion, dry stools
Usually due to blood deficiency

blocks.heading: Children (小兒)
blocks.lead: Same general questions apply, with additional age-specific considerations

items.t: 1) Newborn / neonate (up to 28 days after delivery, 新生兒)
items.detail: Pregnancy: general health or diet of the mother, emotional stress or physical trauma, alcohol/smoking/drugs during pregnancy, ... Delivery: premature birth (<37 weeks), difficult labor, Cesarean, ...

items.t: 2) Infant (1 month–1/2 years) and toddler (1/2–3 years)
items.detail: Breast feeding and weaning (accustoming to food other than mother's milk). Weaning too early → retention of food, skin diseases
Digestive (poor appetite, diarrhea, constipation), respiratory (whooping cough, high fever), and/or sleep problems. Vaccinations

blocks.heading: Palpating Diagnostics (切診) — Pulse Diagnosis (脈診)

items.t: Palpation of the body (按診, 觸診)
items.detail: skin, chest, abdomen, limbs, acupuncture points, etc.
<Pulse Classic (Mai Jing, 脈經)>, WANG Shu He (王叔和, 210–258 CE). <Lakeside Pulse Study (Bin Hu Mai Xue, 瀕湖脈學)>, LI Shi Zhen (李時珍, 1518–1593)

blocks.heading: Why do we do pulse diagnosis?
blocks.lead: Clinical significances of pulse — changes in pulse show:

items.t: Depth of disease → interior vs. exterior
items.detail: [Ex] floating or deep pulse

items.t: Nature of evil Qi → heat vs. cold
items.detail: [Ex] rapid or slow pulse

items.t: State of right and evil Qi → excess vs. deficiency
items.detail: [Ex] weak or forceful pulse

items.t: Progress of a condition → recovering or worsening
items.detail: Chronic condition + pulse becoming moderate → recovery of ST Qi. Chronic deficiency condition + pulse becoming forceful-flooding → worsening, critical

blocks.heading: Relationship between Zangfu organs and the pulse (脈象形成原理)

items.t: HT governs blood and vessels (心主血脈)
items.detail: Circulation of blood within the blood vessels

items.t: LU controls channels and vessels (肺朝百脈)
items.detail: Distribution of blood to the entire body

items.t: SP governs holding of blood (脾主統血)
items.detail: Post-heaven (source of blood) + containing blood within the vessels
Smooth flow and volume control of blood
Pre-heaven (source of blood) + transforming Qi into blood

blocks.heading: Locations where the pulse is taken

items.detail: Not commonly used by modern practitioners; sometimes used in emergency or when pulse is not detected from the radial artery. Includes the modern radial artery pulse (Cunkou Mai, 寸口脈)

blocks.heading: Whole body approach — 3 places and 9 states (三部九候脈法)
blocks.lead: Source: <Su Wen: Chapter 20 (素問: 三部九候論)>. Taking pulses from 3 places of the body (upper, middle, lower) x 3 levels each (heaven, human, earth) = 9 states

blocks.heading: Whole body approach — 3 places (三部脈法)
blocks.lead: Source: <Shang Han Lun (傷寒論)>. Taking pulse from carotid artery, instep, radial artery + foot Shaoyin pulse

blocks.heading: Radial artery pulse approach (寸口脈法)

items.t: Most commonly used in modern pulse diagnosis

items.detail: <Nei Jing (內經)>, <Nan Jing (難經)>, <Mai Jing (脈經)>

items.t: [Note] Also called Cunkou pulse (寸口脈) or radial pulse

items.detail: Middle (Guan, 關) — ulna side of the styloid process of the radius. Distal (Cun, 寸) — between the Guan position and the wrist. Proximal (Chi, 尺) — immediately proximal to the Guan position

blocks.heading: 3 positions (Cun/Guan/Chi) and corresponding Zangfu organs — simplified
blocks.lead: Different classical sources (Nan Jing, Mai Jing, Jing Yue Quan Shu, Golden Mirror of Medical Tradition, Bin Hu Mai Xue) give slightly different correspondences. Commonly used simplified version:

items.t: Distal (Cun) → upper part of the body; Middle (Guan) → middle part of the body; Proximal (Chi) → lower part of the body

rows: Jing Yue Quan Shu (景岳全書)
KI 腎/BL 膀胱, LI 大腸
KI 腎/SJ 三焦, Mingmen 命門, SI 小腸
Golden Mirror of Medical Tradition (醫宗金鑑)
KI 腎/BL 膀胱, SI 小腸
Bin Hu Mai Xue (瀕湖脈學)

blocks.heading: 3 levels (depths, 浮中沉) of pulse and corresponding Zangfu organs
blocks.lead: Different pressure applied by the fingers = different levels; applies regardless of left or right

rows: State of Qi / Yang organs; Upper Jiao; Exterior conditions
State of blood; Middle Jiao; SP/ST disorders
State of Yin; Lower Jiao; Interior conditions

items.detail: Ideally, early in the morning when Yin is calm and Yang has not yet come forth

Patient's arm: parallel (not held higher than HT level) + relaxed. Practitioner: sitting on the side of the patient. Use the left (or right) hand of the practitioner to palpate the right (or left) pulse of the patient. First place the middle finger on Guan (middle), then the index finger on Cun (distal), then the ring finger on Chi (proximal). Use the finger-print areas of the fingers to palpate — natural arching of fingers. [Note] Children — use only the practitioner's thumb (no need to divide 3 positions)
One breathing (exhaling and inhaling) is the basis for determining slow or fast pulse. Focus on maintaining even and calm breathing
Palpate the pulse for a minimum of 50 beats; 100 (50x2), 150 (50x3) beats, etc. if more time is required

items.t: 5) 3 levels/depth (浮中沉: superficial, middle, and deep)
items.detail: Superficial (Fu, 浮): resting the fingers on the radial artery very gently. Middle (Zhong, 中): between superficial and deep. Deep (Chen, 沉): pressing hard to the bottom

blocks.heading: Normal pulse (平脈)
blocks.lead: Should have 3 qualities: ST Qi, spirit, and root — i.e. Qi, mind, and essence

items.detail: 4 to 5 beats per breath = 72–80 beats/min

ST is the root of post-heaven, source of Qi and blood — presence or absence of ST Qi determines life or death. "Evil Qi coming is tight and rapid. Food Qi coming is moderate and harmonious" (邪氣來也緊而疾, 穀氣來也徐而和) <Ling Shu: Chapter 9 (靈樞: 終始)>. Moderate and harmonious pulse indicates the presence of ST Qi → health, good prognosis or favorable development even under illness

HT governs blood and vessels and stores the spirit — when Qi and blood are replete, the spirit becomes strong and the pulse has strength. Soft (but with strength) pulse indicates spirit — neither weak nor tight
KI is the root of the pre-heaven, source of the primordial force of the body. The pulse with root can be felt clearly at the deep level and proximal (Chi) position

items.t: 5) Natural changes to the normal pulse
items.detail: Seasons: more floating than normal during summer, deeper than normal during winter. Gender: men's pulse relatively stronger than women's; relatively stronger left-side (Yang) pulse for men and right-side (Yin) pulse for women; slippery-rapid pulse for pregnancy. Age: faster pulse for small children; stronger pulse for young people; weaker pulse for elderly. Body build: more floating pulse for thin people, deeper pulse for obese people. May also change due to short-term influences (running, emotional upset, little sleep with a long day's work, etc.)

blocks.heading: 28 Pulses (二十八脈) — cross-reference of English translations
When palpating the pulse, try to:

items.t: Feel the pulse as a whole
Look for the ST Qi, spirit, and root
Feel the strength of the pulse
Feel the different levels (3-level: superficial, middle, deep)
Feel the different positions (3-position: proximal, middle, distal)
Feel the quality of the pulse

blocks.heading: Individual pulses (1) — Floating, Deep, Slow, Rapid

items.detail: Felt with light pressure, just resting fingers on the artery. Exterior pattern (表證): floating and slightly reduced going into deeper level — external evil invasion + right Qi moving to the surface to fight. Deficiency pattern (虛證): floating-large-forceless and empty at the deep level — deficient Qi floating outwards

Felt only with heavy pressure. Interior excess pattern (裏實證): deep + strong — stagnant evil Qi in the interior + right Qi struggling against it. Interior deficiency pattern (裏虛證): deep + weak — weak right Qi due to deficient Zangfu organs, unable to reach outwards

Less than 4 beats per breath (usually <60/min). Excess cold pattern (寒實證, 寒積證): slow-strong — cold evil making right Qi stagnant, difficulty moving. Empty cold pattern (虛寒證): slow-weak — Yang deficiency, weak right Qi. [Note] Excess heat accumulation (邪熱結聚): slow-strong — stagnation of evil heat causing sluggish movement of Qi and blood; Yangming pattern (陽明病) of purging type

More than 5 beats per breath (usually >90/min). Excess heat pattern (實熱證): rapid-strong — exuberant heat evil accelerating movement of Qi and blood. Empty heat pattern (虛熱證): rapid-weak — Yin deficiency, empty heat. [Note] Yang deficiency floating outwards (陽虛外浮): rapid-floating-large-weak + empty at deep level — deficient Yang separating from Yin, floating alone outward/upward

blocks.heading: Individual pulses (2) — Full, Large, Thready, Feeble, Scatter

items.detail: Floating-large-rapid (coming forcefully but going away weak). Extreme heat (熱盛) → congested vessels with Qi and blood. Excess → heat in Qi level. Deficiency → exuberant evil Qi + weak right Qi
Floating-large-empty, similar to Hong Mai but weak & empty (only felt at the edges). Deficiency, strong evil Qi with worsening condition

Thin like a fine line, but felt clearly. Deficiency (blood, Qi), damp, Xu Lao (虛勞). Blood and/or Qi deficiency → unable to fill the vessels. Damp → blocks and presses the vessels. [Note] aka. Xiao Mai (small, 小脈)

Extremely thin (thinner than Xi Mai) and soft, difficult to feel, (deep) — too weak to move Qi and blood. Severe deficiency (Yang, Qi, Yin, blood). In chronic condition → extreme deficiency of right Qi. In acute condition → abrupt exhaustion of Yang Qi
Floating-scattered-large without root. Separating original Qi (critical condition). Floating-dispersed on light pressure, but no pulse on middle to deep pressure

blocks.heading: Individual pulses (3) — Long, Short, Slippery, Choppy, Moderate

items.detail: Pulse extending beyond the normal pulse position. Heat (excess Yang), excessive LV Yang. [Note] Healthy if long-moderate — sufficient Qi and blood

Pulse occupying a shorter space than the normal pulse position; opposite of Chang Mai. Stagnation, deficiency. Short-forceless → Qi deficiency. Short-forceful → Qi (and blood) stagnation, (phlegm stagnation, food accumulation)

Smooth-round, as if beads or pearls are rolling through. Phlegm-fluids, food stagnation, excess heat, pregnancy. [Note] Pregnant women with slippery-rapid pulse → sufficient Qi and blood, healthy. [Note] Slippery-moderate-harmonious → healthy person with sufficient Qi and blood

Rough-uneven without smooth quality; opposite of Hua Mai (changes in speed and quality: thin, slow, short, scattered). Deficiency (blood, body fluids, essence), Qi and blood stagnation. Choppy-forceless → deficiency, unable to fill the vessels. Choppy-forceful → Qi and blood stagnation, blockage causing unsmooth flow
4 beats per breath, coming and going moderately. Health, weak SP/ST, damp. Weak SP/ST + damp → moderate and lazy feeling. Health → moderate and harmonious

blocks.heading: Individual pulses (4) — Tight, Wiry, Deficient, Excessive

items.detail: Tight and twisted, rope-like pulse. Excess cold, pain, retention of food. Cold evil blocking flow of Yang Qi → struggle between cold evil and right Qi → tight. Floating-tight → wind-cold. Deep-tight → interior cold

Tight like a guitar string — tighter and harder (thinner) than Jin Mai, not obvious on light pressure but tighter on middle/deep pressure. LV/GB problems, pain, phlegm-fluids, (malaria). Evil Qi affecting smooth flow of LV Qi → tensed Qi, pain, phlegm accumulation → wiry. Wiry-thin → LV overacting on SP/ST deficiency
Soft-large (slow) + weak on pressure. Deficiency (Qi and blood, Zangfu organs)
Large-long-forceful on all positions and levels. Excess

blocks.heading: Individual pulses (5) — Hollow, Drum-skin, Firm, Weak, Soggy, Hidden

items.detail: Floating-soft + empty in the middle (like a scallion stalk). Loss of blood (hemorrhage), damaged Yin. Sudden loss of blood or severe body fluids damage → unable to fill up the vessels + Yang with no place to attach, scattered outwards
Floating-tight (like a stretched drum) at superficial level + empty on pressure. Loss of blood or essence, abortion (半産), vaginal bleeding (漏下)
Felt only at deep level + forceful-wiry. Interior cold, Shan Qi (hernia, 疝氣), abdominal masses (癥瘕)
Only felt at deep level + soft-thin. Deficiency of Qi and blood
Floating-thin-weak-soft + disappearing if pressured. Deficiency (blood, Yin), damp
Only felt at bone level (extremely deep). Hidden evil in the deep-level Qi (邪閉), reversal syndrome (厥證), extreme pain (痛極). Hidden evil Qi → Qi cannot flow

blocks.heading: Individual pulses (6) — Moving, Skipping, Knotted, Intermittent, Racing

items.detail: Round (like a bean) + trembling + slippery-rapid-forceful. Pain, shock, fright. Usually seen in people severely traumatized by emotional shock or in severe pain

Rapid + missing beat (stopping) at irregular interval. Excess heat. Hasty-forceful → heat from Qi and blood stagnation, phlegm-fluids stagnation, or retention of food, (abscess). Hasty-forceless → critical
Slow + missing beat (stopping) at irregular interval. Cold, cold-phlegm, blood stasis, abdominal masses, (HT deficiency)
Missing beat (stopping) at regular interval. Decline of Zang organs' Qi, wind, pain, fright, traumatic injuries. [Note] Stopping every 4 beats or less → serious
Extremely rapid-urgent (7–8 beats per breath). Extreme Yang with exhausted Yin. [Note] Small children may have this rapid pulse without any problems

items.t: Rapid speed → rapid, hasty, hurried, moving
Empty quality → empty, weak, fine, minute, scattered, short
Full quality → full, overflowing, wiry, tight, long

items.detail: Relatively surging pulse on the left middle and distal positions + no other symptoms (bitter taste, fever, abdominal distention, etc.)
General amenorrhea pulse → choppy at the Chi position. Blood deficiency if empty-thin-choppy pulse at Chi. Excess if wiry-choppy pulse at Chi
General pregnancy pulse → slippery-rapid with force and harmony
Surging-choppy pulse → danger of losing the baby or already dead fetus

items.t: Use the thumb (at the middle position) to palpate the 3 levels
Age and normal beats per breath
items.detail: 0–3 years old: 7–8 beats/breath. 5–6 years old: 6 beats/breath (7/breath = rapid; ≤5/breath = slow)

items.t: Not fully developed KI Qi → normal to see no pulse at deep level

blocks.heading: Combined pulses and patterns
blocks.lead: Common to see a combination of different pulses — seldom to see only one pulse quality

items.t: Floating-tight (浮緊) → exterior wind-cold, wind-cold Bi
Floating-moderate (浮緩) → Taiyang wind-strike (exterior wind-cold deficiency)
Floating-slippery (浮滑) → exterior + phlegm (person with phlegm having exterior syndrome), wind-phlegm
Deep-wiry (沉弦) → LV Qi stagnation, water-fluids retention
Deep-choppy (沉澀) → blood stasis (Yang deficiency, cold)
Deep-moderate (沉緩) → SP deficiency + water-damp retention
Wiry-rapid (弦數) → LV Qi stagnation turning into fire, LV/GB damp-heat
Wiry-thin (弦細) → LV & KI Yin deficiency, blood deficiency + LV Qi stagnation, LV Qi stagnation + SP deficiency
Slippery-rapid (滑數) → phlegm-fire, food stagnation + heat
Wiry-slippery-rapid (弦滑數) → LV fire + phlegm, LV Yang rising, retention of phlegm-fire
Surging-rapid (洪數) → excess heat at Qi level (external evil Qi attack)

blocks.heading: Pulse and correspondence of conditions
blocks.lead: Condition + expected pulse(s) = corresponding (順); condition + non-corresponding pulse(s) = against (逆)

items.detail: Corresponding (順): surging-rapid-excess (洪數實) — excess evil Qi + strong right Qi struggling. Against (逆): thin-weak (細弱) — excess evil Qi + weak right Qi, danger of evil Qi falling into the interior relatively easily
Corresponding (順): floating (浮), surging (洪), rapid (數), excess (實) — strong right Qi fighting evil Qi. Against (逆): deep (沉), minute (微), thin (細), weak (弱) — already declined right Qi
Corresponding (順): deep (沉), minute (微), thin (細), weak (弱) — weak evil Qi + recovering right Qi. Against (逆): floating (浮), surging (洪), rapid (數), excess (實) — weak right Qi + strong evil Qi

Not-corresponding pulse and condition = false pulse or condition + right pulse or condition. Right condition, false pulse — e.g. congested interior heat → reversal cold of the limbs + slippery-rapid pulse. Right pulse, false condition — e.g. excess heat in ST/intestines → distended painful abdomen dislikes pressure, dry constipated stools, red tongue with thick-yellow-dry coating + deep-slow pulse. Use all 4 diagnostics to make proper pattern differentiation

blocks.heading: Palpation of the Body (按診, 觸診)

items.detail: Touching (觸): using fingertips or palms, lightly touch local areas to check hot, cold, dryness, moist, etc. Seeking (摸): on the area of local swelling or distension, look for shape and size. Pressing (按): press local areas (usually chest or abdomen) to check pain on pressure, masses (size, quality), swelling, distension, etc.

blocks.heading: Palpating the skin
blocks.lead: In general, hot if Yang Qi is excessive and cold if Yang Qi is declined

items.detail: Very hot at first touch but becoming less hot after a while → heat at the exterior. Becoming hotter with time → heat at the interior. Painful on light pressure → shallow level (exterior). Painful on relatively heavy pressure → deep level (interior)
Soft skin + prefers pressure → deficiency. Affected site hard and dislikes pressure → excess
Dry skin → tendency of no sweating. Dry and thinning → insufficiency of body fluids. Moist-wet → tendency to sweat easily. Dry and scaling → damaged Yin, dry blood
Dips on pressure but rises back up quickly after removing pressure → Qi swelling (氣腫). Dips on pressure & unable to rise up quickly → water swelling (水腫)

Swollen + hard + not hot → cold. Swollen + hot on touch + painful on pressure → heat. Depressed under the skin line → deficiency. Erupted above the skin line → excess. Hard and tight → no abscess yet. Hard surroundings and soft tip (center, top) → abscess formed
Cold limbs → cold (Yang deficiency, excess cold). Hot limbs → heat (excess heat, Yin deficiency)

Stagnant heat in the interior, Qi stagnation (excess, heat) → reversal cold of the limbs. Relatively hotter dorsal sides of hands/feet → heat from exterior invasion. Relatively hotter palm/sole sides → interior heat. Relatively hotter forehead than palms → exterior heat. Relatively hotter palms than forehead → interior heat
Cold finger tips → fright reversal (hands cold due to fear). Only middle finger hot → wind-cold. Only middle finger tip cold → pox (chicken pox, smallpox) to occur

items.t: 4) Prognosis based on cold or warm limbs
items.detail: Yang deficiency with chills and diarrhea + warm limbs → treatable. Yang deficiency with chills and diarrhea + cold limbs → difficult to treat
Normal: not tight or hasty, but moderate. Minute and indistinct → deficiency of Zong Qi (gathering/chest Qi, 宗氣). Surging-large-bouncing → emergency

items.t: 2) Palpating the chest and hypochondriac area
items.detail: Distended chest and panting on pressure → LU distention (肺脹). Distending pain on pressing the chest/hypochondriac area → phlegm-heat stagnation, water-fluids retention. Enlarged-swollen LV + soft or hard → Qi stagnation, blood stasis

items.t: 3) Palpating the abdomen — cold and hot
items.detail: Cold abdominal skin + likes pressure → empty-cold. Hot abdominal skin + dislikes pressure → excess heat
Abdominal pain + likes pressure → deficiency. Abdominal pain + dislikes pressure → excess. Local burning sensation + intolerable pain → abscess
Abdominal distension + hard & painful on pressure → excess type. Abdominal distension + not hard & no pain on pressure → deficiency type

items.t: Piman (stuffy and fullness sensation at the epigastric area, 痞滿)
items.detail: Soft and no pain on pressure → deficiency. Relatively hard and painful on pressure → excess. Distended + pain on pressure + water sound → water-fluids retention in ST
Stuffy and distended epigastric area + pain on pressure → small Jiexiong (小結胸). Hard and full epigastric area + pain (dislikes pressure) → large Jiexiong (大結胸)

items.t: Masses (腫塊) — accumulation and gathering (Ji Ju, 積聚)
items.detail: Pain + fixed location + shape (boundaries) → accumulation (積), blood level (blood stasis). Pain + no fixed location + without shape → gathering (聚), Qi level (Qi stagnation). Left lesser abdominal pain + hard masses → stagnated stools. Right lesser abdominal pain + pain on pressure → intestinal abscess (腸癰). Accumulation of parasites → feels like sinews, moving, erupted or depressed skin

items.t: Look for knots or other congested sensation, pain, or sensitive reaction

items.detail: LU disorders → knots on BL13, painful LU1. ST disorders → BL21 and/or ST36 pain. Intestinal abscess (腸癰) → painful ST37 (闌尾穴)

acu-selection.title: Point Selection Methods (配穴法)
acu-selection.source: Compiled from standard TCM point-selection theory

blocks.heading: Overview — two stages of choosing points
blocks.lead: Prescribing an acupuncture treatment happens in two steps: (1) choosing which points to use, based on their location and/or their known actions, and (2) combining those points together (配穴, "pei xue") according to established pairing strategies that reinforce or balance their effect.

items.detail: Choosing points by location (local, adjacent, or distal) and/or by pattern (which points treat the diagnosed pattern)
Combining the chosen points using a recognized pairing strategy (upper-lower, front-back, left-right, host-guest, same-name channel, etc.) to strengthen the therapeutic effect

rows:
Local (near) point selection
近部取穴
Choosing a point directly at or very near the site of disease — "where it hurts, needle it" (this includes Ashi points)
Knee pain → ST35 (Dubi), local knee points; facial paralysis → ST4, ST6 (local face points)

Adjacent point selection
鄰近取穴
Choosing a point near, but not exactly at, the diseased area — on the same or a nearby channel/region
Eye disorders → GB1, TE23, BL2 (points around the eye, not on it); nasal disorders → LI20 (beside the nose)

Distal point selection
遠部取穴
Choosing a point far from the diseased area, usually below the elbow/knee, based on channel pathway or point function
Facial/mouth disorders → LI4 (Hegu), even though it's on the hand — "the face and mouth belong to LI4" (Four Total Points 四總穴)

blocks.heading: Selection by pattern (辨證取穴 / 隨證取穴)

items.t: Choosing points based on the identified TCM pattern rather than the local anatomical site — e.g., points to clear heat, tonify Qi, resolve phlegm, calm the Shen, etc., regardless of where the symptom is felt

items.detail: Insomnia (Heart/Shen disturbance) → HT7, PC6, Anmian (EX); Phlegm-damp accumulation (regardless of the specific complaint) → ST40 (Fenglong), SP9 (Yinlingquan); Liver Qi stagnation → LV3, LV14, GB34

items.t: In practice, most prescriptions combine local/distal selection with pattern-based selection — e.g., a local point for the symptom plus a distal/pattern point for the underlying cause

items.detail: Pairing a point on the upper body (above the diaphragm/waist) with a point on the lower body (below), to treat conditions that involve both regions or to draw Qi/symptoms from one region to balance the other

LI4 (hand) + ST36 (leg) for general Qi/digestive regulation; CV22 Tiantu (throat) + ST40 Fenglong (leg) for phlegm obstructing the throat; GV20 Baihui (vertex) + KI1 Yongquan (sole) for prolapse/collapse patterns

items.t: The Eight Confluent points (八脈交會穴, see Point Categories) are a specialized form of upper-lower pairing — each combines a hand point with a foot point (e.g., SP4 + PC6)

blocks.heading: Combination method 2 — Front-Back / Shu-Mu (前後配穴法 / 俞募配穴法)

items.detail: Pairing a point on the front (chest/abdomen) with a point on the back for the same or related purpose — most classically, a Front-Mu point with its corresponding Back-Shu point for the same organ

The signature example: Back-Shu + Front-Mu of the same organ, used together to diagnose and treat that organ — e.g., BL13 (Feishu) + LU1 (Zhongfu) for Lung disorders; BL21 (Weishu) + CV12 (Zhongwan) for Stomach disorders; BL25 (Dachangshu) + ST25 (Tianshu) for Large Intestine disorders
Back-Shu points tend to be favored for chronic/deficiency (Zang-type) conditions, Front-Mu points for acute/excess (Fu-type) conditions — using both together covers both aspects

Using the same point bilaterally (both sides), or using a point on the healthy side to treat a disorder on the affected side (and vice versa) — based on the idea that the channels of both sides communicate at the midline/head
The most common approach — most prescriptions simply needle a point on both left and right sides for systemic or midline conditions

items.t: Contralateral (opposite-side) needling — "Miu Ci" (繆刺) / "Ju Ci" (巨刺)
items.detail: Classical technique for one-sided pain or paralysis: needle the unaffected side to treat the affected side, especially useful when the affected side is too painful/swollen to needle directly, or in early-stage facial paralysis/stroke sequelae

blocks.heading: Combination method 4 — Host-Guest / Yuan-Luo (主客配穴法 / 原絡配穴法)

items.detail: Pairing the Yuan-source point of the primarily-affected (diseased) channel — the "host" — with the Luo-connecting point of its exteriorly-interiorly paired channel — the "guest" — to treat disorders that involve both channels
1) Identify which of the paired channels is primarily diseased. 2) Needle the Yuan-source point of that channel (host). 3) Needle the Luo-connecting point of its paired channel (guest)

Primary Lung disease with some Large Intestine symptoms → LU9 (Taiyuan, Yuan of Lung, host) + LI6 (Pianli, Luo of Large Intestine, guest). If the Large Intestine were primarily diseased instead, the pairing would reverse: LI4 (Yuan of LI, host) + LU7 (Lieque, Luo of Lung, guest)

blocks.heading: Combination method 5 — Same-Name Channel (同名經配穴法)

items.detail: Pairing a point on a Hand channel with a point on its "same-name" Foot channel (both Tai Yin, both Yang Ming, etc.) — channels that share the same Yin/Yang-stage designation are considered to communicate closely, even though they belong to different organs
Tai Yin: LU ↔ SP · Yang Ming: LI ↔ ST · Shao Yin: HT ↔ KI · Tai Yang: SI ↔ BL · Jue Yin: PC ↔ LV · Shao Yang: TE ↔ GB
LI4 (Yang Ming, hand) + ST36 (Yang Ming, foot) — a very common combination for digestive and general Qi regulation, partly explained by this same-name relationship

blocks.heading: Quick reference — special-category combinations (see Point Categories chapter for full detail)

rows: Each of the 8 Extraordinary Vessels' points paired across hand+foot
Chest/Heart/Stomach; inner canthus/neck/shoulder; outer canthus/ear/cheek; Lung/throat/chest — depending on the pair
Back-Shu + Front-Mu of the same organ
Diagnosis and treatment of that Zang-Fu organ
Yuan point of the diseased channel + Luo point of its paired channel
Disorders spanning two exteriorly-interiorly related channels
The Five Shu point matching the "mother" element (Sheng cycle) tonifies; the "son" element point drains
Classical Five-Element tonification/sedation on a single channel

blocks.heading: Putting it together — a worked example
blocks.lead: A typical prescription layers several of these strategies rather than using just one.

items.detail: Wind-heat type common cold with sore throat and cough (see Pediatrics — Common Cold, Wind-heat type)
LU10 (Yuji) — near the throat/lung region functionally, clears heat locally in the Lung channel
LU5 (Chize), LI11 (Quchi) — distal points on the arm to clear heat and disperse the Lung
DU14 (Dazhui) — clears heat/exterior generally, as the meeting point of all 6 Yang channels
BL13 (Feishu) + LU1 (Zhongfu) — Back-Shu and Front-Mu of the Lung, layered on top of the above for a complete Lung-focused prescription

acu-selection.title: Mother-Child Tonification-Sedation (補母瀉子法)
acu-selection.source: Compiled from standard Five-Element / Five Shu point theory

blocks.heading: The Principle
blocks.lead: Each of the Five Shu points (Jing-Well, Ying-Spring, Shu-Stream, Jing-River, He-Sea) on every channel is assigned a Five-Element phase. To tonify a deficient channel, needle the point on that same channel corresponding to its "Mother" element (the element that generates the channel's own element) — this is 補母, "tonify the mother." To sedate/drain an excess channel, needle the point corresponding to its "Child" element (the element the channel's own element generates) — this is 瀉子, "drain the child." A more advanced variant selects the mother/child point on a *different* channel (the mother/child organ's own channel) rather than the same channel — both are recognized approaches, but the same-channel method below is the standard starting point.

blocks.heading: Yin Channels — Five Shu Order: Wood · Fire · Earth · Metal · Water
Yang Channels — Five Shu Order: Metal · Water · Wood · Fire · Earth
Worked Example
blocks.lead: A patient presents with Lung-Qi deficiency (chronic weak cough, spontaneous sweating, pale tongue). The Lung channel is Metal; Metal's mother is Earth. Tonify LU9 (Taiyuan), the Shu-Stream/Earth point on the Lung channel itself. If instead the presentation were Lung-Fire excess (rare, but illustrative), Metal's child is Water — sedate LU5 (Chize), the He-Sea/Water point.

acu-selection.title: Eight Confluent Point Pairing (八脈交會穴配穴法)
acu-selection.source: Compiled from standard Eight Extraordinary Vessel point theory

blocks.heading: The Principle
blocks.lead: Each of the 8 Extraordinary Vessels connects to the regular channels through one "confluent" point. These 8 points pair up (one from the upper body, one from the lower body) into 4 functional pairs. Needling a pair together treats the body region and organ systems shared by both connected vessels — a classic, high-yield combination method.

rows: Heart, chest, and Stomach disorders — epigastric pain, chest oppression, palpitations
Inner canthus, neck, ear, shoulder, and back — also insomnia/agitation (Yang Qiao regulates sleep-wake)
Outer canthus, cheek, neck, ear, and shoulder disorders
Lung, throat, chest, and diaphragm — also insomnia (Yin Qiao regulates sleep-wake)

blocks.heading: How to Use
blocks.lead: Choose the pair whose shared region/organ matches the patient's chief complaint, then needle both points together as the core of the prescription — add pattern-specific points around this pair as needed.

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Heart & Small Intestine
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM Zang-Fu pattern differentiation (臟腑辨證)

rows: Palpitations, shortness of breath (worse on exertion), spontaneous sweating, fatigue, pale or dull complexion
Heart Qi insufficient to move Blood and maintain normal heart function
Above symptoms + cold limbs, cold intolerance, possible chest oppression/pain, pale-bright complexion
Yang deficiency adds loss of warming function to Qi deficiency
Profuse sweating, cold limbs, extreme pallor, faint breathing, possible loss of consciousness
Critical collapse of Yang Qi — an emergency pattern
Palpitations, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, poor memory, dizziness, pale lips/complexion
Blood deficiency fails to nourish the Heart and Shen
Palpitations, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, five-center heat, night sweating, malar flush, dry mouth
Yin deficiency generates empty heat that disturbs the Shen
Restlessness, insomnia, thirst, mouth/tongue ulcers, red face, dark scanty urine, possible hematuria
Red, especially the tip; may be ulcerated
Excess fire in the Heart; may transfer heat to the Small Intestine via the interior-exterior relationship
Phlegm Misting the Heart (痰迷心竅)
Mental confusion/dullness, unconsciousness, gurgling phlegm sounds, possible mumbling or muteness
Turbid phlegm obstructs the orifices of the Heart, clouding consciousness
Phlegm-Fire Harassing the Heart (痰火擾心)
Mental agitation, mania, incoherent speech, insomnia, red face, yellow sticky sputum; may present as manic behavior
Phlegm combines with fire to harass the Shen — acute manic-type presentation
Stabbing chest pain radiating to shoulder/arm, palpitations, purplish lips/nails, cold limbs in severe cases
Blood stasis obstructing the Heart vessels — classic angina-type pattern

items.t: Small Intestine Excess Heat (小腸實熱)
items.detail: Usually a downward extension of Heart Fire — thirst, mouth ulcers, scanty-dark or bloody urine, burning urination. Treated by clearing Heart fire and draining it via the Small Intestine/Bladder

items.t: Small Intestine Qi Pain / Cold (小腸氣痛)
items.detail: Lower abdominal pain radiating to the groin/testicles, worse with cold — from cold stagnating in the Small Intestine's Qi dynamic, closely related to Liver channel cold patterns

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Liver & Gallbladder
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM Zang-Fu pattern differentiation (臟腑辨證)

rows: Hypochondriac/chest distension and pain, sighing, emotional depression or irritability, PMS, sensation of a lump in the throat (梅核氣), irregular menstruation
Liver loses its function of ensuring the smooth, free flow of Qi
Liver Fire Blazing Upward (肝火上炎)
Severe headache, red face/eyes, sudden severe tinnitus, bitter taste, irritability, possible hematemesis/epistaxis, constipation, dark urine
Qi stagnation transforms into fire, which flares upward along the channel
Dizziness, headache (vertex), tinnitus, irritability, red face, insomnia, with underlying lower-body deficiency signs (sore lower back/knees)
Liver/Kidney Yin deficiency fails to control Yang — "excess above, deficiency below"
Liver Wind — Extreme Heat Generating Wind (熱極生風)
High fever, convulsions, stiff neck, up-staring eyes, unconsciousness
Extreme heat scorches Liver Yin/Blood and stirs internal wind
Liver Wind — Yang Transforming into Wind (肝陽化風)
Sudden dizziness/collapse, tremor, numbness of limbs, possible stroke, deviated mouth/eye
Unchecked rising Liver Yang transforms into internal wind
Liver Wind — Blood Deficiency Generating Wind (血虛生風)
Dizziness, tremor, numbness/twitching of the limbs, pale complexion
Blood deficiency fails to nourish the sinews, producing a milder, deficiency-type wind
Dizziness, blurred vision, night blindness, numbness of the limbs, dry eyes, pale nails, scanty menstruation
Blood insufficient to nourish the Liver and its associated tissues (eyes, sinews, nails)
Dizziness, blurred vision, dry eyes, five-center heat, malar flush, night sweating
Yin fails to nourish/moisten the Liver; empty heat arises
Cold Stagnating in the Liver Channel (寒滯肝脈)
Lower abdominal/genital pain, cold sensation, testicular pain/swelling, relieved by warmth
Cold pathogen invades the Liver channel (which circles the genitals), stagnating Qi and Blood
Hypochondriac pain/distension, bitter taste, nausea, jaundice, scanty-yellow urine, genital itching/damp sores, possible fever
Damp-heat accumulates in the Liver/Gallbladder, impairing the flow of bile

items.t: Gallbladder Qi Timidity / Deficiency (膽鬱痰擾 / 膽氣虛怯)
items.detail: Timidity, easily startled, insomnia with excessive dreaming, indecisiveness, dizziness — often combined with phlegm disturbing the GB; overlaps with the Damp-Heat pattern above for excess-type GB disorders

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Spleen & Stomach
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM Zang-Fu pattern differentiation (臟腑辨證)

rows: Poor appetite, abdominal distension after eating, loose stools, fatigue, sallow complexion, emaciation
Spleen fails to transform and transport food and fluids
Above symptoms + cold limbs, cold abdominal pain relieved by warmth/pressure, watery/loose stools, edema
Yang deficiency adds loss of warming function; worse cold-damp signs
Sinking of Spleen/Middle Qi (脾氣下陷)
Sensation of downward bearing/dragging, organ prolapse (rectum, uterus, stomach), chronic diarrhea, fatigue
Spleen Qi fails to hold the organs in their proper place
Spleen Not Controlling Blood (脾不統血)
Chronic bleeding (uterine bleeding, blood in stool, easy bruising) plus Spleen Qi deficiency signs
Spleen Qi deficiency fails to hold Blood within the vessels
Cold-Damp Encumbering the Spleen (寒濕困脾)
Heavy sensation of head/body, abdominal fullness, poor appetite, nausea, loose stools, sweet/sticky taste, possible dull-type jaundice
Cold-damp obstructs Spleen Yang, impairing transformation and transportation
Abdominal fullness/nausea, poor appetite, sticky/bitter taste, heaviness, bright-type jaundice, scanty-yellow urine, sticky/loose stool
Damp-heat accumulates in the middle jiao
Dry mouth/throat, hunger without desire to eat, dry stools, epigastric discomfort, hiccup
Stomach Yin insufficient to moisten; empty heat may arise
Stomach Cold (胃寒 / 寒邪犯胃)
Sudden epigastric pain relieved by warmth, worse with cold, vomiting of clear fluid, no thirst
Cold pathogen invades the Stomach, contracting and stagnating Qi
Epigastric burning pain, excessive hunger, thirst for cold drinks, bad breath, gum swelling/bleeding, constipation
Excess heat accumulates in the Stomach
Food Retention in the Stomach (食滯胃脘)
Epigastric/abdominal fullness and pain, foul belching, acid regurgitation, foul-smelling stool or vomit, aversion to food
Undigested food accumulates, obstructing the Stomach's descending function

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Lung & Large Intestine
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM Zang-Fu pattern differentiation (臟腑辨證)

rows: Shortness of breath (worse on exertion), weak cough, low voice, spontaneous sweating, susceptibility to colds, pale complexion
Lung Qi insufficient — fails to govern Qi/respiration and disperse Wei Qi
Dry cough, scanty/blood-streaked sputum, dry throat/mouth, malar flush, night sweating, low-grade afternoon fever
Lung Yin insufficient to moisten; empty heat generated
Wind-Cold Fettering the Lung (風寒束肺)
Cough with thin-white sputum, aversion to cold, mild fever, no sweating, headache, stuffy/runny nose, itchy throat
Exterior wind-cold constrains the Lung's dispersing function
Wind-Heat Invading the Lung (風熱犯肺)
Cough with thick-yellow sputum, fever more prominent than chills, sore throat, thirst, yellow nasal discharge
Exterior wind-heat impairs the Lung's dispersing/descending function
Phlegm-Damp Obstructing the Lung (痰濕阻肺)
Cough with profuse white sputum, easy to expectorate, chest oppression, wheezing
Phlegm-Heat Obstructing the Lung (痰熱壅肺)
Cough with thick-yellow sputum, difficult to expectorate, chest pain, fever, wheezing, possible blood-streaked sputum
Phlegm combines with heat, obstructing the Lung's descending function
Damp-Heat in the Large Intestine (大腸濕熱)
Abdominal pain, urgent-frequent diarrhea/dysentery with blood/mucus, burning anus, fever, thirst
Damp-heat accumulates in the Large Intestine
Large Intestine Fluid Deficiency (大腸液虧)
Dry, difficult-to-pass stools (constipation), dry mouth/throat, occasional dizziness
Fluid deficiency fails to moisten the intestines
Constipation, abdominal fullness/pain worse with pressure, fever, thirst
Heat combines with stool, obstructing the bowel (connects to Yang Ming Fu-stage patterns)

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Kidney & Bladder
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM Zang-Fu pattern differentiation (臟腑辨證)

rows: Cold limbs/body (especially lower back/knees), sore/weak lower back and knees, impotence/infertility, frequent clear urination or urinary difficulty, edema, pale complexion, early-morning diarrhea (五更瀉)
Deep-weak, especially at the Chi position
Kidney Yang insufficient to warm the body and promote water metabolism
Sore/weak lower back and knees, dizziness, tinnitus, malar flush, night sweating, five-center heat, dry mouth, nocturnal emission
Thready-rapid, especially at the Chi position
Kidney Yin insufficient to nourish and moisten
Kidney Qi Not Firm (腎氣不固)
Frequent/copious clear urination, enuresis, incontinence, premature ejaculation, spontaneous seminal emission, thin leukorrhea, tendency to miscarry
Kidney Qi insufficient to consolidate and hold
Kidney Failing to Receive Qi (腎不納氣)
Chronic wheezing/asthma worse on exertion, shortness of breath, spontaneous sweating, cold limbs
Kidney fails to grasp and anchor Lung Qi downward — often a late-stage asthma pattern
In children: delayed growth/development; in adults: premature aging, infertility, poor memory, weak bones/teeth
Essence (Jing) insufficient to support growth, reproduction, and marrow production
Kidney Yin and Yang Both Deficient (腎陰陽兩虛)
Combination of Yin-deficiency and Yang-deficiency signs together (e.g. cold limbs plus five-center heat)
Long-term Kidney deficiency exhausting both Yin and Yang aspects
Damp-Heat in the Bladder (膀胱濕熱)
Frequent-urgent-painful urination, burning sensation, dark/turbid urine, possible hematuria or stones, fever
Damp-heat accumulates in the Bladder, impairing its Qi-transformation function

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Combined Patterns
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM Zang-Fu pattern differentiation (臟腑辨證)

blocks.heading: When two organs are affected together
blocks.lead: In practice, deficiency and disharmony rarely stay confined to a single organ — these combined ("兼證") patterns are extremely common in clinic and on exams.

rows: Insomnia, palpitations, dizziness, tinnitus, sore lower back/knees, nocturnal emission, five-center heat, dry mouth/throat
Kidney Yin deficient, fails to rise and nourish the Heart; Heart Fire fails to descend and warm the Kidney — Water and Fire fail to balance each other
Hypochondriac distension/pain, emotional depression or irritability, poor appetite, abdominal distension, loose stools (worse with stress)
Liver Qi stagnation invades/overacts on the Spleen ("wood overacting on earth")
Epigastric/hypochondriac distension and pain, belching, acid regurgitation, poor appetite, irritability
Liver Qi stagnation invades the Stomach, impairing its descending function
Liver Fire Invading the Lung (肝火犯肺 / 木火刑金)
Cough with hypochondriac pain, irritability, bitter taste, red face, possible coughing of blood
Liver fire rises and rebels against the Lung, impairing its descending function
Cold limbs, chronic/early-morning diarrhea, edema, sore/weak lower back and knees, poor appetite
Long-standing Spleen Yang deficiency and Kidney Yang deficiency reinforce each other
Dry cough, scanty sputum (possibly blood-streaked), sore lower back/knees, night sweating, malar flush, nocturnal emission
Lung Yin fails to descend and nourish the Kidney, or Kidney Yin fails to ascend and moisten the Lung — Metal and Water mutually deficient
Palpitations, insomnia, poor memory, poor appetite, fatigue, abdominal distension, loose stools, pale complexion
Heart Blood and Spleen Qi both deficient — often from overthinking/worry, or chronic blood loss
Palpitations, shortness of breath, cough, weak voice, spontaneous sweating, fatigue
Both Heart and Lung Qi deficient, impairing the circulation of Qi and Blood
Dizziness, tinnitus, sore lower back/knees, dry eyes, blurred vision, five-center heat, night sweating, nocturnal emission
Liver and Kidney Yin share the same source ("肝腎同源") and are frequently deficient together — often underlies Liver Yang Rising

acu-pattern.title: Disease-Pattern Differentiation — Internal Medicine (3)
acu-pattern.source: Compiled from standard TCM internal medicine references — patterns, signs, and treatment points follow conventional TCM clinical teaching

blocks.heading: 23. Atrophy-Flaccidity Syndrome (痿證, Wei Zheng)
blocks.lead: Weakness, wasting, and loss of function of the limbs/muscles without pain. Classic teaching emphasizes treating via the Yangming channels, since Yangming is considered richest in Qi and Blood and governs the nourishment of sinews and muscles ("治痿獨取陽明").
