acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — The Eight Principles
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Foundation for every other pattern-identification method; establishes location and nature of the disharmony and the treatment principle. Applicable in every case.

The Eight Principles are interrelated, not mutually exclusive — a condition can show both Hot & Cold, or both Full & Empty, at once. Not every condition needs to display all four axes (Ext/Int, Hot/Cold, Full/Empty, Yin/Yang).
Exterior–Interior (location) · Hot–Cold (nature) · Full–Empty (state of the struggle between pathogen and Upright Qi) · Yin–Yang (summary of the other six)
Skin, muscles, channels — specifically the space between skin & muscles where Defensive Qi (Wei Qi) and sweat are located.
The Internal Organs.
(1) Skin & muscles, acute onset — invasion of Wind-Cold or Wind-Heat. (2) Channels, slower onset — Painful Obstruction (Bi) Syndrome.

blocks.heading: Exterior patterns — Cold vs Heat, Empty vs Full
blocks.lead: Core exterior sign: aversion to cold (subjective chill) + fever (objective heat on palpation) + aching body, stiff neck, floating pulse. Cold/Heat is told mainly by thirst (Hot) vs no thirst (Cold) and tight (Cold) vs rapid (Hot) pulse. Empty/Full is told mainly by sweating (Empty) vs none (Full), slow (Empty) vs tight (Full) pulse, and severity of body aches (worse in Full).

rows: Slight fever, pronounced aversion to cold, severe body aches, severe stiff neck, no sweating, no thirst
Aversion to cold, fever, slight sweating, thirst
Thin white coating, sometimes red sides/tip
Slight or no fever, sweating, aversion to wind, slight body aches
Fever, no sweating, severe body aches, aversion to cold

blocks.heading: Exterior patterns — Painful Obstruction Syndrome (Bi 痺)
blocks.lead: Second type of exterior pattern: a pathogenic factor invades the channels gradually and obstructs the circulation of Qi in channels/joints.

items.detail: Usually one joint; pain is severe and relieved by heat.
Pain migrates from joint to joint.
Swelling of the joints.
Severe pain with swollen, hot joints.
An exterior pathogen moves inward; once interior, it's an interior pattern — marked by the disappearance of aversion to cold and onset of aversion to heat.
Depends on the organ affected and whether the condition is Hot/Cold, Full/Empty.

blocks.heading: Hot – Cold (nature of the pattern)
blocks.lead: Heat and Cold describe the nature of a pattern; clinical picture depends on whether combined with Full or Empty.

rows: Thirst, feeling of heat, mental restlessness, red face, dry stools, scanty dark urine; also: raised hot red skin eruptions, burning sensations, heavy dark-red bleeding, extreme restlessness/mania (Heart)
Diet (red meat, spices, alcohol), emotional stress, external pathogen penetrating inward and transforming into Heat
Heat in the afternoon/evening, dry mouth with desire to sip, dry throat at night, night sweating, five-centre/five-palm heat, dry stools, scanty dark urine
Overwork, irregular eating, excessive sexual activity, persistent heavy blood loss (Yin deficiency)

Feeling cold, cold limbs, no thirst, pale face, abdominal pain worse with pressure, desire for warm liquids, loose stools, clear abundant urine; can invade Stomach, Intestines, Uterus, Liver channel directly
Cold foods, external Cold penetrating inward (Excess Yin)
Feeling cold, cold limbs, dull pale face, no thirst, listlessness, sweating, loose stools, clear abundant urine
Excessive physical work, inadequate hot food, excessive sexual activity, internal Cold injuring Yang (Yang deficiency)
Desire to drink in small sips

items.t: Cold on Exterior — Heat in Interior
items.detail: Pre-existing interior Heat, then invaded by exterior Wind-Cold. Fever with aversion to cold, no sweating, headache, stiff neck, body aches, irritability, thirst. T: normal/red with yellow coating. P: fine-rapid-floating.

items.t: Heat on Exterior — Cold in Interior
items.detail: Cold-condition patient attacked by exterior Wind-Heat. Fever with aversion to cold, sore throat, thirst, headache, loose stools, chilliness, profuse pale urine. T: normal/red-yellow coating or pale-thick white coating. P: floating-rapid.
Heat tends to rise. Thirst, irritability, sour regurgitation, bitter taste, mouth ulcers, loose stools, borborygmus, profuse pale urine. Tongue/pulse can show either Heat or Cold signs.

Rare, extreme condition; tongue-body colour reflects the true state. Red 'powdery' cheeks but rest of face white, irritability alternating with listlessness, desire to curl up, quiet low breathing/voice, thirst without desire to drink (or warm fluids only), body feels hot but patient wants covers, sore throat without redness/swelling, pale urine. T: pale, wet. P: rapid, floating, big but empty.

Dark face, bright 'spirited' eyes, red dry lips, irritability, noisy breathing, loud voice, thirst for cold drinks, scanty dark urine, constipation, burning sensation in anus, cold limbs but hot chest. T: red-dry. P: deep, full.

blocks.heading: Full – Empty (state of the Upright Qi vs the pathogen)

items.detail: A pathogenic factor is present while Upright Qi is relatively intact and actively fighting it.
Deficiency of Upright Qi with absence of a pathogenic factor.
Deficiency of Upright Qi with a lingering pathogen the body is not fighting effectively.

Strong loud voice, excruciating pain, very red face, restlessness, throwing off bedclothes, outbursts of temper. Any exterior invasion (Cold/Wind/Damp/Heat) is Full by definition; any interior pathogen is Full provided the body's Qi is strong enough to engage it (interior Cold, Heat, Dampness, Wind, Fire, Phlegm, Qi stagnation, Blood stasis).

Weak voice, dull lingering pain, very pale face, slight sweating, listlessness, curling up in bed, quiet disposition. Common manifestations: tiredness, loose stools, weak voice, desire to lie down, pale tongue, weak pulse.

rows: Pale face, weak voice, slight sweating, slight shortness of breath, tiredness, loose stools, poor appetite, empty pulse
Chilliness, bright pale face, cold limbs, no thirst, desire for hot drinks, loose stools, frequent pale urination, weak pulse, pale wet tongue (plus Empty Qi signs)
Dull pale face, pale lips, blurred vision, dry hair, tiredness, poor memory, numbness/tingling, insomnia, scanty periods, fine/choppy pulse, pale thin tongue
Heat in the afternoon/evening, dry throat at night, night sweating, thin body, floating-empty pulse, red peeled dry tongue

blocks.heading: Yin – Yang (summary of the other six)

items.detail: = Interior, Emptiness, Cold
= Exterior, Fullness, Heat
Abundant perspiration, skin hot to touch, hot limbs, dry mouth with desire for cold sips, retention of urine, constipation. T: red-peeled, short, dry. P: floating-empty-rapid.

Chilliness, cold limbs, weak breathing, profuse oily sweating, no thirst, frequent/profuse urination or incontinence, loose stools/incontinence. T: pale, wet, swollen, short. P: feeble (minute), deep.

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — Pathogenic Factors
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: External (climatic): Wind, Cold, Dampness, Dryness, Heat, Fire. Internal (from disharmony): e.g. Qi stagnation, Blood stasis, Phlegm — same six names but arising from within.

A pathogen is a Full pattern by the Eight Principles, but a pathogen can generate deficiency (e.g. Fire weakens Yin) and deficiency can generate a pathogen (e.g. Spleen-Qi deficiency generates Dampness).

items.t: Climate as a cause of disease
items.detail: Disease results when weather is excessive/unseasonal, the body is relatively weak, or the weather changes very rapidly — i.e. only when there is a relative imbalance between the body's Qi and the exterior factor.
Wind–Spring · Cold–Winter · Summer-Heat–Summer · Dampness–Late Summer · Dryness–Autumn · Fire–Summer. Except Summer-Heat, any factor can arise in any season depending on living conditions.

blocks.heading: Wind (風) — general characteristics
blocks.lead: Yang in nature, injures Blood & Yin; the vehicle through which other climatic factors invade the body; arises and changes quickly.

items.detail: Rapid onset; rapid change in signs; signs migrate from place to place; causes tremors/convulsions but also stiffness/paralysis; causes numbness/tingling; affects the top of the body; affects Lungs first (external) or Liver (internal); affects the skin; causes itching.

blocks.heading: External Wind — invasion of Lung Defensive Qi
blocks.lead: Wind penetrates via the skin, disrupts circulation of Defensive Qi between skin & muscles → chilliness/aversion to cold; interferes with Lung Qi's diffusing/descending → sneezing, cough, runny nose. Attacks the Taiyang channels (SI/BL) first → neck stiffness; lodges in the throat → itchy throat.

items.detail: Aversion to cold/wind, sneezing cough, runny nose, fever, occipital stiffness & ache, itchy throat, sweating or not (per Wind vs Cold predominance), floating pulse.
Invade facial channels directly (Bell's Palsy — deviation of mouth/eyebrows); invade Yang channels and settle in joints (stiffness/pain); affect the Liver (migraine, neck ache from windy weather).

rows: Aversion to cold, sneezing, cough, runny nose with white watery mucus, fever, severe occipital stiffness/ache, no sweating, no thirst
Aversion to cold, fever, sneezing, cough, runny nose with slightly yellow mucus, occipital stiffness/ache, slight sweating, sore throat, swollen tonsils, thirst
Aversion to cold, fever, swollen neck glands, nausea, sweating, occipital stiffness, body/muscle ache, heaviness of body, swollen joints
Fever, slight aversion to cold, slight sweating, dry skin/nose/mouth/throat, dry cough, sore throat
Aversion to cold, fever, edema especially face, swollen face/eyes, cough with profuse white watery mucus, sweating, no thirst

blocks.heading: Painful Obstruction Syndrome (channel invasion by Wind)

items.detail: External Wind can invade the channels directly and settle in the joints, giving rise to Painful Obstruction (Bi) Syndrome — see the Cold/Wind/Dampness/Heat Bi table under Eight Principles.

blocks.heading: Cold (寒) — general characteristics
blocks.lead: Yin pathogen, injures Yang; can be exterior or interior, Full or Empty. Exterior Cold is Full by definition; Interior Cold splits into Interior Full Cold (from invading exterior Cold) and Interior Empty Cold (Spleen-Yang / Kidney-Yang deficiency, which Interior Full Cold can turn into over time).

items.detail: Injures Yang, congeals Blood, contracts tissues, causes clear discharges; pertains to the Kidneys.

Feeling cold, cold limbs, thin clear discharges, spastic/crampy pain aggravated by cold & relieved by heat, desire for warm drinks, no thirst, white complexion, pale tongue with white coating, slow pulse.
Lung Defensive Qi (often with Wind → Wind-Cold); channels & joints (Bi Syndrome — intense pain, usually one joint); muscles & sinews (local pain/stiffness); directly into Stomach, Intestines, Uterus.

rows: Feeling cold, cold limbs, thin clear discharges, severe pain worse with pressure, aggravated by cold/relieved by heat, desire for warm drinks, no thirst, bright white complexion
Feeling cold, cold limbs, thin clear discharges, dull pain relieved by pressure, aggravated by cold/relieved by heat, desire for warm drinks, no thirst, dull white complexion

blocks.heading: Summer-Heat (暑) — a Yang, purely external factor
blocks.lead: Related to summer; has no internal equivalent (unlike the other five).

items.detail: Fever, aversion to cold, sweating, headache, feeling of heaviness, uncomfortable epigastrium, irritability, thirst. T: red front/sides, white sticky coating. P: soggy-rapid.
Injures Yin; seasonal; invades the top of the body; scattering; carries some interior Heat; harbours (shelters) Dampness.

blocks.heading: Dampness (濕) — general characteristics
blocks.lead: Yin pathogen; sticky and heavy, hard to eliminate, slows things down; flows downward and causes repeated attacks.

items.detail: Damp weather, living in a damp place, wet clothes, working in damp conditions, sitting on damp ground.
Heaviness, poor appetite, feeling of fullness, sticky taste, urinary difficulty, vaginal discharge, sticky tongue coating, slippery/soggy pulse.
Heavy · Dirty · Sticky.

rows: Acute painful/difficult urination, scanty frequent urination, cloudy urine, heaviness in lower abdomen (+ Heat: burning pain, dark urine, thirst w/o desire to drink)
Acute vomiting and/or odourless watery diarrhea, epigastric pain & stuffiness, cold limbs, no appetite
Acute odourless watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, heaviness
Acute-onset painful period (one-off), excessive vaginal discharge
Acute hypochondrial pain, heaviness, bitter taste

blocks.heading: External dampness — acute channel & Defensive-Qi invasion

items.detail: Sudden achy, swollen, heavy joint(s). One joint = Cold-Dampness; several joints = Damp-Heat.

items.t: External Damp-Heat at Defensive Qi level
items.detail: Aversion to cold, fever (worse in afternoon), body hot to touch, swollen glands, headache 'as if wrapped', oppression of chest/epigastrium, sticky taste, no thirst. T: white sticky coating. P: soggy.
Fever, slight aversion to cold, sweating, headache, heaviness of body, uncomfortable epigastrium, irritability, thirst. T: red with sticky coating. P: soggy-rapid.
Acute attacks of Dampness are generally exterior in origin; chronic Dampness can also give occasional acute flare-ups (e.g. interstitial cystitis).

rows: Epigastric/abdominal fullness & heaviness, nausea, vomiting, sticky taste, loose stools with mucus
Urinary difficulty with little/no pain, cloudy urine
Abdominal fullness & heaviness, loose stools with mucus
Abdominal fullness & heaviness, vaginal discharge, mid-cycle pain/bleeding, painful periods
Urinary difficulty/pain, cloudy urine, hypogastric fullness/distension, vaginal discharge, genital sores/itching
Urinary difficulty/pain, cloudy urine, heaviness/ache of the back

items.detail: Aching, swelling, and heaviness of the joints.
Vesicles, papules, oozing skin lesions, puffiness of the skin.

blocks.heading: Dryness (燥) — Yang pathogen, related to Autumn/Lungs

items.detail: Dry throat, lips, tongue, mouth, skin, stools; scanty urination.
Aversion to cold, fever, dry cough, dry mouth/throat/nose. T: dry. P: floating.

(1) From External Dryness. (2) From Yin deficiency. (3) Arising internally: excessive drying/baked foods, irregular eating (weakens Stomach-Yin), excessive voice use e.g. teachers (weakens Lung-Yin), excessive sexual activity in men (weakens Kidney-Yin), chronic menorrhagia in women (damages Blood, Liver-Yin, Kidney-Yin), smoking (injures Lung-Yin & Kidney-Yin).
From irregular diet (eating late, eating in a hurry, working straight after eating). Dry mouth, dry tongue, cracks in the tongue.
From years of excessive voice use → Lung-Qi deficiency. Dry mouth/throat, dry cough, dry tongue, dry skin, hoarse voice, dry stools if severe.
From excessive sexual activity (men), chronic menorrhagia (women), smoking. Dry throat with night sipping, scanty urination, dry cracked tongue, dry menstrual blood, dry skin.

blocks.heading: Fire (火) — Yang pathogen that rises; mainly internal

items.detail: Physiological Fire (Fire of the Gate of Life) vs Pathological Fire (e.g. Liver-Fire).
Thirst, feeling of heat, some mental restlessness, red tongue, rapid pulse.
Fire is more 'solid', dries more (dark scanty urine, dry stools); moves upward (mouth ulcers); damages blood vessels (bleeding); affects the Mind more (anxiety, agitation, mental illness).
Rises to the head; dries fluids; injures Blood and Yin; causes bleeding; can generate Wind; affects the Mind; causes swollen ulcers.

Red face & eyes, pronounced heat, dry mouth, bitter taste, constipation, scanty dark urine, thirst, mental agitation, dark purple macules under the skin, bleeding. T: red with dry yellow coating. P: full-rapid.

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — Internal Seven Emotions
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Qi is matter-energy giving rise to physical, mental, and emotional phenomena simultaneously; body, mind, and emotions form an integrated whole (expressed through the Three Treasures: Essence/Jing – Qi – Mind/Shen). In Chinese medicine the Internal Organs and their emotional aspects interact in a circle, unlike the Western model of a brain transmitting a nerve pulse to an organ.

Normally emotions are not a cause of disease — only when they are long-lasting, very intense, or both. Emotions can be either a cause of disease or a presenting symptom, and the state of the Internal Organs also affects one's emotional state.
Anger–Liver · Joy–Heart · Sadness–Lungs & Heart · Worry–Lungs & Spleen · Pensiveness–Spleen · Fear–Kidneys · Shock–Heart
Anger makes Qi rise · Joy slows Qi · Sadness dissolves Qi · Worry knots Qi · Pensiveness knots Qi · Fear makes Qi descend · Shock scatters Qi

Any emotion can, over time, cause Qi stagnation → possibly Blood stasis (especially in women) or Heat, which can intensify into Fire (more drying, affects the Mind more). Disrupted Qi may also lead to Phlegm formation.
Resentment, repressed anger, irritability, frustration, rage, hatred, indignation, bitterness.

Long-term anger affects the Liver → Liver-Qi stagnation, Liver-Blood stagnation, Liver-Yang rising, or Blazing Liver-Fire. Makes Qi rise → headache, dizziness, tinnitus, neck stiffness, red blotches/red face.

To Stomach: epigastric/hypochondriac pain, hiccup, belching, nausea, vomiting. To Intestines: abdominal pain/distension, alternating constipation & diarrhea. To Heart (via Liver-Fire → Heart-Fire): mental restlessness, agitation.
Affects the Heart, slows Qi down. 'Joy' here means over-stimulation; sudden joy behaves like shock.
Palpitations, overexcitability, insomnia, restlessness, red tip of the tongue.
Weakens the Lungs, also affects the Heart; includes grief and regret; depletes Qi.
Breathlessness, tiredness, uncomfortable chest, depression or crying; in women, Lung-Qi deficiency can lead to Blood deficiency and amenorrhea.
Knots Qi; affects both Lungs and Spleen.
Uncomfortable chest, slight breathlessness, tense shoulders, dry cough, weak voice, sighing, pale complexion.
Poor appetite, mild epigastric discomfort, some abdominal pain/distension, tiredness, pale complexion.
Palpitations, slight tightness of the chest, insomnia.
Knots Qi; affects the Spleen.
Poor appetite, mild epigastric discomfort, abdominal pain/distension, tiredness, pale complexion.
Palpitations, slight chest tightness, insomnia.
Both a chronic state of fear/anxiety and sudden fright.
Makes Qi descend; affects the Kidneys.
Nocturnal enuresis.
Incontinence of urine or diarrhea.
If the Heart is strong: Qi descends. If the Heart is weak: Qi rises as Empty-Heat — palpitations, insomnia, night sweating, dry mouth, malar flush, rapid pulse.
Suspends Qi; affects the Heart and Kidneys; causes sudden depletion of Heart-Qi.
Palpitations, breathlessness, insomnia.

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — Qi, Blood & Body Fluids
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

rows: Slight shortness of breath, weak voice, spontaneous sweating, poor appetite, loose stools, tiredness
Bearing-down feeling, tiredness, listlessness, mental depression, prolapse of organs (Stomach, uterus, intestines, anus, bladder)
Distension, distending pain that moves from place to place, mental depression, irritability, gloominess, mood swings, frequent sighing
Normal or slightly red on sides

blocks.heading: Rebellious Qi (氣逆)
blocks.lead: Occurs when Qi flows against its normal physiological direction. Normal directions: Spleen-Qi up · Stomach-Qi down · Lung-Qi down · Heart-Qi down · Liver-Qi in all directions/upward · Intestines-Qi down · Kidney-Qi down (also somewhat up) · Bladder-Qi down.

items.detail: Belching, hiccup, nausea, vomiting.
Diarrhea, prolapse.
Cough, asthma.
Asthma.
Mental restlessness, insomnia.
Headache, dizziness, irritability.
Nausea, belching, vomiting.
Diarrhea.
Dry stools.
Burning urination.

rows: Dull white/sallow complexion, dizziness, poor memory, numbness/tingling, blurred vision, insomnia, pale lips, scanty periods/amenorrhea, depression, slight anxiety
Dark complexion, purple lips, boring/fixed/stabbing pain, non-moving abdominal masses, purple nails, dark clotted bleeding, painful periods with dark clots

blocks.heading: Blood Deficiency vs Blood Stasis — mechanisms

items.detail: Dryness (dry tongue/skin/hair, withered nails), Liver-Yang rising, Internal Wind (vertigo, tics, tremors).
Qi stagnation, Qi deficiency, Heat in the Blood, Blood deficiency, Interior Cold, Phlegm.

blocks.heading: Qi Stagnation vs Blood Stasis — comparison

rows: Purple nails, dark face, painful/irregular periods with dark clots, abdominal pain, premenstrual pain
Purple lips, stabbing/pricking chest pain, mental restlessness
Purple sides toward front; purple distended sublingual veins
Oppression of the chest, coughing dark blood
Purple sides toward front; purple distended sublingual veins
Epigastric pain, vomiting dark blood, dark blood in stools
Severe abdominal pain, dark blood in stools
Painful/premenstrual pain, dark clotted menstrual blood, amenorrhea, fixed abdominal masses

items.detail: Feeling of heat, red skin eruptions, thirst, bleeding, red tongue, rapid pulse.
Anxiety, mental restlessness, mouth ulcers.
Itchy, hot, red skin diseases.
Excessive blood loss during the period.
Blood in the stools.
Epistaxis, hematemesis, hemoptysis, melena, menorrhagia, metrorrhagia, hematuria.
Deficient Qi unable to hold Blood, or Blood-Heat pushing Blood out of the vessels.
Dry skin, dry mouth, dry nose, dry cough, dry lips, dry tongue.
Yin deficiency; drying diet or irregular eating; heavy/prolonged fluid loss (sweating, vomiting, diarrhea); heavy Blood loss; severe chronic Blood deficiency.

Lungs: dry skin, dry cough. Stomach: dry tongue with horizontal cracks, dry mouth without desire to drink (or small sips). Kidneys: scanty urination, dry mouth/throat at night. Large Intestine: dry stools.

rows: Lung-Yang deficiency or invasion of exterior Wind-Cold

items.detail: Both a pathological product and an etiological factor; main cause is Spleen deficiency (Lungs and Kidneys also involved).
Swollen tongue body, sticky tongue coating, slippery or wiry pulse; often with chest oppression, nausea, heaviness, muzzy head, dizziness.

Substantial (has form) e.g. phlegm in the Lungs. Non-substantial (no form): under the skin (nerve-ganglia swellings, lymph-node swelling, thyroid swelling, lipomas); in the channels (numbness, common in the elderly, Wind-stroke); misting the Heart (schizophrenia, manic depression, epilepsy); in Gallbladder/Kidneys (stones); in the joints (deformities of chronic rheumatoid arthritis).

rows: Profuse, white, sticky phlegm that is easy to expectorate; chest/epigastric oppression, nausea, sticky taste, no thirst
Sticky yellow phlegm, chest oppression, nausea, red face, dry mouth, restlessness
White watery phlegm, chest oppression, cold limbs, nausea
Dizziness, nausea, vomiting, numbness (esp. unilateral), coughing phlegm, chest oppression, rattling throat, aphasia
Feeling of swelling in the throat with no actual swelling, difficulty swallowing, chest/diaphragm oppression, irritability, moodiness, depression
Abdominal fullness/distension, vomiting watery fluids, dry mouth without desire to drink, splashing stomach sound, chest fullness, loose stools, weight loss
Hypochondrial pain worse with cough/breathing, distension, shortness of breath
Heaviness of body, muscle pain, no sweating, no desire to drink, cough with abundant white sputum
Cough, asthma, edema, chest oppression, dizziness, abundant white sputum
Primarily lower body (urinary issues from Lower Burner/Bladder Dampness; intestinal mucus/blood from Damp-Heat) — exception: Dampness in the head blocks clear Yang, causing heaviness/dizziness
Primarily middle & upper body — chest (oppression), throat (sputum sensation), head (heaviness, muzziness, dizziness)

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — Miscellaneous Causes of Disease
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Every person is born with a constitution shaped by the parents; it is not entirely fixed or unchangeable.
Parents' general health; parents' health at time of conception; advanced parental age; events during pregnancy (emotional stress, drugs, medicines, alcohol, smoking).
Long hours over many years without adequate rest, often with irregular diet and emotional stress (particularly worry).

Qi and Yin are two energy reservoirs — Qi is used by working and replenished by rest; Yin is the longer-term reserve. When overworked, Qi cannot replenish fast enough and Yin is drawn on instead, causing Yin deficiency (particularly Kidney-Yin). Mental overwork weakens the Spleen.

blocks.heading: Excessive physical work / lack of exercise

items.detail: Occupational labour or excessive exercise (gym, football, heavy lifting, ballet, etc.). Reasonable exercise is beneficial; excess depletes Qi (e.g. weightlifting weakens the Kidneys) and stagnates Qi locally (weightlifting → lower back, jogging → knees, tennis → elbow).
Depletes the Spleen (controls the muscles) and the Liver (controls the sinews). Repetitive use of one body part stagnates Qi there (hairdresser's arm, bricklayer's elbow, typist's wrist).
Leads to Qi stagnation and sometimes Dampness. Yoga and Tai Ji Quan aim to develop Qi rather than just muscle.
Depletes Kidney-Essence.
Marked fatigue, dizziness, blurred vision, lower backache, weak knees, frequent urination. Should be adjusted for age and physical condition.
Vitamins & minerals content; chemicals (preservatives, flavourings, colourings), hormones, antibiotics.
Can be a cause of disease from either a quantitative or qualitative standpoint.
Common in developing regions, poor elderly living alone, people on very strict diets, those with anorexia/bulimia, or vegetarians without a properly balanced diet.
Causes Qi and Blood deficiency; weakens the Spleen's transformation & transportation function.

rows: Cold foods (cold-energy e.g. lettuce; cold-temperature e.g. iced water)
Upper respiratory catarrh, abdominal distension/fullness, mucus in stools, vaginal discharge
Hot / spicy foods (curry, alcohol, lamb, beef, spices)
Generates Heat, especially of Stomach or Liver
Bitter taste, burning epigastric sensation, thirst
Greasy / fried foods (fried food, milk, cheese, butter, cream, ice cream, banana, peanuts, fatty meat)
Forms Phlegm/Dampness, obstructs Spleen transformation & transportation
Sinusitis, nasal discharge, muzzy head, dull headaches, bronchitis

items.detail: Eating in a hurry, discussing work while eating, going straight back to work after eating, eating late in the evening, eating while emotionally tense.
Interferes with proper digestion and leads to Stomach-Yin deficiency: tongue with rootless coating or no central coating, thirst, epigastric pain, dry stools.
Counted among the emotional causes of disease.
Causes pain, bruising, swelling. Slight trauma → local Qi stagnation; severe trauma → Blood stasis in the area.

Old accidents/falls can be a hidden cause of disease later (e.g. headache), and can act as a concurrent cause when they overlap with a new trauma or disease (e.g. an old knee injury can predispose to a Painful Obstruction Syndrome triggered later by cold/damp exposure).

Intestinal worm infestation is more common in children. Though worms are an external cause, TCM regards poor diet as a contributory factor — excess greasy/sweet food creates Dampness, a favourable breeding ground.

White facial vesicles, sallow complexion, emaciation, small white spots inside the lips, purple spots inside the eyelids, loss of appetite or pica (desire to eat wax, leaves, raw rice), abdominal pain, itchy nose and anus.

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Pericardium
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: The membrane wrapping the Heart; protects the Heart, governs Blood, and houses the Mind (with the Heart). Its channel's sphere of influence is the centre of the thorax; exteriorly-interiorly related to the Triple Burner. PC points can invigorate or cool Blood and calm the Mind; often used to treat emotional problems.

rows: Fever at night, mental confusion, incoherent speech or aphasia, delirium, body hot but hands/feet cold, macules

Stuffy/uncomfortable, dull-aching chest, slight shortness of breath, palpitations, anxiety, insomnia, dizziness, dream-disturbed sleep, poor memory, easily startled, dull-pale complexion, pale lips, cold hands, scanty periods/amenorrhea

Palpitations, tight/hot chest, slight chest ache, rapid breathing, thirst, mouth/tongue ulcers, mental restlessness, agitation, feeling of heat, insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep, red face, bitter taste, heavy periods
Red, redder swollen tip with red points, yellow coating

Palpitations, chest oppression/heat/pain, rapid breathing, thirst, red face, bitter taste, phlegm expectoration, rattling throat, mental restlessness, insomnia, agitation, incoherent speech, mental confusion, rash behaviour, uncontrolled laughter/crying, manic behaviour
Red, swollen, yellow dry sticky coating, deep Heart crack

Chest distension & slight pain, chest tightness, slight shortness of breath, sighing, lump-in-throat feeling, palpitations, depression, irritability, poor appetite, weak cold limbs, slightly purple lips, pale complexion
Slightly pale-purple on sides, chest area
Empty but slightly overflowing on left front position

Palpitations, stabbing/pricking chest pain radiating to inner left arm/shoulder, oppression/constriction of chest, shortness of breath, cyanotic lips/nails, cold hands, painful periods with dark clots

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Stomach
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Controls receiving; controls 'rotting & ripening' of food (origin of Qi & Blood via Food-Qi/Gu Qi); controls transportation of food essences; controls the descending of Qi; is the origin of Body Fluids.
Diet (nature of food, regularity of meal times, conditions of eating), emotional strain, exterior pathogenic factors.

rows: Uncomfortable epigastrium, no appetite, poor taste sensation, loose stools, tiredness (esp. morning), weak limbs
Dull epigastric pain/discomfort, better after eating & with pressure/massage, no appetite, prefers warm food/drink, vomiting clear fluid, no thirst, cold weak limbs, tiredness, pale complexion

No central coating or rootless coating, normal colour
Epigastric pain & distension, belching, nausea, vomiting, hiccups, irritability

All Stomach-Heat signs plus chest/epigastric oppression, mucus in stools, phlegm expectoration, mental derangement
Sudden severe epigastric pain, feeling cold, cold limbs, prefers warmth, vomiting clear fluid (may relieve pain), nausea, worse after cold fluids (quickly vomited)
Nausea, difficulty swallowing, belching, vomiting, hiccups
Epigastric fullness & pain, heaviness, facial pain, blocked nose/thick sticky discharge, thirst without desire to drink, nausea, feeling of heat, dull-yellow complexion, sticky taste
Retention of Food in the Stomach

Severe stabbing epigastric pain (worse at night), dislike of pressure, nausea, vomiting (possibly blood or 'coffee-ground' material)
Derives from Qi stagnation, Cold, or Heat

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Small Intestine
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Receives & transforms food/drink from the Stomach, separating a clear part (to the Spleen) from a turbid part (to the Large Intestine & Bladder). Separates fluids — clear part to the Bladder as urine (aided by Kidney-Yang), turbid part to the Large Intestine for reabsorption/excretion. Closely related to the Heart.
Diet, emotional strain.

rows: Mental restlessness, insomnia, tongue/mouth ulcers, sore throat, deafness, uncomfortable hot chest, abdominal pain, thirst for cold liquids, scanty dark urine, burning painful urination, blood in urine
Red, redder swollen tip, yellow coating
Twisting lower-abdominal pain radiating to the back, abdominal distension, dislike of abdominal pressure, borborygmi, flatulence, pain relieved by passing wind, testicular pain
Severe abdominal pain, dislike of pressure, abdominal distension, constipation, vomiting, borborygmi, flatulence

Abdominal pain/distension, bad taste, sallow complexion; roundworms — vomiting of worms, cold limbs; hookworms — pica (soil, wax, uncooked rice/tea leaves); pinworms — itchy anus, worse in evening; tapeworms — constant hunger
Dull abdominal pain relieved by pressure, desire for hot drinks, borborygmi, diarrhea, pale abundant urination, cold limbs

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Large Intestine
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Gall Bladder
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation (1) & (2).pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Stores & excretes bile (received from the Liver); Minister Fire warms the Gall Bladder, which transmits heat to aid digestion; bile flow depends on free-flowing Liver-Qi; GB-Qi assists ascending Liver-Qi. Controls decisiveness (decision-making, which supports the other 11 organs), controls the sinews (Metal aspect note in source).
Diet, emotional strain, exterior pathogenic factors.

acu-pattern.title: Zang-Fu Patterns — Bladder

items.detail: Removes water via Qi transformation — receives clear fluids from the Small Intestine and transforms them into urine for excretion; the Qi and heat for this transformation are provided by Kidney-Yang.
Exterior pathogens, emotional strain, excessive sexual activity, excessive physical exercise.

Thick sticky yellow coating on root with red spots

Frequent pale abundant urination, incontinence, enuresis, lower backache, dizziness, nocturia, white urethral discharge, feeling cold
Excessive sexual activity, exterior pathogens, excessive physical exercise

Uncomfortable bladder pressure/pain, urinary frequency & urgency, burning sensation, urinary hesitancy, suprapubic pain/distension, occasional slight incontinence, nocturia. Liver-Qi stagnation type: significant pain, less frequency. Kidney deficiency type: less suprapubic pain but more frequency

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — 12 Regular Channels
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation-3.pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: The oldest of the pattern-identification methods; distinguishes symptoms/signs by involved channel rather than organ.

The organ and its channel form a unity but are also energetically separate. Channels pertain to the Exterior (skin & muscles); organs pertain to the Interior (organs & bones). Channel problems can occur without affecting the organ, and vice versa (e.g. shoulder pain).

(1) Invasion of exterior pathogenic factors (Cold, Wind, Dampness) — pathogen enters the Luo (Connecting) channels first, then the main channels, settling in the joints → Painful Obstruction Syndrome (Bi Syndrome). (2) Disruption of Qi & Blood circulation at the joints. (3) Overuse of a limb/body part → local Qi stagnation. (4) Sports injuries → local Qi stagnation. Internal Organ disharmonies can also cause channel problems.

Some organ symptoms may appear and can safely be ignored if not applicable. Channel problems can affect orifices/sense organs unrelated to the Internal Organs. Diagnosis requires thorough knowledge of channel pathways. Any pain, numbness, stiffness, tingling or ache along a channel's course is a channel symptom of that channel.

blocks.heading: Full vs Empty along the channel

rows: Intense pain, stiffness, contractions, cramps. Red colour & hot to the touch = Heat; bluish colour = Cold.

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — 8 Extraordinary Vessels
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation-3.pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

items.detail: Act as reservoirs of energy for the main channels — absorb overflow of excess Qi from the main channels and transfer Qi back when necessary. Warm the organs internally and irrigate the space between skin and muscles externally.

Interact with the 12 regular channels but have no internal/external organ relationship with them. Have no points of their own (except Governing & Directing Vessels) — borrow points from the 12 regular channels.
Conduits of pre-natal Yuan Qi, derived from Kidney essence. Qi does not flow in one direction but ebbs and flows like the tides (exception: Governing & Directing Vessels).

Named after their special function, unlike the 12 regular channels, whose names relate to organ systems and Qi functions. Each has a master point and a couple point that open/activate two regular channels and treat both that channel's indications and the extraordinary-vessel indications.

rows: Sea of the Yang channels — regulates Qi of all Yang channels

items.detail: Originates from the Shang Han Lun ('Discussion of Cold-induced Diseases').

Systematically describes the pathology and treatment of diseases caused by invasion of exterior pathogenic factors, focused first on external conditions and progressing to internal conditions as disease deepens.

acu-pattern.title: Pattern Differentiation — 4 Levels
acu-pattern.source: T120 - TCM Pattern Differentiation-3.pdf — guide by Hengameh Keshvari, Summer 2023

blocks.heading: Overview — School of Warm Diseases (溫病學派)

items.detail: Clinically the most useful tool for interpreting the pathology and establishing treatment of diseases deriving from invasion of Wind-Heat.
All manifest with fever; pathogen enters through the nose and mouth; infectious; rapid pathological development; strong tendency to injure Yin.

All Warm Diseases are caused by invasion of Wind-Heat, but not every Wind-Heat invasion is a Warm Disease. Warm Diseases are more virulent/infectious, characterized by more intense Heat, develop rapidly, and strongly tend to injure Yin. Examples: influenza, measles, chicken pox, mumps, SARS, COVID-19.

blocks.heading: Defensive Qi Level (Wei, 衛分) — beginning stage, the only exterior level

items.detail: Yin deficiency present. Heat affects the Blood → bleeding (macules under the skin). Heat affects the Mind → delirium or coma. Internal Wind may develop → convulsions and tremors. Collapse of Yin or Yang may occur.

acu-safety.title: Contraindicated & Caution Points — By Anatomical Risk

blocks.heading: ⚠️ Important Note
blocks.lead: This section is an exam-prep summary. For actual clinical practice, always follow the official Clean Needle Technique (CNT) manual and your province's regulatory college safety handbook as the final authority. The content here summarizes standard infection-control principles and does not replace any institution's official guidance.

blocks.heading: Pneumothorax Risk (Thoracic/Shoulder Points)
blocks.lead: Points overlying the lung apex or pleura — deep or medially-angled insertion risks pneumothorax.

blocks.heading: Full Bladder Caution (Lower Abdominal Points)

blocks.lead: Points classically avoided in pregnancy — strong stimulation can provoke uterine contraction. Some (e.g. BL67) are used therapeutically via moxa for breech correction but are otherwise avoided.

Perpendicular, deep insertion is intentional for sciatica but requires precise landmark location to avoid direct nerve trauma; elicit De Qi radiating down the leg, not sharp shooting pain

Deep/lateral misdirection near the elbow can affect the radial nerve — keep to standard perpendicular depth
Both sit near the ulnar nerve groove at the elbow — shallow, precise insertion; sharp electric sensation into the fingers signals nerve contact, withdraw slightly
Deep/misdirected insertion at the wrist can affect the median nerve (carpal tunnel region) — moderate depth only
ST6 (頰車), ST7 (下關), GB2 (聽會), TE17 (翳風)

rows: Palpate and avoid the artery directly; shallow perpendicular insertion at ST9

acu-safety.title: Infection Control — Dilutions, Spills & Sharps
acu-safety.source: Compiled from standard infection-control principles (WHO hand hygiene guidance, standard bloodborne pathogen protocols) — see the note above regarding official references

blocks.heading: ⚠️ Important Note
blocks.lead: This section is an exam-prep summary. For actual clinical practice, always follow the official Clean Needle Technique (CNT) manual and your province's regulatory college safety handbook as the final authority. The content here summarizes standard infection-control principles and does not replace any institution's official guidance.

rows: 1:10 (~5000 ppm) — stronger dilution for blood spills
Leave wet on the surface for the time stated on the product label (commonly 1–10 minutes) before wiping
70% — used undiluted (see below for why 70% specifically)

blocks.heading: Why 70% Alcohol (Not Higher)
blocks.lead: A common exam trap: higher percentage is not more effective. Concentrations near 99% evaporate too fast to keep enough contact time with microbes, and pure alcohol coagulates surface proteins on bacteria too quickly, forming a protective barrier that blocks penetration into the cell. The ~30% water in a 70% solution slows evaporation and allows the alcohol to penetrate the cell wall before denaturing proteins inside — giving better overall germicidal effect.

rows: Swab from the center of the insertion point outward in a spiral motion
Never reuse a swab on a second pass or second site — one swab, one use
Put on gloves before any contact with the spill
Absorb the fluid with paper towel or another absorbent material
Disinfect the area with a 1:10 bleach dilution, allowing full contact time
Dispose of contaminated materials in a biohazard bag — never in general waste
Never touch a blood/body fluid spill with bare hands; restrict others from the area until cleaned
Puncture-proof, labeled biohazard sharps container (typically yellow)
Dispose of the needle immediately after use — do not set it down first
Never recap a used needle — this is the single most common cause of needlestick injury
Replace/seal the container once it reaches about ¾ full
Collected by a licensed medical/biohazard waste service — never in regular trash
Contact with bleeding, open wounds, or bloodletting (瀉血) techniques
Routine single-use needle insertion on intact skin
Gloves not strictly required by most standards, but hand hygiene before/after is mandatory regardless
Remove gloves properly and perform hand hygiene immediately after
Before a clean/aseptic procedure (e.g. needle insertion)
After exposure to body fluid risk
After touching a patient's surroundings (treatment table, linens)

acu-safety.title: Clinical Practice Safety — Equipment, Reactions & Special Settings
acu-safety.source: Compiled from standard infection-control and clinical safety principles (Spaulding classification, standard bloodborne pathogen exposure protocols) — not transcribed from any single regulatory handbook

blocks.heading: ⚠️ Important Note
blocks.lead: This section is an exam-prep summary. For actual clinical practice, always follow the official Clean Needle Technique (CNT) manual and your province's regulatory college safety handbook as the final authority. The content here summarizes standard infection-control principles and does not replace any institution's official guidance.

blocks.heading: Equipment Reprocessing Categories (Spaulding Classification)
blocks.lead: How an item must be cleaned between patients depends on what it touches — not all instruments need the same level of reprocessing.

rows: Single-use disposable, or sterile (steam autoclave) if reusable — never reused across patients without sterilization
Contacts mucous membranes or non-intact skin, no penetration
Cupping glass rims, gua sha tools, ear-seed applicators, moxa boxes touching skin
TENS/e-stim pads and leads, treatment table surface, tuina tools not breaking skin, massage tools
Low-level disinfection (hospital-grade surface disinfectant) between patients

blocks.heading: Needlestick / Blood Exposure Protocol
blocks.lead: A structured 4-step response — speed matters, especially for post-exposure prophylaxis timing.

rows: Stop treatment. Do not squeeze or suck the wound. Wash gently with soap and water under running water, encouraging natural bleeding. Cover with a waterproof dressing.
Identify the source patient. Assess bloodborne pathogen risk factors. Discuss and, where possible, obtain consent for source-patient testing (Hepatitis B/C, HIV).
Refer the exposed person to a physician, urgent care, or occupational health immediately — post-exposure prophylaxis for HIV is time-sensitive (ideally within 2 hours, no later than 72 hours).
Complete an incident report: date, time, circumstances, actions taken, and follow-up plan. Report to the regulatory college as required by their bylaws.

blocks.heading: Adverse Reactions & Side Effects — Beyond Needle Shock

rows: Bruising/mild bleeding at needle site, localized soreness, drowsiness or fatigue after treatment, transient flare of symptoms
Reassure, document; usually self-resolving within a day
Vasovagal fainting (needle shock), dizziness, mild nausea
Stop treatment, remove needles, position patient safely (legs elevated), monitor until resolved — see Techniques section for full protocol
Pneumothorax, nerve injury, infection at needle site, injury from a fall during syncope
Stop treatment immediately, assess severity, refer/call emergency services as needed, document thoroughly, report to the college

Expected: temporary skin discoloration/marks. Risk: blistering from excessive suction or heat (fire cupping), burns from the flame. Avoid over broken skin, varicose veins, or the pregnant abdomen/lumbosacral area. Caution/avoid with bleeding disorders.

Burn risk with direct moxa — always monitor sensation. Ensure adequate ventilation for smoke (respiratory patients, allergies). Avoid on insensate skin, the face, or near the eyes. Caution with certain points in pregnancy (lower abdomen/lumbosacral). Fire-safety awareness in clinic (smoke detectors, ventilation).

Contraindicated with cardiac pacemakers or other implanted electronic devices. Never pass current directly across the chest/heart. Avoid over the carotid sinus. Start at low intensity and titrate up. Caution in patients with a seizure history. Never leave a patient unattended while connected.

blocks.heading: Treatment Outside the Standard Clinic (Home Visits, Community Settings)
blocks.lead: Same safety standard applies wherever treatment happens — the setting doesn't lower the bar.

rows: Bring a portable sharps container and sterile single-use supplies — same standard as the clinic
Adequate lighting, privacy, and space to needle and monitor the patient safely
Know the nearest emergency service; carry basic emergency supplies
Same informed consent and charting standards as an in-clinic visit

blocks.heading: Patient Privacy & Draping (brief)
blocks.lead: A quick-reference summary, not a full policy — check your college's professional conduct standards for the complete requirements.

rows: Explain the area to be treated and get verbal consent before draping or exposing it
Drape/gown so only the area being treated is exposed — cover everything else
Offer a chaperone, especially for treatment of sensitive areas or cross-gender treatment
Private, secure treatment space; maintain professional boundaries throughout

herb-single.title: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Wind-Cold (辛溫解表藥)
herb-single.source: Compiled from standard TCM Materia Medica references (Bensky-style categorization)

blocks.heading: Acrid, Warm Herbs that Release the Exterior
blocks.lead: These herbs are acrid and warm in nature; they induce sweating to disperse Wind-Cold from the exterior. Used for Wind-Cold exterior patterns (chills, fever, no/little sweating, headache, body aches).

Releases the exterior, disperses Cold; regulates Qi in the Middle Jiao; harmonizes the Stomach

5–9g. Do not decoct for long (add near the end)
Releases the exterior, disperses Cold; warms the Middle Jiao, stops vomiting; warms the Lung, stops coughing
Wind-Cold exterior (mild), vomiting ("the sacred remedy for vomiting"), cough with thin phlegm
3–9g (or larger for fresh use); moderates toxicity of other herbs
Releases the exterior, expels Wind; stops bleeding when charred
Wind-Cold or Wind-Heat exterior (mild, either), skin rashes/itching, early-stage sores

herb-single.source: Compiled from standard TCM Materia Medica references

blocks.heading: Open the Orifices, Revive Consciousness
blocks.lead: Used for sudden loss of consciousness (Wind-stroke, high fever, seizure) from orifice-blockage.

rows: Opens the orifices, revives consciousness; invigorates blood, dispels stasis; reduces swelling, relieves pain

Spleen-Qi deficiency, cough, abdominal/limb spasm-pain, harmonizes formulas, drug/food toxicity
2–9g (larger for toxicity). Caution: dampness/edema; incompatible with Gan Sui/Da Ji/Yuan Hua/Hai Zao
Tonifies Spleen/Lung/Kidney Qi and Yin; astringes

Tonifies the Spleen, transforms dampness; resolves summerheat

Tonifies the Spleen, nourishes Qi; nourishes Blood, calms the Spirit; moderates harsh herbs
Spleen-Qi deficiency, Blood deficiency, used to buffer harsh/toxic formulas

herb-single.title: Tonifying Herbs — Tonify Blood (補血藥)

herb-single.title: Astringent Herbs (收澀藥)
herb-single.source: Compiled from standard TCM Materia Medica references

blocks.heading: Astringent Herbs
blocks.lead: Used for chronic, unconsolidated leakage — sweating, diarrhea, seminal emission, urinary frequency, and excessive discharge — always in conjunction with treating the underlying deficiency.

herb-single.title: Herbs for Topical / External Use (外用藥)
herb-single.source: Compiled from standard TCM Materia Medica references

blocks.heading: Topical / External-Use Herbs
blocks.lead: Applied externally for sores, skin disease, and toxic swellings — rarely taken internally due to toxicity.

rows: Resolves toxicity, kills parasites (topical); dries dampness, dispels phlegm
Topical only — powder or ointment. Internal use highly restricted; contains arsenic sulfide, caution with liver/kidney function

백두옹탕 白頭翁湯 (Pulsatilla Decoction)
Bai Tou Weng, Huang Lian, Huang Bai, Qin Pi

청호별갑탕 青蒿鱉甲湯 (Artemisia Annua and Soft-shelled Turtle Shell Decoction)
Qing Hao, Bie Jia, Sheng Di Huang, Zhi Mu, Mu Dan Pi

맥미지황환 麥味地黃丸 (Ophiopogonis, Schisandra, and Rehmannia Pill)
Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Mai Men Dong + Wu Wei Zi
Nourishes Kidney/Lung Yin; astringes the Lung
Kidney-Lung Yin deficiency — chronic dry cough, wheeze, dry mouth/throat

Zuǒguī Wán
좌귀환 左歸丸 (Restore the Left Kidney Pill)
Liu Wei Di Huang Wan minus Ze Xie/Mu Dan Pi, plus Gou Qi Zi, Niu Xi, Lu Jiao Jiao, Gui Ban Jiao, Tu Si Zi
Strongly nourishes Kidney Yin and Essence, without draining
Severe Kidney-Yin/Essence deficiency — soreness, seminal emission, night sweating, thirst

Zuǒguī Yǐn
좌귀음 左歸飲 (Restore the Left Kidney Decoction)
Shu Di Huang, Shan Yao, Gou Qi Zi, Zhi Gan Cao, Fu Ling, Shan Zhu Yu
Gently nourishes Kidney Yin — milder decoction form of Zuo Gui Wan
Milder Kidney-Yin deficiency — dizziness, dry mouth, weak back, night sweating

Dàbǔ Yuánjiān
대보원전 大補元煎 (Great Tonify the Primal Decoction)
Ren Shen, Shan Yao, Shu Di Huang, Du Zhong, Gou Qi Zi, Dang Gui, Shan Zhu Yu, Zhi Gan Cao
Tonifies Qi and Kidney; nourishes Essence and Blood
Severe primal (Yuan) Qi-Kidney deficiency — extreme fatigue, weak back/knees, near-collapse states

Dàbǔyīn Wán
대보음환 大補陰丸 (Great Tonify the Yin Pill)
Shu Di Huang, Gui Ban, Huang Bai, Zhi Mu, (Zhu Ji Sui/honey)
Severe Yin deficiency with fire flaring — steaming bone fever, night sweating, seminal emission, cough with blood

Hǔqián Wán
호잠환 虎潛丸 (Hidden Tiger Pill)
Huang Bai, Zhi Mu, Shu Di Huang, Gui Ban, Suo Yang(or Hu Gu sub.), Chen Pi, Bai Shao, Gan Jiang
Nourishes Yin, drains fire; strengthens sinew/bone
Kidney-Liver Yin deficiency with weak sinew/bone — atrophy disorder (痿證), weak legs unable to stand

Yīguàn Jiān
일관전 一貫煎 (Linking Decoction)
Sha Shen, Mai Men Dong, Dang Gui, Sheng Di Huang, Gou Qi Zi, Chuan Lian Zi
Nourishes Yin, nourishes the Liver; soothes Qi stagnation
Liver-Yin deficiency with Qi stagnation — hypochondriac pain, dry mouth/throat, acid regurgitation

Èrzhì Wán
이지환 二至丸 (Two Solstice Pill)
Nu Zhen Zi, Han Lian Cao
Mild Liver-Kidney Yin deficiency — dizziness, premature graying, lower back soreness

Bǎihé Gùjīn Tāng
백합고금탕 百合固金湯 (Lily Bulb Decoction to Preserve the Metal)
Bai He, Shu Di Huang, Sheng Di Huang, Mai Men Dong, Bei Mu, Xuan Shen, Dang Gui, Bai Shao, Jie Geng, Gan Cao
Nourishes Lung/Kidney Yin; stops cough, transforms phlegm
Lung/Kidney Yin deficiency with dry cough, blood-streaked sputum, sore throat

Yuèhuā Wán
월화환 月華丸 (Moonlight Pill)
Sha Shen, Mai Men Dong, Tian Men Dong, Shu Di Huang, Sheng Di Huang, E Jiao, Bai Bu, Bie Jia, Fu Ling, Shan Yao, Sang Ye, Ju Hua, Xuan Shen
Nourishes Yin, moistens the Lung; stops cough, stops bleeding
Chronic Lung-Yin deficiency (TB-type pattern) — dry cough with blood, night sweating, emaciation

Shíhú Yèguāng Wán
석곡야광환 石斛夜光丸 (Dendrobium Pill for Night Vision)
Shi Hu, Ren Shen, Fu Ling, Shu Di Huang, Sheng Di Huang, Tian Men Dong, Mai Men Dong, Gou Qi Zi, Ju Hua, Niu Xi, Chuan Xiong, Wu Wei Zi, (and others)
Nourishes Liver/Kidney Yin; brightens the eyes
Liver-Kidney Yin deficiency with visual decline — blurred vision, cataracts, night blindness

Liángdì Tāng
양지탕 兩地湯 (Rehmannia and Lycia Radicis Decoction)
Sheng Di Huang, Xuan Shen, Mai Men Dong, Di Gu Pi, E Jiao, Bai Shao
Nourishes Yin, clears heat; cools the blood
Yin deficiency with heat causing early/scanty menstruation with heat signs

Yīyīn Jiān
일음전 一陰煎 (One-Yin Decoction)
Sheng Di Huang, Shu Di Huang, Bai Shao, Mai Men Dong, Zhi Gan Cao, Niu Xi, Zhi Mu
Yin deficiency with mild heat — dry mouth, low fever, irregular menstruation

Jīnkuì Shènqì Wán
금궤신기환 金匱腎氣丸 (Kidney Qi Pill from the Golden Cabinet)
Liu Wei Di Huang Wan + Fu Zi + Rou Gui
Gently warms and tonifies Kidney Yang
Kidney-Yang deficiency — cold lower back/legs, frequent clear urination, mild edema

Jìshēng Shènqì Wán
제생신기환 濟生腎氣丸 (Kidney Qi Pill from Formulas that Aid the Living)
Jin Gui Shen Qi Wan + Che Qian Zi + Niu Xi
Warms Kidney Yang; promotes urination to reduce edema
Kidney-Yang deficiency with more pronounced edema/difficult urination

Wúbǐ Shānyào Wán
무비산약환 無比山藥丸 (Incomparable Dioscorea Pill)
Shan Yao, Rou Cong Rong, Shu Di Huang, Shan Zhu Yu, Fu Ling, Ze Xie, Wu Wei Zi, Tu Si Zi, Du Zhong, Niu Xi, Ba Ji Tian, Chi Shi Zhi
Tonifies Kidney Yang and Essence; astringes
Kidney-Yang/Essence deficiency — seminal emission, weak back/knees, tinnitus, chronic diarrhea

Yòuguī Wán
우귀환 右歸丸 (Restore the Right Kidney Pill)
Shu Di Huang, Shan Yao, Shan Zhu Yu, Gou Qi Zi, Du Zhong, Tu Si Zi, Fu Zi, Rou Gui, Dang Gui, Lu Jiao Jiao
Strongly warms and tonifies Kidney Yang and Essence
Severe Kidney-Yang/Essence deficiency — impotence, infertility, cold limbs, weak back/knees

Yòuguī Yǐn
우귀음 右歸飲 (Restore the Right Kidney Decoction)
Shu Di Huang, Shan Yao, Shan Zhu Yu, Gou Qi Zi, Du Zhong, Fu Zi, Rou Gui, Zhi Gan Cao
Warms and tonifies Kidney Yang — milder decoction form of You Gui Wan
Milder Kidney-Yang deficiency — cold limbs, weak back, frequent urination

Gùshèn Wán
고신환 固腎丸 (Restore the Kidneys Pill)
Sha Yuan Ji Li, Tu Si Zi, Bu Gu Zhi, Rou Cong Rong, Hu Tao Rou, Wu Wei Zi, Lian Xu
Warms and stabilizes the Kidney, secures Essence
Kidney deficiency with unstable Essence — seminal emission, frequent urination, weak knees

Bǔshèn Gùchōng Tāng
보신고충탕 補腎固沖湯 (Kidney-Tonifying and Penetrating Vessel Consolidation Decoction)
Ju Sheng Zi, Xu Duan, Bu Gu Zhi, Tu Si Zi, Dang Gui, Bai Shao, Shan Zhu Yu, Ren Shen, Bai Zhu, Sha Ren, Gan Cao, Da Zao
Tonifies the Kidney, stabilizes the Chong/Ren; calms the fetus
Kidney deficiency with restless fetus/habitual miscarriage

Dìhuáng Yǐnzǐ
지황음자 地黃飲子 (Rehmannia Drink)
Shu Di Huang, Ba Ji Tian, Shan Zhu Yu, Shi Hu, Rou Cong Rong, Fu Zi, Rou Gui, Wu Wei Zi, Fu Ling, Mai Men Dong, Shi Chang Pu, Yuan Zhi
Tonifies Kidney Yin and Yang; opens the orifices, transforms Phlegm
Kidney Yin-Yang deficiency with Phlegm obstructing the orifices — post-stroke aphasia, stiff tongue/limbs

Guīlù Èrxiān Jiāo
귀록이선교 龜鹿二仙膠 (Tortoise Shell and Deer Antler Two-Immortal Syrup)
Gui Ban, Lu Jiao, Ren Shen, Gou Qi Zi
Strongly tonifies Kidney Yin and Yang; fills Essence, nourishes Blood
Severe Essence-Blood-Yin-Yang deficiency — infertility, extreme fatigue, weak back/knees, poor vision

Èrxiān Tāng
이선탕 二仙湯 (Two Immortal Decoction)
Xian Mao, Yin Yang Huo (Xian Ling Pi), Ba Ji Tian, Dang Gui, Huang Bai, Zhi Mu

Kidney Yin-Yang deficiency with deficiency fire — menopausal syndrome, hot flushes with cold signs

herb-formula.title: [9] Stabilize & Bind (固澀)
herb-formula.source: Structured to match the official 287 Pan-Canadian Formulas exam map (HB Kim, AcupunctureMedia.com)

rows:
Mǔlì Sǎn
모려산 牡蠣散 (Oyster Shell Powder)
Mu Li, Huang Qi, Ma Huang Gen, Fu Xiao Mai
Astringes sweating; tonifies Qi, stabilizes the exterior
Spontaneous/night sweating from Qi/Yang deficiency with unstable exterior

Jiǔxiān Sǎn

Ying Su Ke, Wu Mei, Wu Wei Zi, Sang Bai Pi, Jie Geng, Ren Shen, Fu Ling, E Jiao, Bei Mu
Astringes the Lung, stops cough; tonifies Qi
Chronic Lung-deficient cough — long-standing cough with sweating, shortness of breath

Zhēnrén Yǎngzàng Tāng
진인양장탕 真人養臟湯 (True Man's Decoction to Nourish the Organs)
Ren Shen, Bai Zhu, Rou Dou Kou, Rou Gui, He Zi, Ying Su Ke, Mu Xiang, Bai Shao, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao
Warms and tonifies the Spleen/Kidney; astringes the intestines, stops diarrhea
Chronic Spleen-Kidney Yang deficient diarrhea/dysentery — long-standing loose stools, rectal prolapse

Táohuā Tāng
도화탕 桃花湯 (Peach Blossom Decoction)
Chi Shi Zhi, Gan Jiang, Geng Mi
Warms the Middle, astringes the intestines to stop diarrhea
Spleen-Kidney Yang deficiency dysentery — chronic diarrhea with undigested food/blood, cold limbs

Sìshén Wán
사신환 四神丸 (Four-Miracle Pill)
Bu Gu Zhi, Rou Dou Kou, Wu Zhu Yu, Wu Wei Zi (with Sheng Jiang, Da Zao)
Warms and tonifies Spleen/Kidney; astringes the intestines, stops diarrhea
Kidney-Yang deficiency diarrhea — classic "5 AM diarrhea", cold limbs

Jīnsuǒ Gùjīng Wán
금쇄고정환 金鎖固精丸 (Metal Lock Pill to Stabilize the Essence)
Sha Yuan Ji Li, Qian Shi, Lian Xu, Long Gu, Mu Li
Kidney deficiency with unstable Essence — seminal emission, spermatorrhea, weak back/knees

Sāngpiāoxiāo Sǎn
상표소산 桑螵蛸散 (Mantis Egg-case Powder)
Sang Piao Xiao, Long Gu, Gui Ban, Ren Shen, Fu Shen, Dang Gui, Yuan Zhi, Shi Chang Pu
Tonifies the Heart and Kidney; stabilizes Essence, stops enuresis
Heart-Kidney deficiency — frequent/cloudy urination, enuresis, poor memory

Suōquán Wán
축천환 縮泉丸 (Restrict the Fountain Pill)
Yi Zhi Ren, Wu Yao, Shan Yao
Warms the Kidney, stabilizes reduction of urination
Kidney-Yang deficiency with frequent urination — enuresis, frequent clear urination, esp. in children/elderly

Shǒutāi Wán
수태환 壽胎丸 (Fetus Longevity Pill)
Tu Si Zi, Sang Ji Sheng, Xu Duan, E Jiao
Tonifies the Kidney, stabilizes the Chong/Ren; calms a restless fetus
Kidney deficiency with restless fetus/habitual miscarriage — threatened miscarriage, lower back soreness

Gùchōng Tāng
고충탕 固沖湯 (Stabilize Gushing Decoction)
Bai Zhu, Huang Qi, Long Gu, Mu Li, Shan Zhu Yu, Bai Shao, Hai Piao Xiao, Qian Cao Gen, Wu Zei Gu, Wu Bei Zi
Tonifies Qi, stabilizes the Chong; astringes to stop bleeding
Spleen-Qi deficiency with unstable Chong Mai — profuse/continuous uterine bleeding

Gùjīng Wán
고경환 固經丸 (Stabilize the Menses Pill)
Gui Ban, Bai Shao, Huang Qin, Huang Bai, Chun Gen Pi, Xiang Fu
Nourishes Yin, clears heat; stabilizes the Chong, stops bleeding
Yin deficiency with heat causing uterine bleeding — heavy irregular bleeding with heat signs

Wándài Tāng
완대탕 完帶湯 (End Discharge Decoction)
Bai Zhu, Shan Yao, Ren Shen, Bai Shao, Cang Zhu, Che Qian Zi, Gan Cao, Chen Pi, Jing Jie Sui, Chai Hu
Tonifies the Spleen, dries Dampness; soothes the Liver, stops discharge
Spleen-deficient Damp leukorrhea — profuse thin white/clear discharge, fatigue, loose stools

Yìhuáng Tāng
역황탕 易黃湯 (Change Yellow Decoction)
Shan Yao, Qian Shi, Huang Bai, Che Qian Zi, Bai Guo
Tonifies the Spleen/Kidney, clears damp-heat, stops discharge
Damp-heat with Kidney deficiency leukorrhea — thick yellow, foul-smelling discharge

Zhǐdài Fāng
지대방 止帶方 (Discharge-Stopping Formula)
Zhu Ling, Fu Ling, Che Qian Zi, Ze Xie, Chi Shao, Dan Pi, Huang Bai, Zhi Zi, Niu Xi, Yin Chen Hao, Ku Shen
Damp-heat leukorrhea — profuse yellow discharge with odor, vulvar itching

herb-formula.title: [10] Calm Spirit (安神)
herb-formula.source: Structured to match the official 287 Pan-Canadian Formulas exam map (HB Kim, AcupunctureMedia.com)

rows:
Tiānwáng Bǔxīn Dān
천왕보심단 天王補心丹 (Emperor of Heaven's Special Pill to Tonify the Heart)

Heart-Kidney Yin-Blood deficiency — insomnia, palpitations, forgetfulness, dry mouth, constipation

Bǎizǐ Yǎngxīn Wán
백자양심환 柏子養心丸 (Arborvitae Seed Pill to Nourish the Heart)
Bai Zi Ren, Gou Qi Zi, Mai Men Dong, Dang Gui, Shi Chang Pu, Fu Shen, Xuan Shen, Shu Di Huang, Gan Cao
Nourishes the Heart, calms the Spirit; nourishes Blood
Heart-Blood deficiency — palpitations, insomnia, forgetfulness, anxiety

Suānzǎorén Tāng
산조인탕 酸棗仁湯 (Sour Jujube Decoction)
Suan Zao Ren, Fu Ling, Zhi Mu, Chuan Xiong, Gan Cao
Nourishes Blood, calms the Spirit; clears heat, relieves irritability
Liver-Blood deficiency with heat — insomnia, irritability, palpitations, night sweating

Kǒngshèng Zhènzhōng Dān
공성침중단 孔聖枕中丹 (Special Pill from Confucius' Pillow)
Gui Ban, Long Gu, Yuan Zhi, Shi Chang Pu
Nourishes the Heart/Kidney; calms the Spirit, sharpens the memory
Heart-Kidney deficiency with poor memory/insomnia — forgetfulness, distractibility, restless sleep

Gānmài Dàzǎo Tāng
감맥대조탕 甘麥大棗湯 (Licorice, Wheat, and Jujube Decoction)
Zhi Gan Cao, Fu Xiao Mai, Da Zao
Nourishes the Heart, calms the Spirit; harmonizes the Middle, relieves urgency

Ānshén Dìngzhì Wán
안신정지환 安神定志丸 (Calm the Shen and Settle the Emotion Pill)
Ren Shen, Fu Ling, Fu Shen, Yuan Zhi, Shi Chang Pu, Long Chi
Tonifies the Heart and Gallbladder Qi; calms the Spirit
Heart-Gallbladder Qi deficiency (timidity) — easily startled, anxiety, insomnia, palpitations

Zhūshā Ānshén Wán
주사안신환 硃砂安神丸 (Cinnabar Pill to Calm the Spirit)
Zhu Sha(mineral, small amount, caution), Huang Lian, Sheng Di Huang, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao
Clears heat, calms the Spirit; nourishes Yin, tonifies Blood

Cízhū Wán
자주환 磁朱丸 (Magnetite and Cinnabar Pill)
Ci Shi, Zhu Sha(caution), Shen Qu
Settles and calms the Spirit; subdues rising Yang; brightens the eyes
Liver-Yang/Heart-fire disturbance — insomnia, palpitations, tinnitus, blurred vision

herb-formula.title: [11] Regulate Qi (理氣)
herb-formula.source: Structured to match the official 287 Pan-Canadian Formulas exam map (HB Kim, AcupunctureMedia.com)

rows:
Yuèjú Wán
월국환 越鞠丸 (Escape Restraint Pill)
Xiang Fu, Cang Zhu, Chuan Xiong, Zhi Zi, Shen Qu
Moves Qi to resolve the "6 stagnations" (Qi, Blood, Phlegm, Fire, Damp, Food)
Combined stagnation — chest/epigastric distension, belching, poor appetite

Cháihú Shūgān Sǎn
시호소간산 柴胡疏肝散 (Bupleurum Powder to Spread the Liver)
Chai Hu, Chen Pi, Chuan Xiong, Xiang Fu, Zhi Ke, Bai Shao, Gan Cao

Zhǐshí Xièbái Guìzhī Tāng
지실해백계지탕 枳實薤白桂枝湯 (Unripe Bitter Orange, Chinese Garlic, and Cinnamon Twig Decoction)
Zhi Shi, Xie Bai, Gua Lou, Hou Po, Gui Zhi
Opens the chest Yang, breaks Qi stagnation; transforms Phlegm
Chest-Bi (angina-type) from Phlegm obstructing chest Yang — chest oppression/pain radiating to back

Gualóu Xièbái Báijiǔ Tāng
과루해백백주탕 瓜蔞薤白白酒湯 (Trichosanthes Fruit, Chinese Garlic, and Wine Decoction)
Gua Lou, Xie Bai, Bai Jiu(rice wine)
Opens the chest Yang, disperses Phlegm-stagnation

Gualóu Xièbái Bànxià Tāng
과루해백반하탕 瓜蔞薤白半夏湯 (Trichosanthes Fruit, Chinese Garlic, and Pinellia Decoction)

More severe chest-Bi with copious phlegm — cannot lie flat, severe chest oppression

Bànxià Hòupò Tāng
반하후박탕 半夏厚朴湯 (Pinellia and Magnolia Bark Decoction)
Ban Xia, Hou Po, Fu Ling, Sheng Jiang, Zi Su Ye
Moves Qi, transforms Phlegm; softens nodulation

Hòupò Wēnzhōng Tāng
후박온중탕 厚朴溫中湯 (Magnolia Bark Decoction for Warming the Middle)
Hou Po, Chen Pi, Gan Jiang, Mu Xiang, Cao Dou Kou, Fu Ling, Zhi Gan Cao
Warms the Middle, moves Qi; dries Dampness, relieves distension
Cold-Damp Qi stagnation in the Middle Jiao — cold epigastric/abdominal distension and pain

Liángfù Wán
양부환 良附丸 (Galangal and Cyperus Pill)
Warms the Middle, disperses Cold; moves Qi, relieves pain
Cold-Qi stagnation stomach pain — epigastric cold pain relieved by warmth

Jīnlíngzǐ Sǎn
금령자산 金鈴子散 (Melia Toosendan Powder)
Chuan Lian Zi, Yan Hu Suo
Soothes the Liver, clears heat; moves Qi, relieves pain
Liver Qi stagnation transforming to fire causing pain — hypochondriac/epigastric pain aggravated by irritation

Sìmó Tāng
사마탕 四磨湯 (Four Milled-Herb Decoction)
Ren Shen, Bing Lang, Chen Xiang, Wu Yao (ground/milled and steeped)
Moves Qi, directs rebellious Qi downward; tonifies Qi
Liver Qi stagnation with underlying Qi deficiency — chest/abdominal fullness, shortness of breath

Liùmó Tāng
육마탕 六磨湯 (Six Milled-Herb Decoction)
Si Mo Tang + Zhi Shi + Da Huang
Moves Qi, breaks stagnation; purges accumulation
Qi stagnation with constipation — abdominal distension/pain with difficult defecation

Wǔmó Yǐnzǐ
오마음자 五磨飲子 (Five Milled-Herb Decoction)
Wu Yao, Chen Xiang, Bing Lang, Zhi Shi, Mu Xiang (ground/milled and steeped)
Severe Qi stagnation from emotional constraint — sudden severe distension/pain from anger

Tiāntái Wūyào Sǎn
천태오약산 天台烏藥散 (Top-quality Lindera Powder)
Wu Yao, Mu Xiang, Xiao Hui Xiang, Qing Pi, Gao Liang Jiang, Bing Lang, Chuan Lian Zi, Ba Dou(processed)
Moves Qi, disperses Cold; relieves pain
Cold-type hernial/lower abdominal pain from Liver-channel Qi stagnation

Júhé Wán
귤핵환 橘核丸 (Tangerine Seed Pill)
Ju He, Hai Zao, Kun Bu, Hai Dai, Chuan Lian Zi, Tao Ren, Hou Po, Zhi Shi, Yan Hu Suo, Gui Xin, Mu Xiang, Mu Tong
Moves Qi, dispels stasis; softens hardness, disperses masses
Cold-Damp/Blood stasis hernial masses — swollen, hard, painful testicles/scrotum

Nuǎngān Jiān
난간전 暖肝煎 (Warm the Liver Decoction)
Dang Gui, Gou Qi Zi, Xiao Hui Xiang, Rou Gui, Wu Yao, Chen Xiang, Fu Ling, Sheng Jiang
Warms the Liver channel, disperses Cold; nourishes Blood, relieves pain
Liver-channel Cold with Blood deficiency — hernial/lower abdominal cold pain

Sūzǐ Jiàngqì Tāng
소자강기탕 蘇子降氣湯 (Perilla Fruit Decoction for Directing Qi Downward)
Su Zi, Ban Xia, Chen Pi, Qian Hu, Hou Po, Rou Gui, Dang Gui, Zhi Gan Cao
Directs rebellious Qi downward, calms wheezing; warms the lower, transforms phlegm above
Excess phlegm above with deficient Kidney Yang below — wheezing/cough with copious phlegm, cold limbs

Dìngchuǎn Tāng
정천탕 定喘湯 (Arrest Wheezing Decoction)
Bai Guo, Ma Huang, Su Zi, Kuan Dong Hua, Xing Ren, Sang Bai Pi, Huang Qin, Ban Xia, Gan Cao
Disperses Wind-Cold, directs Qi downward; transforms Phlegm-heat, calms wheezing
Wind-Cold exterior with internal Phlegm-Heat wheezing — cough/wheeze with thick yellow sputum

Xuánfù Dàizhě Tāng
선복대자탕 旋覆代赭湯 (Inula and Hematite Decoction)
Xuan Fu Hua, Dai Zhe Shi, Ren Shen, Ban Xia, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao, Zhi Gan Cao
Directs rebellious Qi downward, resolves epigastric focal distension; transforms Phlegm
Stomach-deficiency with Phlegm causing belching/hiccup — persistent belching, epigastric fullness

Júpí Zhúrú Tāng
귤피죽여탕 橘皮竹茹湯 (Tangerine Peel and Bamboo Shavings Decoction)
Chen Pi, Zhu Ru, Ren Shen, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao, Zhi Gan Cao
Directs rebellious Qi downward, stops hiccup; tonifies Qi, clears heat

Dīngxiāng Shìdì Tāng
정향시체탕 丁香柿蒂湯 (Clove and Persimmon Calyx Decoction)
Ding Xiang, Shi Di, Ren Shen, Sheng Jiang
Warms the Middle, directs rebellious Qi downward, stops hiccup
Stomach-deficient Cold causing hiccup — persistent hiccup with cold signs

Gānjiāng Rénshēn Bànxià Wán
건강인삼반하환 乾薑人參半夏丸 (Ginger, Ginseng, and Pinellia Pill)
Gan Jiang, Ren Shen, Ban Xia

Spleen-Stomach deficient Cold with rebellious Qi vomiting — chronic vomiting worse with cold, e.g. morning sickness

herb-formula.title: [12] Regulate Blood (理血)
herb-formula.source: Structured to match the official 287 Pan-Canadian Formulas exam map (HB Kim, AcupunctureMedia.com)

rows:
Táohé Chéngqì Tāng
도핵승기탕 桃核承氣湯 (Peach Pit Decoction to Order the Qi)
Tao Ren, Da Huang, Gui Zhi, Mang Xiao, Zhi Gan Cao

Xuèfǔ Zhúyū Tāng
혈부축어탕 血府逐瘀湯 (Drive Out Stasis in the Mansion of Blood Decoction)

실소산 失笑散 (Sudden Smile Powder)

Huángtǔ Tāng

Zao Xin Huang Tu(Fu Long Gan), Fu Zi, Bai Zhu, Sheng Di Huang, E Jiao, Huang Qin, Zhi Gan Cao

Spleen-Yang deficiency bleeding (distal, chronic) — blood in stool, uterine bleeding, cold limbs

Jiāo'ài Tāng
교애탕 膠艾湯 (Ass-Hide Gelatin and Mugwort Decoction)
E Jiao, Ai Ye, Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Bai Shao, Shu Di Huang, Zhi Gan Cao

herb-formula.title: [13] Expel Wind (祛風)

herb-safety.title: Prescription Safety — Special Populations & Duration
herb-safety.source: Compiled from standard TCM herbal safety principles

rows: Avoid/use extreme caution with classically contraindicated herbs (see per-herb notes — e.g. strong blood-movers, purgatives, many toxic herbs); dosage generally reduced even for permitted herbs

Dose is a fraction of the adult dose, scaled to age/weight — general teaching guidelines scale roughly by age bands (infant, toddler, child, adolescent); extra caution with toxic or strongly purging herbs
Reduced dosage; more caution with strong purgatives/diaphoretics due to physical fragility; higher risk of herb-drug interaction due to polypharmacy — always ask about current medications
Toxic herbs (e.g. Fu Zi, Xi Xin)
Not intended for continuous long-term use without monitoring and reassessment
Gan Cao (甘草) — high dose, long-term
Can cause pseudoaldosteronism — hypokalemia, edema, hypertension — with prolonged high-dose use
Reassess the formula regularly; a formula appropriate for an acute pattern is not automatically safe indefinitely
Blood-invigorating herbs (e.g. Dan Shen, Chuan Xiong, Tao Ren)
Sedative/calm-Shen herbs (e.g. Suan Zao Ren, Zhu Sha-containing formulas)
Hypokalemia risk compounded; digoxin toxicity risk
Strongly Qi/Yang-tonifying herbs (e.g. Ren Shen, high-dose Huang Qi)
Possible altered drug effect — always screen

herb-safety.title: 18 Incompatibilities & 19 Antagonisms (十八反 · 十九畏)
herb-safety.source: Classical TCM pharmacology — standard reference lists

blocks.heading: 18 Incompatibilities (十八反) — Never Combine
blocks.lead: Three groups of herbs classically never combined with the paired herb(s) — the interactions are considered to amplify toxicity or negate therapeutic effect.

rows: Gan Sui 甘遂, Da Ji 大戟, Hai Zao 海藻, Yuan Hua 芫花
Bei Mu 貝母 (all types), Gua Lou 瓜蔞, Ban Xia 半夏, Bai Lian 白蘞, Bai Ji 白芨
Ren Shen 人參, Sha Shen 沙參, Dan Shen 丹參, Xuan Shen 玄參, Ku Shen 苦參, Xi Xin 細辛, Shao Yao 芍藥 (both Bai/Chi)
牽牛 Qiān Niú (Morning Glory Seed)
犀角 Xī Jiǎo (Rhino Horn — now substituted, see CITES chapter)
五靈脂 Wǔ Líng Zhī (Flying Squirrel Feces)

herb-safety.title: High-Risk / Toxic Herbs — Summary
herb-safety.source: Compiled from standard TCM Materia Medica toxicity classifications

blocks.heading: Herbs Requiring Special Handling
blocks.lead: These are already flagged individually within their category chapters — this table collects them as a single at-a-glance safety reference.

rows: Must be properly processed (制附子); decoct first 30–60 min; contraindicated in pregnancy
Contains aristolochic-acid-family concerns / essential oil toxicity at high dose
Classic teaching: do not exceed ~3g in decoction; contraindicated with Yin-deficient heat
오두/천오/초오 Wūtóu/Chuānwū/Cǎowū 烏頭/川烏/草烏 (Aconite roots)
Must be processed and properly decocted long; strictly dosed; incompatible with the Bei Mu/Gua Lou/Ban Xia group
반하 (raw) Shēng Bànxià 生半夏 (unprocessed Pinellia)
Irritant to mucous membranes, toxic raw
Only processed forms (法/薑/清半夏) used internally; raw form is topical/external only
Small amounts only, short-term use, avoid in pregnancy/renal-hepatic impairment, avoid prolonged use
Topical use preferred; internal use highly restricted; monitor liver/kidney function
Topical only, highly diluted; internal use requires extreme professional caution

herb-safety.title: Dispensary Storage & Management
herb-safety.source: Compiled from standard pharmacy/dispensary safety principles

rows: Generally ≤20°C, dry, away from direct sunlight
Roughly 1–10°C (many perishable/prepared items, e.g. certain tinctures or fresh-processed herbs)
Keep low and stable to prevent mold growth and insect infestation
Store in opaque or light-protected containers
Pinyin name, Chinese characters, and (where applicable) Latin botanical name; lot number; expiry date; source
Periodic inventory checks for spoilage, pest damage, and expiry

herb-safety.title: CITES & Legally Restricted Animal-Derived Substances
herb-safety.source: Compiled with reference to CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species) — Canada is a signatory; standard TCM substitution practice

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.lead: Canada is a CITES signatory. Many classical animal-derived substances are banned or require special permits due to endangered-species status. Modern TCM practice in Canada uses accepted substitutes — several of which already appear marked "(substitute)" throughout the Formulas section of this app.

rows: 수우각 Shuǐniújiǎo 水牛角 (Water Buffalo Horn)
Often omitted or replaced with sinew/bone-strengthening herbs (e.g. Xu Duan, Gu Sui Bu) rather than a direct animal substitute
사향 Shèxiāng 麝香 (Musk Deer Musk)
Restricted — CITES Appendix I/II depending on species/source
Synthetic musk, or reformulation using Su He Xiang / other aromatic orifice-opening herbs where appropriate
영양각 Língyángjiǎo 羚羊角 (Saiga Antelope Horn)
산양각 Shānyángjiǎo 山羊角 (Goat Horn), or reformulation emphasizing Gou Teng for Wind-extinguishing action
조각자 Zàojiǎocì 皂角刺 (Gleditsia Spine), or Wang Bu Liu Xing
Banned — CITES Appendix I/II depending on species
黃連 Huang Lian / 龍膽草 Long Dan Cao substituted for the heat-clearing action

Cold/stagnation-type discomfort in the treated region
Increases muscle excitement, promotes Blood circulation, relaxes tendons, activates the channels
Back, lumbar, hypochondrium, limbs (upper limbs especially)

Stiffness and tension along the treated region
Tranquilizes, opens the orifices, improves eyesight

Headache, epigastric pain, aching pain, numb muscles

acu-extras.title: Biomedicine — Western Medicine Quick Reference

items.detail: Compiled from personal Western-medicine exam notes. Seed content for this section — a starting reference, not a complete biomedicine curriculum.

blocks.heading: CBC — Complete Blood Count
blocks.lead: The most common routine blood test. HIV-positive individuals should test at least every 3 months.

rows: Oxygen-carrying cells made in the bone marrow
Low = fatigue, shortness of breath, pallor → suspect anemia
Low = immunosuppressants, viral infection, HIV. High = bacterial infection, possible leukemia
% of blood volume that is RBC
Tracks with hemoglobin; high = dehydration, low = anemia
Low = bleeding risk; high = clot risk
PMNs ↑ with bacterial infection; lymphocytes ↓ with viral infection; monocytes ↑ in chronic infection

blocks.heading: Common Lab Tests — Quick High/Low Reference

rows: Muscle damage, heart attack, intense exercise
Liver Function (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin)
Some values can rise transiently without disease
Drug use, viral infection, fatty liver, heart/muscle damage

items.detail: Aspirin, Propranolol, Prednisone, Metformin, Heparin
Autonomic → sympathetic/parasympathetic · CNS → sedate or stimulate · Endocrine → sugar or steroid
"Which drug affects blood/bleeding?" → anticoagulants, steroids, NSAIDs

rows: ↓ blood glucose, moves glucose into cells
↑ blood glucose (breaks down stored liver glucose)
Cold sensitivity, fatigue, weight gain, constipation
Exophthalmos, weight loss, anxiety, profuse sweating
Cortisol deficiency → hypotension, fatigue, weight loss

blocks.lead: Grouping of autoimmune targets: RA/AS → joints · T1DM, Hashimoto's, Graves', Addison's → endocrine glands · MS, GBS, MG → nervous system · SLE, Crohn's, Sjögren's → systemic/mucosal.

acu-extras.title: Landmark — WHO Standard Point Location Guidelines
acu-extras.source: WHO_Standard_Landmark.pdf — WHO Standard Acupuncture Point Locations, General Guidelines for Point Location (Fig 15-24)

items.detail: General guidelines for acupuncture point location from the WHO Standard Acupuncture Point Locations reference — the conventions and bony/surface landmarks used to find points consistently, independent of body size.

blocks.heading: Proportional (Bone-Length / Cun) Measurement
blocks.lead: The body is divided into standard proportional units ("cun", 寸) between fixed bony landmarks, so measurements scale to each patient's own body rather than a fixed ruler length.

rows: Between the eyebrows to the anterior hairline
7th cervical spinous process to posterior hairline
Umbilicus to the upper border of the pubic symphysis
Nipple to nipple (or corresponding rib spaces)
Back, spine to medial scapular border
Posterior midline to the medial border of the scapula
Upper arm, axillary fold → elbow crease
Anterior/posterior axillary fold to the cubital crease
Cubital crease to the wrist crease
Medial condyle of the tibia to the tip of the medial malleolus
Lower leg, popliteal crease → lateral malleolus
Popliteal crease to the tip of the lateral malleolus
Width between the interphalangeal creases of the patient's flexed middle finger = 1 cun
Width of the patient's thumb at the interphalangeal joint = 1 cun
Width of the four fingers together (at the level of the PIP joints) = 3 cun

blocks.heading: Common Surface & Bony Landmarks Used for Point Location

rows: Reference point for numbering the thoracic spinous processes and Back-Shu points
Scapular spine, inferior angle of scapula
Level markers on the upper back (roughly T3 and T7)
Vertical reference points down the abdomen
Level marker for the lumbar spine (roughly L4)
Joint-fold reference lines for the 12/13/16-cun forearm and leg measurements

items.detail: Just below the skin — shallow insertion depth

blocks.heading: Ethics, Registration & Recordkeeping
blocks.lead: Personal exam-note highlights — a starting reference, not a complete jurisprudence curriculum.

items.detail: An active serious condition must not be treated without consulting a medical professional (e.g. physician) first.
No improvement within 2 months → recommend medical consultation. No improvement within 4 months, worsening, or new symptoms → discontinue treatment.
Annual renewal deadline: March 31. Late renewal → loss of registration, recoverable within 3 months (reinstatement fee: annual fee + 35%).
50 hours of CE required every 2 years; 200 hours of patient care required within 4 years.
Patient records must be kept a minimum of 10 years. Respond to personal-information requests within 45 days.
Minimum $1,000,000 per-occurrence liability coverage.
